Title: Doncaster Pride 2026 - Social
Summary:
Join us for
Doncaster Pride 2026
and some proper Yorkshire LGBTQ+ fun.
Doncaster Pride is an up-and-coming vibrant celebration of inclusivity, diversity and community spirit.  Having hosted UK Pride in 2024 it continues to grow in popularity year on year.
Although OutdoorLads are not participating in the official parade (beginning 11am from St. Sepulchre Gate), we will meet for a social event at Town Fields where the parade ends.
The Town Fields pride arena offers a lively mix of music and performances on the main stage plus a host of entertainment, bars, food and stalls, creating a welcoming space for everyone to connect with others, enjoy a sense of belonging and experience the joy of a community coming together.
Taking place in August, we can once again hope for a very sunny afternoon!
We will meet at the main entrance at 12.30pm
Feel free to wear your striking red OutdoorLads T-shirts to allow us to be found and to promote the organisation.
Tickets are usually available on the gate, however to avoid disappointment
buy tickets ahead
.
The pride arena closes at 9.30pm. If there is an appetite for more socialising we can look at the options Doncaster city centre has to offer.
Difficulty & Distance: Pride / Social Event

Title: Leave your ego at the door - of Freud's Museum (with lunch)
Summary:
Join Shaun and Chris as we discover the history of psychoanalysis.  This tour of the
Freud Museum
will provide insight into the mind and teachings of the "Father of Psychoanalysis", seeing the family dining room (showcasing photos of Freud's Vienna flat), his study with the original couch, which is apparently one of the most famous pieces of domestic furniture in the world!!
After the ground floor, we can venture upstairs to view the Freud family tree on the landing, before proceeding into Anna Freud's room, where she pioneered the practice of child psychoanalysis.  Next door there is a 20 minute film about the family, with footage from Vienna and London, as well as an interview with the man himself!  There is also an exhibition of works by Leonora Carrington, entitled
The Symptomatic Surreal.
Please note that you will need to book tickets in advance.
Use this
link
and select 11.00 on 08 August.  Admission is £16.90 with donation / £14.90 without.
We will anticipate spending about 75 to 90 minutes exploring the museum before we head off for a rather different early afternoon lunch - Vietnamese!
Pho Ta Finchley Road
serves traditional Vietnames dishes including pho, nom salads, stir frys and vegetarian.  All meals will be paid separately and there is no obligation to join us.
Photos courtesy of
www.pexels.com
and ODL members and used with thanks and permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Just walking through a museum

Title: The Castleton Classic Circular
Summary:
Join Mark for a classic circular hike taking in the very best sights of Castleton!
We will start by walking past the historic remains of Peveril Castle before heading up through the dramatic limestone gorge of Cave Dale. From there, we’ll join the Limestone Way and make our way towards the iconic Great Ridge. We will take in the stunning panoramic views from Mam Tor, follow the ridge along to Hollins Cross, and Lose Hill.
After taking in the scenery, we will head down into Hope and follow a leisurely, picturesque route back to Castleton. To finish off a brilliant day on the hills, we will head to a welcoming local pub for a well-earned drink and the option to grab some food.
New Members:
If this is your first event or you are new to OutdoorLads we’d love to see you. Please message me if you have any questions.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
Waitlist:
If this event is fully booked and you really want to attend, add yourself to the waitlist because places often become available. If you are on the list but can no longer come, please cancel your attendance as soon as possible to allow waitlisted people to attend in your place.
Image Credits: All images taken by the event leader with permission for ODL use.
Difficulty & Distance: Total distance is around 15 km (9.5 miles) with 600m (2,000 feet) of ascent. Approximately 6 hours. Some paths over fields are uneven and may be boggy in wet conditions.

Title: Mountain Biking in the Goyt Valley
Summary:
The 2nd of 2
evening
events exploring the upper Goyt Valley by Mountain Bike.
The route takes us on a mixture of paved roads, bridleways and forest tracks from Errwood Reservoir high onto the Moorlands to Derbyshire Bridge before taking the Old Road To Buxton, now a gravel track.
From Buxton we climb out on the old Buxton Whaley Road, before following a few Bridleways above Coombs before returning to the Goyt Valley and the old tramway along Fernilee Reservoir.
Although there are some tough climbs, the whole route is rideable with nothing too technical.
For this ride you will need a good hard tail or full suspension mountain bike. Bring lights just in case of unexpected delays.

Title: Cycle the Monsal Trail and White Peak Lanes
Summary:
A leisurely cycle in the White Peak area of the Peak District.
We will ride a full length of the Monsal Trail, and Bridleways to Rowsley, before returning along quiet lanes through several White Peak Villages including Youlgreave and Monyash.  After a cafe visit for refreshments in Monyash we have a choice of either on road or a slightly longer route along a section of the Tissington Trail and Pennine Bridleway.
We are in the Peak District, so we do have a reasonable amount of climbing, however this is generally on the road sections, approximately half the route is off road, but surfaces are generally good.
For this ride, Mountain Bike, Road, Gravel or Hybrid bike is suitable, surfaces are good on the off road sections. The length of the ride maybe a little challenging for Mountain Bikes with knobbly tyres.
Whilst the ascent may appear significant, we have plenty of time to take the climbs slowly and we will ride at the pace of the slowest, wait at the top of climbs, none of the climbs are excessively steep, but should you need to walk, no one we be left behind.
Should you have any questions about the route please message the leader.

Title: Trent Lock & Attenborough Nature Circuit
Summary:
I am excited to get this in the diary! We are heading out for a classic circular hike that ticks every box: industrial history, waterside trails, a solid lunch spot, and a proper pint to wrap things up.
Our route kicks off at Trent Lock, tracks up the Erewash Canal, cuts past the massive Toton Sidings (the infamous spot where the HS2 station was originally planned), and winds down to the Attenborough Nature Reserve visitor centre for lunch. The final leg follows the River Trent right back to where we started.
We start at the historic Trent Lock—the meeting point of three canals and two rivers. From here, we head north along the peaceful Erewash Canal, keeping a steady pace on the flat towpaths.
We'll loop past Toton Sidings. Once a bustling railway hub, this is the exact land where the East Midlands HS2 hub was meant to sit before the plans were scrapped. It’s a massive bit of local transport history to walk through.
After working up an appetite, we’ll head into the stunning wetlands of Attenborough. We are aiming to hit the visitor centre right around lunchtime to grab some hot food, coffee, and refuel.
The final stretch is a brilliant, scenic walk right through the heart of the reserve, tracking the River Trent all the way back to Trent Lock.
The Grand Finale: Once we get back into Trent Lock, we’ll be heading straight to the pub for a well-deserved drink and maybe some food to cap off the day.
Difficulty & Distance: Total distance is around 17.5 km (11 miles) with 12m (40 feet) of ascent. Approximately 5 hours. Some paths over fields are uneven and may be boggy in wet conditions.

Title: NOTTINGHAM Indoor Climbing (Basford) – Beginners welcome
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads at Nottingham Climbing Centre
Climbing evenings are held regularly and we tend to go to the local pub – The Lion for refreshments and a social gathering afterwards where they have a wide variety of beverages both alcoholic and non-alcoholic.
NOTE: If you are climbing with us for the first time
, please send a note to the
Event Leader
telling them about your experience and goals.
Nottingham Climbing Centre - Basford
Nottingham Climbing Centre is equipped with walls and routes to suit all skill levels and groups of climbers, whether you are a novice looking to try climbing for the first time, or an experienced lead climber, you’ll find an area to challenge you. With 28 Top Ropes routes available as well as 24 Lines of Lead climbing for the more advanced individuals. In addition, 6 auto-belays are available, so there is always the opportunity to climb, even if the numbers don’t match up. The available bouldering walls also provide that extra physical and mental challenge for those looking for something slightly different from rope work. Both Bouldering and Rope Climbing routes range from 4a – 8a.
Do I need to be Supervised as a Climber?
If you answer
NO
to any of the following three questions, then you will need to arrange to be supervised and will need to book onto a future beginners event.  If you can answer YES to all these questions, you can sign up to this event as normal:
Can you fit a climbing harness correctly?
Can you tie yourself to the end of a rope using the correct knots?
Can you belay another climber correctly and confidently?
To come along, you will need to register via this event page and to climb you will need to pay an entry fee directly to the climbing centre when you arrive, please see their
website
for up-to-date prices. We ask that all members sign up through the event page to make sure the event leader has all information of the members attending.
To climb at Nottingham Climbing Centre you will need to:
-     Complete a climbing waiver for the centre (please arrive early to do this the first time, or better still complete it online before you arrive)
-     Pay the Lifetime Registration Fee - £6.00
-     Pay an Entry Fee directly to the climbing centre - £9.50 (ensure you mention ODL)
-     Hire shoes (if you don’t have your own) - £3.50*
-     Hire a harness (again, if you don’t have your own) - £3.50*
-     Hire shoes and harness - £6.00*
*Equipment hire charges will be waived for your first visit to the centre!
Can I just come along and boulder?
Absolutely! Most of us do roped climbing, but if you prefer bouldering, you can sign yourself in and do it, as long as you can fill in the climbing centre's form correctly, watch their
safety video
and can answer the simple safety questions at reception on your first visit. Meet the group at the start; some others might be bouldering. We will make sure we come and find you when we have our tea break!
If you have any questions or need any more information just get in touch with the event leader by clicking on their profile below, or take a look at the
My first climbing event page
for more information.
Photos:
All images supplied by event leaders and used by OutdoorLads with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Beginners welcome.

Title: Nottingham Bouldering (Lenton) - MONDAY - Beginners Welcome
Summary:
Swap trainers for climbing shoes and join OutdoorLads for an evening of INDOOR BOULDERING at Nottingham’s newest bouldering venue
NOTE: If you are climbing with us for the first time
, either as an experienced climber or beginner, please send a note to the
Event Leader
telling them about your experience and goals. There is a limit to the number of beginners that we can accommodate at each session. Each beginner needs to be supervised so we need to ensure there are enough experienced climbers to help you out. Climbing is great fun! It's a fab workout, in a social environment, and anyone can do it! Give it a go!
What is Bouldering?
It's a form of climbing where instead of long high routes, the 'problems' tend to be shorter and focus on technique, balance, body positioning and sometimes power. Because of this different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, harnesses and belaying, which makes it an excellent activity for newcomers to the sport. No wonder it's often regarded as the 'purest' form of climbing.
If you're a seasoned climber you won't need convincing about the benefits of bouldering, but for those who've never climbed more than a staircase, the benefits are wide ranging: from the physical - improved strength, tone, balance, power and coordination; mental - problem solving, focus, concentration and body awareness; to the emotional - relaxation, a sense of achievement and a feeling that mind and body are working together perfectly ('feeling psyched'). On top of all this, bouldering tends to be a very social sport where you can hang out with mates and each have a go as you feel regardless of what level you climb at.
I'm new to Bouldering - can I come along?
If you have never tried bouldering before it is recommended that you have a guided session from the event leader, who will go through everything you will need to know to boulder safely before joining in with the rest of the group.
I've been Bouldering before?
As above, everyone attending the event  must abide by the centre's T&Cs etc. As an experienced boulderer you are responsible for your own safety, actions and involvement at all times. As such, you should use your own judgement in taking up advice given either by OutdoorLads Volunteers or other experienced climbers.
To climb at Nottingham Climbing Centre you will need to:
-     Complete a climbing waiver for the centre (please arrive early to do this the first time, or better still complete it online before you arrive.) You can find the
link
to the waiver and bouldering safety video
here
on Nottingham Climbing Centre's website.
-     Pay the Lifetime Registration Fee - £6.00
-     Pay an Entry Fee directly to the climbing centre - £9.50 (ensure you mention ODL)
-     Hire shoes (if you don’t have your own) - £3.50*
*Equipment hire charges will be waived for your first visit to the centre!
If you have any questions or need any more information just get in touch with the event leader by clicking on their profile below, or take a look at the
My first climbing event page
for more information.
IMPORTANT!
Please note that every person participating should be aware of (and accept) that those members perceived as ‘in charge’ or leading the event are not necessarily qualified, do not need to be qualified, and are not regarded by OutdoorLads, or themselves as ‘technical experts’ but are amateur climbers with some experience and who are happy to impart their knowledge. Any advice given should be considered with this in mind by the recipient.
All those attending the event must also be aware of the centre's own terms and conditions of participation and abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre’s staff. Everyone must also take responsibility for their own safety while participating in Indoor climbing events.
Photos:
All images supplied by event leaders and used by OutdoorLads with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Suitable for all abilities. If you're new to bouldering, just let the event leader know and we'll get you started!

Title: Up North to Helmsdale
Summary:
Head to the North Highlands and absorb some of the long and varied history of this part of Scotland, which now sits on the modern North Coast 500 route.
The east coast north of Inverness was very much a key part of the Pictish kingdom between the 3rd and 9th centuries, although we know very little of these ancient people, they left many clues behind of their existence in the form of beautiful carved stones. But people have kept moving in and out of the area - Kildonan, a few miles inland, was the site of Scotland's short lived gold rush in 1869 - and the clearances left their mark with Badbea clearance village a little further up the coast which was where all those cleared from the surrounding areas were moved to. These inland areas are wide and expansive with landscapes that roll for miles and miles, some parts now make up the Flow Country which was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2024.
Other attractions in the area include Dunrobin Castle, the largest building in the north highlands, which has a long and varied history for which there's lots on the
wikipedia
page, and locally the Timespan centre in Helmsdale itself - a cultural hub for the region with lots of stuff on the history of the area... and a cafe!
If you simply want to come and explore the area on two feet, you can explore some of the John O' Groats trail which runs along the coast by the town, or go climb Morven, the highest summit in Caithness at 706m.
Dogs:
This venue isn't suitable for dogs.

Title: HULL PRIDE 2026
Summary:
Welcome to Hull Pride 2026! 🌈
Join Chris and Shaun for a fantastic day out at
Hull Pride 2026
, celebrating the event’s
25th anniversary
!
This is a great opportunity to experience one of Hull’s biggest celebrations, support LGBTQ+ rights, and enjoy a welcoming and inclusive atmosphere. Whether you're attending for the first time or are a regular Pride-goer, everyone is welcome to come along and have a great time.
What to Expect
Watch the
Hull Pride Parade
, which starts at
11:00am
Explore the various Pride activities and entertainment across the city
Stop for tea, coffee, and refreshments during the day
Decide as a group what activities you'd like to enjoy throughout the day
Please help to promote OutdoorLads by wearing your red
OutdoorLads t-shirt
if you have one!
Pride Locations and Entertainment
There will be entertainment and activities taking place throughout the city, including:
Hull City Centre
Zebedee's Yard
Hull Marina
Other Pride venues and event spaces
Evening Theatre Performance
If you're interested in continuing the celebrations into the evening,
Hull Truck Theatre
will be presenting
Swamplesque
performances at
7:00pm
and
9:00pm
.
For tickets and more information, visit:
https://www.hulltruck.co.uk/whats-on/general-entertainment/swamplesque/
Find Out More
For the latest information about the event, visit the Hull Pride website:
https://prideinhull.co.uk/
We look forward to seeing you there for a fun-filled day celebrating Pride, community, and being yourself!

Difficulty & Distance: It's easy - you can take a break anytime you need

Title: Reading Indoor Bouldering
Summary:
This bouldering session presents an ideal opportunity for a fun evening of what I call sociable workout where we take on a wide range of physical (and in fact mental) challenges with the atmosphere always friendly and cheerful. All levels are welcome!
The beauty of bouldering lies in its simplicity: you don't need ropes or a harness, just some climbing shoes which can be hired at the centre and a drive to get the top and overcome some trepidation. It's also a fundamentally social experience, with most of the session spent discussing how to solve particular climbing routes, aka problems, or catching up with each other in between climbs. Bouldering is a great way to get into climbing, come and see what all the fuss is about!
All skill levels are welcome
. If you're new to climbing, we've got you covered with tailored guidance, boosting your skills and confidence while ensuring your safety on and around the wall. Don’t worry about needing to invest in gear before you try. The Climbing Hangar offers
free shoe rentals
so you don’t have to shell out for shoes until you know bouldering is for you.
Experienced boulderer? Dive in and embrace the challenges independently. Just remember to follow the centre's rules for your own safety.
Our ethos is inclusivity. I keep our group tight-knit to foster connection and ensure personalised coaching for everyone, regardless of your experience level. Many climbers started their journeys relatively “late” in life, incl. some session leaders
🤫
We convene at the
Climbing Hangar in Reading
, one of the largest climbing centres in the region. The generously spacious and friendly cafe/lounge is my personal favourite where you can find us socialising, having a cuppa, snacks or even pizza before and after the climbs.
If you have never climbed at the Climbing Hangar in Reading before, you must book an induction slot:

Title: London Bouldering Thursday Evening
Summary:
Come and join OutdoorLads for some bouldering in central London.
This event is for members of all levels of experience and all are welcome to come along to any of the sessions.
Bouldering is a form of climbing where instead of long high routes, the 'problems', as they are referred to, tend to be shorter and focus on technique, balance, body positioning and sometimes power. Because of this different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, harnesses and belaying, which makes the activities excellent for newcomers to the sport.
If you're a seasoned climber you won't need convincing about the benefits of bouldering, but for those who've never climbed more than a staircase, the benefits are wide ranging: from the physical - improved strength, tone, balance, power and coordination; mental - problem solving, focus, concentration and body awareness; to the emotional - relaxation, a sense of achievement and a feeling that mind and body are working together perfectly ('feeling psyched'). On top of all this, bouldering tends to be a very social sport where you can hang out with mates and each have a go as you feel regardless of what level you climb at.
Do I need a guided session?
If you have never tried bouldering before it is recommended that you have a guided session from the event leader, who will go through everything you will need to know to boulder safely before joining in with the rest of the group.
All those attending the event must also be aware of the centre's own terms and conditions of participation and abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre’s staff. Everyone must also take responsibility for their own safety while participating in Indoor climbing events.
Independent boulderer?
As above all attending the event  must abide by the centre's T&Cs etc. As an experienced boulderer you are at all times responsible  for your own safety actions and involvement. As such, you should use your own judgement in taking up advice given either by OutdoorLads Volunteers or other experienced climbers.
Can't make this event?
We run weekly climbing and bouldering events in the South East, so why not check out what's coming up near you here:
http://outdoorlads.events/SouthEastIndoorClimbing
. Alternatively, stay updated on upcoming events using the social media channels below:
Telegram
(Climbing and bouldering only):
OutdoorLads (SE) Climbing
Facebook:
http://fb.com/OutdoorLadsSouthEast
Twitter:
@OdlSouthEast
Difficulty & Distance: With climbs at every grade from complete beginner to seasoned pro, you decide how hard it gets!

Title: Parkrun - Withernsea Promenade
Summary:
Join Richard and lots of other folk for a 5k run at
Withernsea Promenade - Withernsea
Course Description:
The route consists of 3 laps of Withernsea’s stunning promenade with flat paths
parkrun, is a weekly event that takes place every Saturday at locations across the UK. Whilst I'll be running, you can walk or jog the 5k at your own pace. I've been park running for 5 years and enjoy the positive, welcoming and inclusive atmosphere created at these events. As someone who wouldn't call myself a runner, these events are a great way to motivate you to get up and active on a Saturday morning. There is no time limit and, no one finishes last. The Couch to 5k App can be really useful as you develop your 5k runs.
Following this parkrun I will also be leading a coastal walk at
Spurn point
, which you might like to join me for also
About parkrun
To take part in a parkrun and get your official time,
you will need to have
registered
to receive your all important barcode
.
You will need to bring this barcode with you either printed or on your phone.
Parkrun events are run nationwide and indeed internationally, so once you're signed up you can attend anywhere.  If you don't have a barcode you can still run but won't get a time.
Dogs:
parkrun allow one dog per person on a short lead (not extending).  Running with more than one dog, extending leads or waist leads are not permitted at parkrun events.  As a third party event, OutdoorLads does not have any control over this.
OutdoorLads Running Groups:
If you want to join the Outdoorlads' national group on the parkrun website, on your parkrun account select Profile, then groups and type "Outdoorlads (UK)" and click the Join button.
You may also wish to sign up to the
Strava OutdoorLads running club:
https://www.strava.com/clubs/439735
On occasion, parkruns can be cancelled due to lack of volunteers or adverse weather. Should this happen I will try to email members as soon as I am made aware.
All photos under the creative
commons licence
from
Geograph
Malc McDonald. Also:
Chris Morgan, Arek Adeoye and Huckster - Unsplash; Colin Renshaw.
Difficulty & Distance: The course is run on tarmac paths and grass

Title: Spurn National Nature Reserve
Summary:
Spurn point is Yorkshire's very own Lands End - an iconic and constantly moving peninsula which curves between the North Sea and the Humber Estuary. At over three miles long but as little as 50 metres wide, this landscape is unique and ever-changing. Big skies and ever changing wildlife make the evocative landscape of Spurn one of the Yorkshire's most iconic nature reserves which is cut off from the mainland at high tide.
The walk is 12.5K. We will carefully follow this coastline to the restored lighthouse which is over a century old. There we can enjoy our lunch in isolation with nature. We should also have the opportunity to climb the winding flight of stone steps of the lighthouse for an amazing panoramic birds-eye view of the Spurn (ticket £4). This is an ideal location to spot seals and numerous birdlife, an unspoilt wonder of Yorkshire for us to enjoy before we return safely to the mainland before the tide comes in!
Please note: dogs are not allowed at Spurn, in order to protect the sensitive wildlife and habitats.
Car Parking Costs:
£5 per car for all-day parking. This money goes directly back into managing and protecting Spurn. (Members of Yorkshire Wildlife Trust: free)
In addition to this walk, I will be completing
Withernsea Promenade parkrun
should you wish to join me for this before-hand.
All photos under the creative
commons licence
from
Geograph
. Photo credits: Gerald McKenna, G.Laird, Oscar Taylor, Paul Harrop, Mat Fascione
Difficulty & Distance: The walk is relatively flat, it is exposed to the elements on the coast and is a reasonable distance.

Title: North Wales June Social - Llandudno
Summary:
Join us for a Friday evening social in Llandudno.
We'll be meeting for a drink, a chat and a catch-up with fellow OutdoorLads members. The pub does food and is opposite a chippy, so there'll be an opportunity to get food also.
Whether you're a regular on events, new to OutdoorLads, or simply haven't been along for a while, you'll be very welcome.
We'll be meeting from 6pm until around 9:30pm, giving everyone plenty of time to get home afterwards (Llandudno really isn't a late night destination!)
It would be great to see some familiar faces and hopefully a few new ones too.
All images are event leaders own with permission given to OutdoorLads for use and reuse

Title: Manchester Board Game Night
Summary:
Join us for a fun evening of games at an easily accessible and excellent board game cafe in Manchester.
Games vary based on what those attending would like to play, but we mostly choose easy to learn games that anyone, from brand new players to seasoned veterans, can easily pick up and enjoy.
There's a cover charge of £8 per person for the full 3 hours. This is paid separately at the cafe
.
If you're new to OutdoorLads, you're especially welcome. It's a great way to meet and get to know people in a fun and relaxed environment.
Images created by event leader, used with their permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Social

Title: Hike The Cotswold Line Part 2 Kingham To Moreton-In-Marsh
Summary:
Please note that this is a
linear walk
between two stations on the Cotswold Line and works equally well for those travelling by train or by car. If travelling by car, you simply need to take the train one stop at the end of the walk to return to your vehicle.
Last August, I led a linear walk between Kingham and Charlbury, titled
"A Tolkien-Inspired Walk in the Wolds"
. This walk starts from Kingham once again but heads in the opposite (northerly) direction.
Since then, I have discovered that it is possible to create a series of enjoyable day walks along the entire Cotswold Line – the railway linking Oxford and Hereford. These walks would showcase some of the landscapes of the Cotswolds and Malvern Hills, while also passing through attractive villages, historic market towns and cathedral cities, including Oxford, Worcester and Hereford. If there is sufficient interest, I hope to develop this into an occasional series, gradually extending the route along the line from where we began.
This walk takes us through gentle, peaceful countryside, passing picturesque honey-stone villages, with a lunch stop at the historic Chastleton House (National Trust), where there will be time to visit the house and grounds if you wish. The walk finishes in a charming market town.
The sights:
Kingham:
is renowned for its honey-toned stone cottages and tranquil village green. It was named "England’s Favourite Village" by
Country Life
magazine in 2004. We won't see the village centre during this walk, but if you arrive early then feel free to explore (it's over 1 mile away from the station though).
Lower Oddington:
is known for its traditional limestone cottages and the historic St Nicholas Church, which contains remarkable medieval wall paintings including one of Britain’s best-preserved “Doom” paintings.
Adlestrop:
is a tiny, very traditional Cotswold village, best known for inspiring the famous Edward Thomas poem
Adlestrop
, written after a brief train stop there in 1914, which turned the village into a symbol of quiet rural England.
Chastleton House:
is a rare Jacobean country house, largely untouched since the early 17th century. Unlike many stately homes that were heavily remodelled over time, Chastleton survived in an almost time-capsule state, which is what makes it distinctive. Built between 1607 and 1612 for the wealthy wool merchant Walter Jones, the house still contains original furnishings, panelling, tapestries and even graffiti from past centuries. It has a slightly faded, atmospheric quality rather than polished grandeur. The property is now managed by the National Trust, and for those that are members or are happy to pay we can allow people time for a quick visit during the lunch break.
Evenlode:
is a tiny, timeless Cotswold village of honey-coloured stone cottages, ancient farmland and quiet river meadows near the Oxfordshire border.
Moreton-in-Marsh:
is a historic Cotswold market town known for its broad high street, elegant honey-stone buildings and traditional Tuesday market dating back centuries. It combines classic rural charm with independent shops, inns and is one of the Cotswolds’ most accessible and popular small towns.
The Route:
Leaving Kingham station, we briefly follow a B-road before passing signs marking the boundary of Gloucestershire and the Cotswolds. We then cross the River Evenlode and take a footpath into open fields.
The first few miles of the walk are very quiet, pleasant but unremarkable as we make our way across a succession of fields and eventually into woodland. The scenery becomes increasingly rewarding as the day progresses.
After around four miles, we reach our first settlement, the attractive village of Lower Oddington. We first pass its church, which sits apart from the village itself, before continuing into the village with its picturesque cottages and welcoming pub.
Following a short stretch along a main road, we detour into delightful parkland, passing a cricket pitch on our left with an impressive country house visible ahead. We then arrive in Adlestrop, a village full of character and home to a particularly attractive church.
Leaving Adlestrop behind, we begin a long but steady climb through open fields towards Chastleton. Looking back, there are increasingly fine views towards the Cotswold escarpment. At the top of the climb we reach Chastleton House, where I plan to stop for an extended lunch break. There will be plenty of space to enjoy lunch in the parkland, together with the option of visiting the house or enjoying tea and cake at the nearby church.
After lunch, the route descends through rolling farmland to the charming village of Evenlode.
Beyond Evenlode, we continue through a mixture of farmland and woodland, crossing the railway line before taking a direct route towards Moreton-in-Marsh, with the church tower visible on the horizon for much of the approach. On reaching the town, you can either head straight to the station or take advantage of the many inns, cafés and tea shops before returning home.
The route can be found
here
:
Dogs:
Well-behaved dogs are welcome, there are some road sections, crossings. stiles and some fields with farm animals (sheep, cows and horses in this area) where they will need to be on a lead. Please abide by the Countryside Code (
https://tinyurl.com/rlqvp3h
) and remember not everyone loves dogs as much as you do so be mindful of other members.
Photos taken by the leader 27.5.2026 and permission given to OutdoorLads for re-use.
Difficulty & Distance: 12.5 miles, paths of grass, dirt and tarmac, fairly flat with just one notable climb up to Chastleton, a total ascent of 200 meters

Title: A Pepperbox Perambulation with a Lover and a Cuckoo
Summary:
Pepperbox Hill is the highest point on the escarpment of Dean Hill that runs between the Test and Avon valleys in the south-east corner of Wiltshire, and on a clear day, it commands extensive views across the Solent and to the Isle of Wight to the south, with the tall spire of Salisbury cathedral to the north-west and Salisbury Plain beyond.
The
route
starts in the tiny hamlet of Hamptworth. We will set off through Langley Wood National Nature Reserve, an extensive ancient broadleaf woodland of international importance which is the northernmost point of the New Forest National Park.
After crossing the A36, we'll continue north through the village of Whiteparish and then out on to the steady climb up to the escarpment of Dean Hill, taking plenty opportunities to pause and enjoy the wide-ranging views opening up behind us over the entire New Forest and beyond to the Solent and the Isle of Wight.
Upon reaching the top, sweeping views to the north and west will open up, with Salisbury cathedral prominent in the near distance and Salisbury Plain beyond. Heading west along the escarpment, we'll reach the highest point at Pepperbox Hill where we'll pause for lunch in the shadow of the Pepperbox aka Eyre's Folly.
The folly is a three-storey hexagonal brick tower, believed to have been built over 400 years ago in 1606 by Giles Eyre who owned the nearby Brickworth estate. Rather than being a true folly with no real purpose, it is widely thought that it was originally built to provide a sheltered viewing stand for the landowners' wives to observe their husbands engaging in the local hunt while sheltered from the rain and mud. In the early 18th century, it is believed that highwaymen would hide out in the tower, and attack passers-by tired out from the climb. The tower was also used as a lookout point by the local Home Guard during World War II.
Continuing on, we'll cross back over the A36 and on to Standlynch Down where the views continue to open up, particularly to the west across the Avon Valley.
The route then gradually descends back down to re-enter the New Forest in the village of Redlynch, and then shortly afterwards on to Lover, which is actually pronounced like Dover! The village post office closed down in 2008, but a temporary office now opens up in the week leading up to Valentine's Day for those wishing to send cards stamped with "Lover".
The final section of the walk returns into woodland around Hamptworth Lodge and leads us back to our start point conveniently located by the lovely Cuckoo Inn where we can have some final refreshments.
Dogs
: well-behaved dogs are welcome on the walk, but must be under close control at all times and on a lead in the vicinity of livestock or where wildlife may be disturbed. Note that the route has a prolonged climb with little shelter from the sun and there are very few water features on the entire route, and so it would be inappropriate to brings dogs in hot weather.
Don’t Waste That Space!
Walks get filled up quite quickly, so please only sign up if you know you are free to attend. Plans do sometimes change, but remember to free up your space so someone else can attend.
New Members:
If this is your first event or you are new to OutdoorLads we’d love to see you. Please message me if you have any questions.
All images used on this page taken by the event leader and permission granted for use by ODL
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is 20km (12.5 miles) with 300m (1,000ft) of ascent, with one long sustained climb

Title: Warmington, Edge Hill & Hornton - aka Banburyshire
Summary:
On Sunday 23 October 1642, the Royalist army descended from Edge Hill to begin the first major battle of the English Civil War. The battle was actually fought in the flat fields south-east of Kineton but took its name from the prominent ridge that dominates the local landscape.
This part of the world is known informally as “
Banburyshire
”. It's the immediate catchment area of the old Banbury Cattle Market which was one of the largest livestock markets in Europe until 1998 – and it includes parts of Northamptonshire and Warwickshire as well as north Oxfordshire.
Our 2-counties walk begins in the idyllic village of
Warmington
, which is situated on the slopes of Edge Hill, on the northernmost tip of the
Cotswolds Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty
. It features picturesque ironstone cottages, a large village green and duckpond.
As we leave the village, we also gradually leave the distant roar of the M40 and cross fields where bleating sheep and singing skylarks become our companions.
We climb up onto the escarpment and follow a delightful woodland path down
Jacob's Ladder
and back up into the hamlet of
Edge Hill
. We'll pass
The Castle
– a classic English folly, built in the 1740s. Grade II listed, it includes an octagonal tower which is said to have been inspired by a tower at Warwick Castle. It's operated as an Inn since 1822.
From Edge Hill, we cross fields and, for a while, walk along the county boundary between Warwickshire and Oxfordshire. It's here that we'll break for a picnic lunch before picking up more field paths to take us to Hornton.
Hornton
includes numerous historic houses, with the St. John the Baptist Church dating back to the 12th century, featuring 14th-century wall paintings.
From Hornton we head north to cross back over the border from Oxfordshire into Warwickshire, returning to our starting point in Warmington.
The Plough
in Warmington provides an opportunity for refreshments for those that want to stay on at the end of the walk, and the nearby
National Herb Centre
is open until 5.30pm for anyone wanting to stock up on new plants for the garden!
Dogs are welcome:
Our walk includes village streets and we need to cross a fairly busy B road a couple of times. We may encounter sheep, lambs, cows or horses and there are a few fields where landowner signs request dogs on a lead.
Waiting List:
If there are no places left on this event, please join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event, and you are very likely to get a place.
Don't waste that space!
If your plans change and you are unable to make the event, please cancel your attendance so that others get the opportunity to sign up and make travel plans.
New Members:
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions then please
Message Me
, or go to the
My First Event
section of the website for more information.
Pictures:
All images taken by the Event Leader (April 18, 2026) with permission for
OutdoorLads
to use and reuse.
Difficulty & Distance: 8.4 miles on a mix of good field paths, country roads and village streets. Total ascent: 280m / 920ft.

Title: August Bank Holiday- 3 night hostel in the Yorkshire Dales
Summary:
EARLY BIRDS! The first 3 members to book will save 10% at the checkout! 👛
"The charm of Kettlewell is an abiding charm, and to those of us whose life is spent amid the hurley-burley of city life, the village seems the peculiar abode of peace and quiet beauty; its limestone terraces, with their fringes of hazel and rowan coppices, give to the district a characteristic beauty." (Professor Moorman)
Don't want to waste the last bank holiday before Christmas?
Want to get away from the hurley-burley of city life?
Just want to relax and recharge?
We have just what you need - a
3 night
hostel weekend in the Yorkshire Dales, staying in a spacious independent hostel, right in the heart of the village. The village is a stunning, picture-box classic Yorkshire Dales village, with stone house, a stream running through it, a proper old-fashioned village shop and three utterly superb public houses. Oh and absolutely stunning views in every direction. Kettlewell will not disappoint you!
On Saturday, there will be a led full day dales walk, and on Sunday, as we have a bonus day, we will be doing some activities based around the village (more details will be provided at the welcome meeting)
There's a good size lounge and separate dining room, cosy conservatory area and a large kitchen together with bike store and a range of bedroom sizes. Outside, there is a large garden with beautiful views in every direction. There is no TV and Wi-Fi is not brilliant, so a chance to socialise instead!
Note:
Bedding is provided. You may wish to bring your own pillow from home.
Dogs:
There are two well-behaved dogs booked to attend. Two is the maximum allowed so there are no more dog spaces available.
Photos:
Provided by the event leader and used by OutdoorLads with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Hostel Event: Easy.  Walks: Moderate, and will involve some uneven ground

Title: Yorkshire Three Peaks Hostel 2026
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads for an early September hostel break to walk the
Yorkshire Three Peaks!
You've probably heard of the National Three Peaks of Ben Nevis, Snowdon/Yr Wyddfa and Scafell Pike, in many ways more a driving challenge than a walking challenge, and requiring a certain level of insomnia.
But did you know there is also a famous walking challenge in Yorkshire too, walking (approximately) a marathon
over a circuit of Whernside, Ingleborough and Pen y Ghent in the Dales?  It's that challenge we will be undertaking on this hostel weekend, with the added benefit of a comfortable lounge to sit in and a proper (bunk) bed to sleep in afterwards!
Why not do some other OutdoorLads mountain walks to get your fitness up before tackling the challenge?
Don't worry, though, if it turns out you can't make it all the way round, the route takes us past Horton-in-Ribblesdale station where it would be possible to take a train back to Ribblehead, and it'd be possible to bail off after our first mountain, Whernside, and head for the pub.  So if you think you can do it and fancy a go please do sign up!
The Plan
Friday evening
: arrive from 4:30pm (no earlier please), we will have the traditional OutdoorLads buffet and a briefing around 8pm before socialising at the hostel or maybe a visit to the local pub.
Saturday
: we will be up quite early to head out after breakfast onto the main event, the Yorkshire Three Peaks walk, with the aim being to complete it within 12 hours if possible.  When we get back we'll have tea/coffee and cake, and a hearty dinner which I'm sure we will all need by then, then we can socialise in the hostel, pop out to the pub or if you're really worn out maybe an early night - or perhaps bring your favourite board game if that's your thing?
Sunday
: after breakfast it'll be time to pack, sort out the hostel and say our goodbyes.  I don't plan as things stand to offer a Sunday walk (we'll all be tired!) but you might want to do some touristing around the local area before heading home?
Sponsorship?
Unlike the Keswick to Barrow walk which OutdoorLads also takes part in as a fundraiser, this isn't specifically being run as a sponsored event, but if you fancy doing some fundraising yourself as part of this walk, either for OutdoorLads or for another charity of your choice, please feel free (and thanks in advance if you do decide to support OutdoorLads!)
Other Activities
While the main intention of this hostel is to complete the Three Peaks of Yorkshire walk, if other mountain or lowland leaders join the event it may be possible to offer shorter walks as well.  If you are such a leader, please send me a message via my profile below to let me know what you are interested in offering and I will note it on here.  Thank you :)
It would also be possible to stay with us in the hostel and do your own thing if you're a cyclist or just want to "train geek out" and have a ride on the scenic Settle-Carlisle Line (the town of Settle is said to be a nice day out, as is the city of Carlisle), take some photos of the famous Ribblehead Viaduct, have a meal and a pint at the Station Inn or just chill at the hostel!  If this is your intention please send me a message when you've booked on so I know I don't need to account for you on the main walk.
Dogs
Regrettably this event is not suitable for dogs - the walk is probably too long for most and they are not allowed in the hostel by the owners.  Sorry!
Photo Credits
Ribblehead Viaduct and Pen y Ghent by Kreuzschnabel, CC BY-SA 3.0 <
https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0
>, via Wikimedia Commons
Blea Moor Road at Gearstones Lodge by David Dixon, CC BY-SA 2.0 <
https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0
>, via Wikimedia Commons
Junction of Pennine Way and Yorkshire Three Peaks Path by Roger Templeman, CC BY-SA 2.0 <
https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0
>, via Wikimedia Commons
Yorkshire Three Peaks sign - Dave Morton, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
Difficulty & Distance: 43km/26.7 miles (a marathon!), 1729m/5670ft height gain

Title: Bouldering MANCHESTER - Beginners welcome
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads For An Evening of INDOOR BOULDERING in Manchester!
This event is intended as a safe space for the LGBTQ+ community; everyone will be warmly welcomed
Bouldering is a form of climbing that focuses on shorter "problems" emphasising technique, balance, body positioning, and sometimes power, rather than long, high routes. Without the need for ropes, harnesses, and belaying, bouldering is an accessible activity for newcomers and is often considered the "purest" form of climbing.
Bouldering offers a wide range of benefits, including:
Physical:
Improved strength, tone, balance, power, and coordination.
Mental:
Enhanced problem-solving, focus, concentration, and body awareness.
Emotional:
Relaxation, a sense of achievement, and a feeling of mind-body connection.
Bouldering is also a fantastic way to meet new people and build lasting friendships, as it's an inherently social sport where climbers share tips and strategies to conquer the routes.
New to Bouldering?
If you're new to bouldering, it is recommended that you have a guided session from the event leader to learn the basics of safe bouldering. The leader can then introduce you to the rest of the group where you can refine your newly learnt skills in a social environment.
Experienced Boulderer?
If you’re new to climbing with ODL, the leader will help to introduce you to the rest of the group so you can explore the centre together.
You are responsible for your own safety and actions while bouldering. Please use your own judgement when accepting advice from OutdoorLads Volunteers or other experienced climbers, regardless of your bouldering experience.
Ready to boulder?
You will need to sign up on the ODL event page. We would ask you to do this each time so that the leader has access to important information
If you are not already registered with Rock Over Climbing, please do so
here
to save time when you arrive. You only need to do this once.
When you arrive at the centre you will need to pay for entry and hire climbing shoes if you don't own a pair. ODL members benefit from the concessionary rate so please mention this when paying.
Get bouldering! You’ll find most of the group already bouldering so feel free to join them. If you’re new to bouldering with ODL, the leader will be looking out for you so if you spot them, please go speak with them first.
Any questions?
If you have any questions, please contact the leader directly.
Photos : Copyright granted to OutdoorLads by
Sieh
and joenellist.
Difficulty & Distance: Suitable for all abilities. If you're new to bouldering, just let the event leader know and we'll get you started!

Title: Indoor Climbing MANCHESTER - Beginners welcome!
Summary:
This event is intended as a safe space for the LGBTQ+ community; everyone will be warmly welcomed
Join us for an evening of climbing in one of the UK's top climbing centres. Whether you are new to climbing or a seasoned climber, this centre offers something for everyone with a huge selection of routes including top rope, lead and auto belay.
Prefer to boulder? That's well catered for as well, with an extensive modern bouldering area ensuring there is plenty to keep you busy, or you can swap between climbing and bouldering on the same night.
Climbing is the main focus of the event, but this is also a great chance to meet other members and build lasting friendships. Many Outdoor Lads members join through climbing, so if you're new to the group and want to know more, come along and chat over a cuppa during the tea and cake break.
What to expect on your first climb with us?
If you are an experienced climber and have passed the centre's proficiency test, we'll pair you up with a climbing buddy and you'll be out exploring the centre in no time.
If you have never climbed before or think you could do with a refresher, we offer supervised climbing sessions with one of our leaders, subject to availability. That session will focus on introducing you to climbing, making sure you get plenty of time on the wall whilst also showing you how to belay safely. Due to the centre's rules, we are limited in the number of climbers the leader is able to supervise in each session so if you think you may need a supervised session,
please contact the leader before signing up to make sure there is enough space.
As a general guide, if you answer "no" to any of the following questions, you should opt for a supervised session:
Can you fit a climbing harness correctly?
Can you tie into a rope using the correct knots?
Can you belay and lower down another climber correctly and confidently?
After a few supervised sessions, you should be ready to complete the centre's proficiency test and once that has been passed, you'll be able to climb with the rest of the group.
Everything else you need to know
You will need to register with Big Depot before your first visit to the centre but you only need to do this once. If you are an experienced climber, you can save time and do this online
here
, selecting the 18+ unsupervised option. This also applies if you just want to boulder, just select the relevant options during the registration. If you are a beginner and require a supervised session, the leader will help register you when you are at the centre.
Photos provided by Sieh and Dexmonster.
Difficulty & Distance: There is a range of routes of varying difficulty, but there should be something to suit everybody

Title: Midweek Royal Park & Gardens Big Circuit
Summary:
Rescheduled date after first attempt was thwarted by the late May heatwave.
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Please note that this event is on a Thursday.
Midweek Escape - 13 Miles Enjoying Windsor Great Park
Join us for a civilised midweek escape – a walk designed for those who are either enjoying the pleasure of retirement, have flexible work hours, those wishing to explore the boundary limits of working from home or just fancy taking a sneaky day off. This is not merely a walk in the park as being 13 miles long it's still enough for a decent workout, and with an expected smaller group than typical weekend walks attract it should feel relaxed, friendly and more intimate.
We begin in Sunningdale, conveniently accessible by train, and explore the southern and central reaches of Windsor Great Park. Highlights include the Valley Gardens (free entry), Obelisk Pond, the deer park, the Copper Horse with its sweeping views over the Long Walk, Cow Pond, Chapel Wood and an extended stretch beside Virginia Water.
The Sites:
Windsor Great Park
A historic royal park of approximately 5,000 acres, comprising ancient woodland, open grassland, formal avenues and lakes, including
Virginia Water
. It originated in the 13th century as private hunting grounds for
Windsor Castle
.
Valley Gardens
A significant woodland garden on the northern shore of Virginia Water, with undulating valleys planted with azaleas, rhododendrons, magnolias and camellias. Developed from the 1940s under
George VI
, the gardens are particularly striking in spring.
Copper Horse
An imposing equestrian statue of
George III
, commissioned by
George IV
and unveiled in 1831. Standing on Snow Hill, it marks the southern end of the Long Walk and is visible for miles across the surrounding countryside.
Virginia Water
An 18th-century man-made lake created as part of royal landscape design. Notable features include a 100-foot totem pole gifted by British Columbia and ornamental ruins relocated from Leptis Magna. A full circuit of the lake measures approximately five miles.
The Route:
Starting at Sunningdale Station, we will take a quiet route through the Sunningdale village. We then cut through Coworth Park and the polo grounds before entering via the southern gate to Virginia Water.
We cross Five Arch Bridge and loop through the Valley Gardens then follow woodland paths overlooking Obelisk Pond and on towards Cumberland Lodge. A brief stretch along the Three Castles Path brings us to the halfway point at the Copper Horse, where we stop for lunch.
Afterwards, we continue through the deer park, skirt Cow Pond, walk alongside Obelisk Pond, pass the entrance to Savill Garden and the totem pole, and then enjoy an extended stretch along the southern edge of Virginia Water. At the western end, there is an optional stop at an outdoor café. We then exit the park and return to Sunningdale via a similar route.
The route can be found
here
:
Dogs:
Well behaved dogs are welcome. Whilst in the Great Park dogs need to be on the lead in some of the sections where we are walking to include the Obelisk Lawn, Valley Gardens, the Deer Park and the visitors centre if we go there. There are some road sections in and out of Sunningdale where dogs need to be on a lead.
Photos
: All pictures taken by the leader February 2026, and permission given to OutdoorLads for re-use.
Difficulty & Distance: 13 mile walk with only gentle climbs

Title: Kayaking On The Quays - 5 September 2026
Summary:
We're back on the Quays for 2026!
This is a 90-minute session with a qualified instructor. You'll be shown the basics, have some fun and games on the water, and probably have some silly challenges to complete. The session is appropriate for all abilities, and the instructor will tailor the session based on the needs of the group.
Please arrive by 09:40 to get changed and start the session on time for 10:00.

Title: Summer Ramble in South Warrington 2026
Summary:
Join Andrew for a summer walk in south Warrington.
We will start by visiting Walton Hall and Gardens and then walk up to the Walton reservoir and make our way to Stretton via tracks, round the side of fields and along the route of an old Roman Road (see if we can spot the statues of Roman soldiers).
When we get to Stretton we will stop for a welcome drink at the Stretton Fox. It might be possible to eat our butties here or you could buy food in the pub if you haven't bought any butties.
We will then set off down Hatton lane, across fields and down country lanes until we get to Hill Cliffe where we can enjoy a panoramic view of Warrington. We will then make our way back to the start and could then call at the Walton Arms for a summer drink or coffee and cakes in the cafe at Walton gardens.
The nearest railway stations are Warrington Central and Warrington Bank Quay. If you're coming by train let Andrew know and it should be possible for someone to meet you at the station and give you a lift to the start of the walk.
Are you new to Outdoorlads and considering your first event
?
Have a look at this link, hopefully it will help.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
Don't worry if the event is full, put your name on the waiting list and you will get a place if someone cancels before the event.
All photos taken by Andrew
Difficulty & Distance: Mainly country lanes and tracks; some road walking;  about 8 miles.

Title: Open water swim (Yorkshire)
Summary:
If you enjoy open water swimming or have been thinking about giving it a go, then come with OutdoorLads to take the plunge!
You will need to be a confident and strong swimmer.
This event would not suit new or less confident swimmers.
There are 2 set routes, 250 metres and 500 metres.
We can swim between 8am - 10am. We will meet at about 8am and then people can change and hire suits if needed. If we get in the water at 8.30am it still gives us plenty of time. The venue is an old quarry, so the water is still, clean and safe.
Safety: the venue is fully organised and staffed with lifeguards and medics to ensure the safety of swimmers.
Please read the following link if you intend to swim. The videos are very good and help explain how things work.
https://www.bluelagooners.com
Kit: you can hire gear at the venue if you don't own your own. Even on a very warm day the water will be cool, so it is advisable to swim in a full swim suit.
The facilities are decent. There are changing cubicles but no showers. There is also a cafe if you want a drink or bite to eat.
You will need to book a place through the
Bluelagooners website
. The swim costs £10.
Signing up to this event page does not guarantee you entry to the swim. You need to be booked onto both to be part of this swim with OutdoorLads.
The activity is not run by OutdoorLads but by Bluelagooners.The event is open to the public but there will be plenty of room for everyone 🙂.
This is a third part event - OutdoorLads is arranging a group to meet together there only. You will be subject to the standard terms, conditions and insurance of Bluelagooners and will need to sign any waiver they require to participate
.
Dogs are not allowed at this venue.
Image credits:  all photos provided by the event leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: You will need to be a strong swimmer.

Title: Tom a' Chòinich & Toll Creagach, Glen Affric
Summary:
A duo of Munros in arguably the most beautiful glen of all, Affric!
These are two very different but neighbouring Munros closer to the entrance (and thus the car parks) in Glen Affric, but nevertheless they are still a challenging day out.
Tom a' Chòinich has rocky ridges and an appealing peak, Toll Creagach is a more rounded, mossy summit. The former is 1112m, the latter 1054m.
Our route is 16.5km or 10.25miles. Some of the route involves boggy ground and crossing a couple of burns (if in spate, the walk will be altered). Total ascent 1096m.
Dogs are welcome but must be kept under close control.
Photos are taken by AMD and are used with his gracious permission.

Title: See Loch Ness From Above
Summary:
Probably the most famous stretch of water in the world.
If you've not yet seen the beloved Nessie, maybe viewing her home from above will improve your chances!! If you don't know what to look for, shots of Nessie and her offspring are included in the photos on this page......
Loch Ness, as well as being home to Nessie, has a few other impressive facts about it (yes, Nessie's residence in the Loch is a fact!!) Loch Ness contains more water that all the rivers and lakes of England combined. It is far deeper than any part of the vast North Sea.
Enough marketing, let's get on with our day out.
We'll be seeing Loch Ness from above by climbing up a lovely hill called Meall Fuar-mhonaidh. That's easy for you to say. It stands proud at 699m above sea level.
The walk is out and back, 9km / 6miles and takes in some beautiful woodland before ascending slopes on open hillside. There are a few short, steep sections, but nothing of any real challenge. The walk is suitable for any reasonably active hill walker.
From the summit, if conditions allow, we should be able to see almost the full length of Loch Ness, with just a litre or two towards Inverness obscured by the hills. In the other direction, way beyond Loch Ness in the Great Glen, we should see Ben Nevis towering above Fort William. In other directions, we get a glimpse of seemingly endless summits in the Highlands. Finally, with a stroke of luck, we might see Nessie in her waters or sunning herself on a remote lochside beach.
Dogs are welcome on this walk, kept under close control.
Photos:
taken by Rob Mitchell and used with permission.

Title: Pentland Hills
Summary:
A joint walk with the
Gay Outdoor Club
in the Pentland Hills from Hillend.
An eight mile walk from the Pentland ski centre car park at Hillend, climbing Caerketton Hill, Allermuir Hill, with an option to also climb Capelaw Hill.  About eight miles, 4 - 5 hours walking . The start point is accessible by public transport from Edinburgh. This is a hill walk so expect some steep climbs and descents and paths may be muddy after rain.
There are many variations of the route we can take and we can cut the walk short or extend it if the weather is great. An idea of some of the route is on the
Walkhighlands website.

on a previous GOC walk in the Pentlands in 2009.

Title: Woburn Estate
Summary:
Join David and Alex for varied circular route through the Woburn Estate and surrounding countryside. We’ll start from the main car park opposite St Mary’s Church and head out through the Deer Park, skirting lakes and woodland and keeping an eye out for black squirrels and deer along the way.
We’ll follow a mix of public footpaths, estate tracks and quiet lanes, including sections alongside the safari park fences - so there’s a good chance of seeing wildlife, even if the lions stay put. The route leaves the Woburn Estate and takes us through villages like Eversholt and Milton Bryan, where there’s an optional pub stop near the halfway point.
Later on we’ll pass some interesting historical spots, including a former WWII propaganda site built in 1942 as a studio for the Political Warfare Executive, and Paris House (which a previous Duke of Bedford brought back from the Paris Exhibition of 1878), before looping back through the estate past ponds and open grassland to finish where we started.
It’s mostly gentle walking with a few steady rises, nothing technical, and we’ll keep a relaxed pace with stops as needed. A short section may involve paddle along a stream, which can be avoided if not everyone is wearing waterproof footwear!
There are warning signs in the Woburn estate as the large number of deer means there may be ticks present, so please be aware. The grass us usually grazed short so usually only a problem if picnicking!
Don't waste that space!
Summer means lots of members looking to get out more so please only book if you know you can make it. If your plans change please free up the space for someone else.
New members
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions then feel free to message me, or go to the "My First Event" section under "About Us".
Is it dog friendly?
The walk is not suitable for dogs as it crosses a deer park and the edge of a safari park.
Image / photo credits:
All leader's own images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse
Difficulty & Distance: Mostly gentle walking. Around 4 hours walking time.

Title: From Harbour to Shore - a Wittering Walk to Adore
Summary:
Only five people attended this walk when I ran it before, so perhaps I can get away with repeating it to plug a gap in the southeast walk schedules, and more guys will find it a novel experience. Starting in Chichester, we'll follow the calm, tree-lined Chichester Canal south until it opens into Chichester Harbour, sparkling, spacious and special. We'll stroll past Birdham’s marinas and along Itchenor’s winding lanes, with seabirds calling overhead and sails on the horizon. We'll end our journey on the golden sands of West Wittering. Fresh air, fine views, and a frolic on the sand on one of the best beaches around, and as this is a linear walk, a spell relaxing on the bus back.
The sights:
Birdham:
Some pleasant houses, especially the Westlands Estate. Birdham Pool was a tidal mill pond but became one of the first public marinas in the country in 1946. Chichester Marina is the chief attraction: a big rectangular harbour with sleek yachts along pontoons, created in 1966. St James' Church is big and bulky C14 but harshly restored in 1882.
Chichester Harbour AONB:
'Chichester Harbour is one of the few remaining undeveloped coastal areas in Southern England...Its wide expanses and intricate creeks are at the same time a major wildlife haven and among some of Britain's most popular boating waters. The massive stretch of tidal flats and saltings are of outstanding ecological significance.' (From Wikipedia)
West Itchenor:
'One short, nicely unpretentious street of cottages, mostly 18th century, running down to the water's edge.' (Ian Nairn,
The Buildings of England: Sussex
). Huge waterside properties all around. From 1744 to the early C19 warships where intermittently made there.
West Wittering:
An attractive and exclusive village with large thatched houses and villas set in large gardens. Cakeham Manor was once a palace of the bishops of Chichester, now an attractive group of medieval, Tudor and Georgian parts. The tall brick tower of the early C16 stands out amid the flat fields.
East Head
and West Wittering Beach:
'One of the last surviving pieces of natural coastline in West Sussex. East Head is an example of the nationally rare and fragile, yet dynamic sand-dune habitat. Walk, play and sunbathe on the beach, which is considered one of the best in Sussex [it has Blue Flag status (see the
website
)].' (From the National Trust website). The car park and beach get horribly busy in the summer, but our walk beforehand will be relatively quiet.
Dogs:
I love having dogs on my walks and this walk is suitable for them. Dogs are excluded from the main swimming beach at West Wittering but are allowed nearby. There are dog waste bins. Warm weather could be exhausting for dogs although we are close to water most of the way. Any dog off its lead must be under control.
The
route
(please click on the link to see it)
:
Starting at Chichester Railway Station, we'll follow the New Lipchis Way all the way along the canal to the beach. After the beach, we'll walk to Cakeham Road to catch the bus back to Chichester.
Swimming
This walk
does not
include swimming. If you choose to swim, you do so at your own risk as it is not covered by OutdoorLads's insurance. The sea water is always clean as there are no sewage outfalls nearby and the beach retains its Blue Flag award every year. Lifeguards are in attendance at the beach.

. The other pictures were taken by the leader.)
Difficulty & Distance: Distance: 18 km (11.5 miles); terrain: almost flat and on beach shingle, grass, dirt, gravel, sand and tarmac; total climb is just 14m.

Title: Mellor and Matley Moor evening MTB
Summary:
SUITABLE FOR BEGINNERS
The ride starts with a descent into the Goyt Valley before a short technical climb of Linnet Clough. After getting our breath back as we cross Mellor Golf Course, we will take a very steep paved track to Mellor Cross, to be walked by all but the fittest!  Once at Mellor Cross, we stay high on Bridleways and Lanes to Matley Moor near Lantern Pike, before the final big climb takes us over Cown Edge with its fine views over Greater Manchester and the Cheshire Plain.
From here the route  is mainly downhill back to the Goyt Valley, via Etherow County Park.
Whilst the whole route is rideable with nothing too technical, riders will require good level of fitness as some hills are quite steep.
For this ride you will need a good hard tail or full suspension mountain bike.
NOTE: Ride may to postponed if severe weather is forecast.

Title: London Bouldering Morning - All Abilities welcome!
Summary:
Come and join OutdoorLads for some bouldering in central London!
If you have not been to one of the London Climbing Centres you will need to pre-register
HERE
. You only need to register once to use any of their 8 centres in London.
This event is for members of all levels of experience and all are welcome to come along to any of the sessions.
Bouldering is a form of climbing where instead of long high routes, the 'problems', as they are referred to, tend to be shorter and focus on technique, balance, body positioning and sometimes power. Because of this different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, harnesses and belaying, which makes the activities excellent for newcomers to the sport.
If you're a seasoned climber you won't need convincing about the benefits of bouldering, but for those who've never climbed more than a staircase, the benefits are wide ranging: from the physical - improved strength, tone, balance, power and coordination; mental - problem solving, focus, concentration and body awareness; to the emotional - relaxation, a sense of achievement and a feeling that mind and body are working together perfectly ('feeling psyched'). On top of all this, bouldering tends to be a very social sport where you can hang out with mates and each have a go as you feel regardless of what level you climb at.
First time boulderer?
If you have never tried bouldering before, the event leader can give you a short introduction to get you started and give a few a few pointers on technique. After which we will join in with the rest of the group.
All those attending the event must also be aware of the centre's own terms and conditions of participation and abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre’s staff. Everyone must also take responsibility for their own safety while participating in Indoor climbing events.
Experienced boulderer?
Simply register with the wall and turn up on the day!
As above, all attending the event must abide by the centre's T&Cs etc. As an experienced boulderer you are at all times responsible for your own safety and so should use your own judgement in taking up advice given either by OutdoorLads volunteers or other experienced climbers.
Difficulty & Distance: With climbs at every grade from complete beginner to seasoned pro, you decide how hard it gets!

Title: Hereford and Breinton Springs
Summary:
Join Alex for a slow and largely flat 10km / 6 mile walk from Hereford Cathedral to the National Trust site of
Breinton Springs
.
This short walk starts and finishes in the cathedral grounds in Hereford and takes a leisurely stroll along the river Wye.
Along the way you get some stunning views of the cathedral and the river before ending up in a peaceful churchyard surrounded by ancient orchards and the possibility of coming face to face with some endangered wildlife in an SSSI. We also walk past the spot where a dog fell into the Wye more than a hundred years ago, inspiring Elgar to write one of the
Enigma Variations
. On our return to the city we take a loop through the site where
Hereford Castle
once stood.
The walk is planned for the end of September, so if you wished to stay in Hereford until the following day, you could join the
Queers & Beers monthly meet up
, which normally takes place on the last Sunday of the month. And if you were to arrive early, you could pop to the café in All Saints Church to check out a
particularly rude wood carving
.
If this is your first ODL event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the ODL website.
Note:
If there are no places left on this event, join the waiting list, as people frequently drop out during the week before an event, and you are likely to get a place.
Want to keep in touch with what's going on in the Midlands / ODL Central Region?
Join our
Central Region Telegram Chat Group
, and bookmark our upcoming events list via the short link:
https://outdoorlads.events/central
Image Credits:
All images provided by the walk leader and permission is granted to ODL for their use.
Difficulty & Distance: A 10km / 6miles flat river walk with a small bit of uphill in the middle

Title: An Evening with the Sheffield Symphony Orchestra
Summary:
**SOLD OUT
Join us for an evening of culture at Victoria Hall, with the Sheffield Symphony Orchestra, featuring our very own Andy Ling on the viola.
Prepare for a performance of overwhelming power and beauty with Messa da Requiem by Giuseppe Verdi—one of the most dramatic and moving works ever written.
From the barely audible opening to the earth-shattering Dies Irae, this is music that grips you from the first note and never lets go. Thunderous percussion, blazing brass, and a full chorus combine to create a sound world that is both terrifying and transcendent. Moments of intimate reflection give way to sheer sonic force, capturing the full range of human emotion—fear, grief, hope, and redemption.
Featuring a world-class orchestra, a powerful chorus, and outstanding soloists, this performance brings Verdi’s masterpiece to life in all its intensity. Whether you are a lifelong classical music lover or experiencing it for the first time, this is a concert that will stay with you long after the final note fades.Don’t miss this unforgettable musical experience.
Tickets for this event are now sold out. It is no longer possible to purchase  tickets online and it is unlikely that any will be available on the door.
We will go for a drink at a nearby pub after the concert.
Image credits  Main Image - Andy Ling; Image 1 - Samual  Sianipar  (free to use under the unsplash license); Image 2: -Samuel Ramos (free to use under the unsplash license); Image 3  Mea Morrowhart (free to use under the unsplash license)

Title: Sheffield Social - Peddler Market
Summary:
This is an easy-going evening where you can catch up with OutdoorLads you have met on previous events and make new friends. The event is open to new and existing members.
If you are new to OutdoorLads, our socials are great ways to meet other members and find out more about the group.
We will start the evening
Peddler Market
, where a variety of great food and drink is available. Later we may move on to explore bars and pubs in the Kelham Island area.
Image credits:
all images provided by the event leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: Social Event

Title: BIG20: Islands on the Edge - The Bus
Summary:
Saturday 13th to Saturday 20th June.
Join us for a week away on the Outer Hebrides to explore an archipelago of over 100 islands that stretch for 130 miles across the Atlantic Ocean.
This event page is for booking a space on the minibus
that we will be hiring for the week away - only book onto this page once you have a space confirmed on the main event page -
The Event
We will spend the first four nights staying at the amazing Blackhouses on Lewis before transferring on Wednesday to the five star accommodation of Nunton House for the remaining nights.  This event page is for travel on the minibus throughout the week, from Edinburgh on the outward journey, our various escapades during the week and the return trip to Edinburgh the following Saturday.
IMPORTANT TRAVEL INFORMATION
Please note
space is limited on the minibus so everyone will be restricted to two bags
-
one large
bag and
one small
day pack maximum.  It would also be helpful if the large bag is a rucksack or duffel bag, please don't bring a hard travel case, as these will be easier to fit into the space we have on the bus.
Outward Journey -
We will depart from Edinburgh Regent Road at 12:00 on Saturday 13th, with pickups at Stirling railway station at 13:30 and Inverness (Morrisons) at 16:30.
We have to be checked in at Ullapool BY 18:15,
therefore if you are not at one of these locations by the above times we will have no choice but to depart without you.
The ferry departs at 19:00, with arrival into Stornoway for 21:40, thence it's a 40 minute drive to the bunkhouse.
Return Journey -
The ferry departs from Lochboisdale at 14:45 on Saturday 20th, and we can drop off at Fort William railway station for around 19:30, Stirling railway station at 22:00 and Edinburgh at 23:00. (Our return ferry is further south than the outward one, so we don't go near Inverness on the return journey)

Title: Reading Pride - Sports & Outdoor Zone Stall
Summary:
Come join us as Reading celebrates Pride 2026
Reading pride are increasing the size and focus for their dedicated Sports & Outdoor activities zone within the pride event park.  So, whether you like climbing, bouldering, hostelling, cycling, camping, canoeing or day walks grab your OutdoorLads RED T-shirt and come help us encourage new members and help show Reading that OutdoorLads does #GetOutMore!
As part of Reading's commitment to helping the community get outside for their mental and physical well being we will have a stall in the Sports & Outdoor zone and this gives OutdoorLads the chance to engage with potential new members.  We are looking for 6 ODLer's to help cover the stall.
Reading is easily accessible so why not make a mini break of it and join us for a variety of events over the weekend!
Pride Events
Reading Pride is
free,
there will be a main stage and a number of different zones you are free to enjoy the rest of pride, part take in the planned sports events or just enjoy the day
New Members
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions then feel free to message me, or go to the "My First Event" section under "About Us".

All leader's own images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse

pride
