Title: Castle Combe and Slaughterford
Summary:
Located within the Cotswold Hills, with its rolling hills and picturesque villages, the Cotswold Hills were designated an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty in 1966 (now renamed as Cotswolds National Landscape) .  Castle Combe is a quintessentially English village often named as the ‘prettiest village in England’. It has featured regularly as a film location, most recently in The Wolf Man, Stardust and Stephen Spielberg’s War Horse. It was also used in the original Dr Doolittle film. The village has a rich history and the houses are made up of the honey coloured Cotswold stone, typical for a village of this area.
Today’s walk is a circular route starting and ending at the 14th Century market village of Castle Combe, that has a mix of both meadows and woodland.  We start by following the Bybrook River upstream to West Kington, before heading south, passing through North Wraxall, before we re-join the Bybrook River in Slaughterford.  From here we then follow the valley of the Bybrook River back up to pretty market village of Castle Combe.
Want to Get Out More?
Check out our
South Wales & South West Calendar View
Photo Credits
: All photos taken by event leader and permission is granted to OutdoorLads for further use.
Difficulty & Distance: 16.5km (10 miles) and steep in places due to hilly nature of the terrain. 157 metres at highest point. Total height gain 403 metres

Title: Sheffield Bouldering - All Abilities (beginners welcome)
Summary:
Swap trainers for climbing shoes and join OutdoorLads for an evening of INDOOR BOULDERING at The Climbing Hanger in Sheffield.
Cards/contactless ONLY  - this venue does not accept cash
If you haven't been to The Hangar before, please click
here
to pre-register. Click on the link, scroll down, select Sheffield and follow the on-screen instructions. Completing the waiver prior to arrival will get you climbing faster.
What is Bouldering?
It's a form of climbing where instead of long high routes, the 'problems' tend to be shorter and focus on technique, balance, body positioning and sometimes power. Because of this different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, harnesses and belaying, which makes it an excellent activity for newcomers to the sport. No wonder it's often regarded as the 'purest' form of climbing.
If you're a seasoned climber you won't need convincing about the benefits of bouldering, but for those who've never climbed more than a staircase, the benefits are wide ranging: from the physical - improved strength, tone, balance, power and coordination; mental - problem solving, focus, concentration and body awareness; to the emotional - relaxation, a sense of achievement and a feeling that mind and body are working together perfectly ('feeling psyched'). On top of all this, bouldering tends to be a very social sport where you can hang out with mates and each have a go as you feel regardless of what level you climb at.
I'm new to Bouldering - can I come along?
If you have never tried bouldering before it is recommended that you have a guided session from the event leader, who will go through everything you will need to know to boulder safely before joining in with the rest of the group.
I've been Bouldering before?
As above, everyone attending the event  must abide by the centre's T&Cs etc. As an experienced boulderer you responsible for your own safety, actions and involvement at all times. As such, you should use your own judgement in taking up advice given either by OutdoorLads Volunteers or other experienced climbers.
Photos : Copyright granted to OutdoorLads by the event leader / venue.
Please note that every person participating should be aware of (and accept) that those members perceived as ‘in charge’ or leading the event are not necessarily qualified, do not need to be qualified, and are not regarded by OutdoorLads, or themselves as ‘technical experts’ but are amateur climbers with some experience and who are happy to impart their knowledge. Any advice given should be considered with this in mind by the recipient.
All those attending the event must also be aware of the centre's own terms and conditions of participation and abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre’s staff. Everyone must also take responsibility for their own safety while participating in Indoor climbing events.
Difficulty & Distance: Suitable for all abilities. If you're new to bouldering, just let the event leader know and we'll get you started!

Title: A Wander round Wheelton and Brindle
Summary:
Join Stephen on an 8.5 mile walk in and around Wheelton and Brindle in Central Lancashire
Highlights of the walk will be-
Walking along the Leeds and Liverpool canal
Wondering around the picturesque village of
Withnell Fold
Meandering across the fields to the village of
Brindle
Taking in the church yard and maybe a swift drink at the Cavendish Arms in Brindle
Heading to Top O' th' Lane to then walk along the edge of
Denham Hill Quarry
On a clear day, you can see as far as the Lake District
Then heading along the remains of an old canal
Picking up the start of a series of locks on the Leeds and Liverpool canal at Johnson's Hillock -
Lots of photo opportunities here
.
Having a drink at the
Top Lock
pub -
Rude not to
!
Then wandering back to the start through Wheelton village or the canal
If open , we may be able to have a brew and cake at Whin's Kitchen cafe before we finish the walk
Image / photo credits:  All leader's own images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.
Are you new to Outdoorlads and considering your first event?
Have a look at this link, hopefully it will help.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
Don’t worry if the event is full.
Do sign up and join the waiting list.
Often those on the main list cancel nearer the time.
There is a very high chance you will still get a place
Difficulty & Distance: 8.5 miles with some inclines

Title: Summer Days in the Cairngorms
Summary:
Join us for three nights in the western Cairngorms
The plan for this weekend is to have a couple of big hill days in the area, there are
no low level options planned for this event
.
We have the western Cairngorm plateau that comes off the back of Braigh Riabhach with a couple of hills around the remote and little visited Glen Eidart. Also to our west is the Monadhliath, a lesser explored area but if you're bagging the munros there are four over there to be climbed. Plus it's not too far to drive to pick up other hills - Creag Meagaidh, the Laggan hills and as for Corbetts...!  Not expecting any snow to be left this summer, but the photos here were taken in June 2021.
Our base for the weekend is a Mountaineering Scotland hut at the top of Glen Feshie, simple but clean and comfortable accommodation and is a great location for getting up the hills that make the upper Speyside area. The hut has a mix of bunk beds and an alpine sleeping room, mattresses are provided but you will need to bring bedding.
Dogs:
Sorry, dogs are not permitted at this venue.
Photos:
Taken by AMD and used by OutdoorLads with full permission.

groups, and bookmark our upcoming events list via our
Scottish Calendar
.

Title: Bristol Indoor Bouldering - all abilities welcome!
Summary:
The Climbing Academy is one of the best bouldering centres in the South West. With over 200 problems in a 750 square metre area there is enough to keep even the avid of boulderers occupied.
Bouldering is a form of climbing where instead of long high routes, the 'problems' as they are referred to tend to be shorter and focus on technique, balance, body positioning and sometimes power. Because of this different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, harnesses and belaying, which makes the activities excellent for newcomers to the sport. For this reason, it is often regarded as the 'purest' form of climbing.
If you're a seasoned climber you won't need convincing about the benefits of bouldering, but for those who've never climbed more than a staircase, the benefits are wide ranging: from the physical - improved strength, tone, balance, power and coordination; mental - problem solving, focus, concentration and body awareness; to the emotional - relaxation, a sense of achievement and a feeling that mind and body are working together perfectly ('feeling psyched'). On top of all this, bouldering tends to be a very social sport where you can hang out with mates and each have a go as you feel regardless of what level you climb at.
This event is for members of all levels of experience and all are welcome to come along to any of the sessions.
To come along, you will need to register via the event page, and to climb you will need to pay an entry fee directly to the centre when you arrive. For entry fee prices go to:
https://www.theclimbingacademy.com/prices/
. As we start at 8pm you will pay the off-peak price.
For your first time climbing with us, especially if you are a novice climber, please contact the event leader in advance to let him know what your skills and experience are.
It is important that all members sign up through the event page to make sure the event leader has all information of the members attending.
Climbing and Bouldering Participation statement
Please note that every person participating should be aware of (and accept) that those members perceived as ‘in charge’ or leading the event are not necessarily qualified, do not need to be qualified, and are not regarded by OutdoorLads, or themselves as ‘technical experts’ but are amateur climbers with experience and who are happy to impart their knowledge. Any advice given should be considered with this in mind by the recipient.
All those attending the event must also be aware of the centre's own terms and conditions of participation and abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre's staff. Everyone must also take responsibility for their own safety while participating in Indoor climbing events.

Title: BIG20: New Forest Celebration Weekend!
Summary:
It's OutdoorLads' 20th anniversary! Come join us for a Summer weekend in the New Forest to celebrate this huge milestone... the BIG 20 events this year are a showcase of some of our very best events from the last 20 years, where we can enjoy a weekend celebrating all that OutdoorLads has to offer with some great activities and a few little surprises to mark the occasion, too!
We have a full weekend of events, details will be added once confirmed:
Sat 18/07 09:00am - Brockenhurst Park Run (no event page just turn up)
Sat 18/07 10:30am - New Forest Walk (free event)
Sat 18/07 10:30am - Cultural & Heritage event - visiting Beaulieu House & Motor Museum (pay on arrival event)
Sat 18/07 12:00pm - Paddle to Pub on the Beaulieu River (paid for event))
Sat 18/07   2:00pm - Brewery Tour (paid for event)
Sun 19/07 10:00am - New Forest Walk (free event - for those in no rush to head off)
The Accommodation
We have used this bunkhouse/hostel a number of times and you can expect dormitory style accommodation with some spare beds available allowing us to spread-out throughout the building, there is a decent size lounge and dining area.  Showers are in a separate block 10 metres from the Bunkhouse
Itinerary
The following is all subject to change but should give you an idea of what to expect if this is your first ODL hostel event.
Friday
You'll be able to arrive at the site from around 4.30pm on the Friday, and find a bed in the dorms. Once settled in, there is plenty of room to relax in the lounge, with hot drinks available throughout the weekend. If your preference is for an alcoholic tipple you'll need to bring these yourself.
Friday evening will be the traditional OutdoorLads buffet. Bring along something to feed one or two people, and home-cooked is always preferred.
Once we've had a briefing over the plans for the weekend, we'll be looking for some volunteers to tidy up the kitchen and dining room, whilst the others can head back to relax in the lounge (or outside if its warm!), chat to others or perhaps enjoy some board games (don't forget to bring your favourites!)
Saturday
After breakfast on Saturday morning you'll head out on your choice of activity (we'll list these out above as they are confirmed), returning to our accommodation early afternoon. This means you will likely need a packed lunch that you will need to bring yourself.
For those of you that like your Parkrun on a Saturday morning, Brockenhurst has you covered and we'll time our daytime activities to give you a chance to do both.
OutdoorLads hostel events always rely on everyone mucking in and helping in the kitchen over the weekend, so whilst a few volunteers are busy in the kitchen, we'll hopefully have some anniversary themed activities in the lounge.
We will provide dinner for the Saturday night, so please make sure your dietary requirements are up to date. We'll also raise a glass to OutdoorLads tonight with dinner! We will need some volunteers to clear up in the kitchen and dining room, but then it's a chance to natter to fellow OutdoorLads, listen to some music and likely that there will be board games available to partake in. Being mid-Summer, lets hope it's nice and we can enjoy being outside again, too!
Sunday
Start the day with breakfast and as much tea/coffee as needed to recover from the night before, and then we'll spend an hour or so tidying up the venue and leaving it as clean as we found it, we will need to be out by midday.
Our plan is to offer a short walk or cultural event on the Sunday for those that are not in a hurry to leave, but this is subject to volunteers stepping up to lead this for you!
Good to Know....
Dogs
Sorry, this event is not suitable for dogs. Registered animals are welcome, but please let us know at the time of booking if you will be bringing an assistance dog.
Car Sharing
The is parking available but sharing is good, so if driving to the event please consider offering spare seats to other members heading the same way.
Want to Get Out More?
To be informed of all our Southeast events please click on this
Telegram link.
OutdoorLads Foundation
Fancy coming along but having a difficult time of it (for whatever reason), and/or finding it hard to justify the expense? Apply for a place on this event through the OutdoorLads Foundation! Nobody apart from staff members will know and we don't need lots of financial info from you either. To apply through the Foundation, you'll need to complete a very short form, which can be found along with more information
here
.
Want to Pay By Instalments?
You can arrange to pay in instalments if that helps. Please contact the office on
support@outdoorlads.com
who will help you to secure a place and set up paying in instalments. The event must be fully paid up a week before the event takes place.
Images:
Big thanks to Colin, Glenn and James (ODL Leaders) for the event photos, used with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: A weekend of events and celebration!

Title: London June Social at The Rising SE1 (Date changed to avoid tube strike)
Summary:
Our monthly London pub socials are the perfect gateway to Outdoor Lads and our busy diary of brilliant events. For June, we're returning to The Rising in Elephant & Castle!
If you’re feeling hungry and fancy a bite, The Rising partners with Yard Sale Pizza, so you can order delicious pizzas to enjoy on the premises. They’ll even provide plates and cutlery.
Typically our socials have a rich mix of regulars, occasionals, and newbies, not only from London and the South East, but dropping in from across the country.
Whether it's hiking, camping, cycling, climbing, or a range of other activities - we do it!  Outdoors is our focus, but our socials are the chance to catch up with lads you've already met, find out about the group, and meet new members and event leaders.
Look forward to seeing you!
Is this your first ODL event?
If you have never been to one of our events before, attending your first event with OutdoorLads might feel daunting. What will happen, who will be there, and what can you expect? We know that new members in particular like to know in advance more about what an OutdoorLads event will be like. Check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the ODL website
.
If you have any other questions you can send a message to the leader.
---
Feedback from our London Social:
"it was my first ever event and I was super nervous walking in, but I was really welcomed, and ended up having a good evening, the guys were great. I heard about the ODL activities, I'm in!"
"I don't live in London, but was down for work that day, so I came along. Brilliant to catch up with some of the lads I haven't seen in a long while"

Title: BIG20: North Yorkshire Coastal Weekend
Summary:
BIG20 events are our programme to celebrate our 20th birthday year
- showcasing some of the best and most loved locations and venues we've been to in the last 20 years! We are returning again to one of our favourite hostels in the quiet and quaint village of Robin Hoods Bay, a small fishing village, surrounded by cliffs, 8km south of Whitby. The village, which consists of a maze of tiny streets, has a tradition of smuggling and there is reputed to be a network of subterranean passageways linking the houses. Situated at the end of Wainwright's Coast to Coast path and also on the Cleveland Way, the village is ideally located for coastal and moorland walks and lies within the North York Moors National Park.
Our accommodation is an independent hostel high up on the cliffs overlooking the village.
Itinerary
On Friday evening, there will be a briefing followed by the usual buffet - please bring a suitable food item.
A light breakfast will be provided on Saturday morning, following which there will be at least one walk. We may be able to offer more than one walk option, depending on leader availability. After the walk, there will be an opportunity to relax, explore the village and visit some of the excellent pubs.
On Saturday evening a meal will be provided in the hostel.
Following breakfast on Sunday morning, once we have cleaned and vacated the hostel, a shorter walk will be available for those who don't need to dash off.
Image Credits:
Provided by the hostel venue and OutdoorLads members. Used by OutdoorLads with full permission.
Dogs
Sorry this event is not suitable for dogs.

Title: Marshes & Meadows: Parkgate to Neston
Summary:
Hi all,
Join Jay as we explore another beautiful corner of the Wirral.
This relaxed, mostly flat walk is perfect if you’re new to OutdoorLads, or simply looking to meet new people and make friends in an easy-going setting.
We’ll start by heading along a scenic path towards Parkgate, with the option to grab an ice cream along the way. From there, we continue along the King Charles III England Coast Path towards Gayton, before looping back via Backwood Hall. We’ll then join the Wirral Way for a stretch, before making our way back to the cars.
To round off the day, we’ll stop at Nets Café for a well-earned cuppa and cake, just a short distance from where we finish.
Distance:
19 km (11 miles)
Dogs:
Well-behaved, socialised, and fully under-control dogs are welcome. Dogs must be kept on a lead where signposted and whenever we are near sheep or livestock.
New Members:
Please feel free to sign up to this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions, don’t hesitate to message me, or visit the “My First Event” section under “About Us”.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
If the event is fully booked, please join the waiting list, as places often become available in the week leading up to the walk.
Image/Photo Credits:
All images are the event leader’s own and are shared with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.
Difficulty & Distance: 11 mile mainly flat walk

Title: A Sunday Lunch Social and Leisure Walk in Llanbedr
Summary:
Come and join OutdoorLads for
Sunday
lunch and leisurely stroll in Llanbedr !
There’s something quietly special about an afternoon at the Victoria Inn in Llanbedr. Nestled between the mountains and the sea, with the wild beauty of Snowdonia close by, it feels less like a stop along the way and more like a place people naturally gather — where conversations stretch comfortably into the night and strangers rarely stay strangers for long.
The charm of the Victoria Inn isn’t only in its stone walls, warm fires, or the comforting rhythm of glasses clinking behind the bar. It’s in the social fabric that forms there every day. Walk through the door and you’re met with the kind of atmosphere that invites people to slow down, settle in, and share a moment together. Walkers fresh from the hills compare routes over a pint. Locals exchange stories that grow richer with every telling. Travellers find themselves welcomed into conversations as though they’ve been coming for years.
In a world increasingly shaped by screens and schedules, places like the Victoria Inn remind us how important simple togetherness can be. A pub table becomes a meeting point for laughter, debate, storytelling, and connection. Meals are shared, friendships strengthened, and ordinary evenings transformed into memorable ones through the company we keep.
Llanbedr itself seems to encourage this closeness. The village carries a slower pace, where people still greet one another warmly and community feels tangible rather than abstract. The Victoria Inn sits at the heart of that spirit — a place where muddy boots, holiday excitement, local pride, and genuine hospitality all meet under one roof.
Whether it’s a quiet drink after a coastal walk, a lively gathering with friends, or the comfort of hearing familiar voices at the bar, the Victoria Inn offers more than food and drink. It offers belonging. And perhaps that’s why people return to it time and again: not simply for the setting, but for the feeling of being part of something shared.
Our socials are a brilliant way for both new and long-standing members to connect. If you’ve been curious about joining
OutdoorLads
, wondering whether it’s the right fit for you, or feeling unsure about fitness levels or experience — this is the perfect opportunity to come along and see what we’re all about. There’s no pressure, just a friendly group of people, a relaxed environment, and the chance to make genuine connections.
So why not give us a try? Come and share a drink, a chat, and a few laughs — and discover why so many members have built lasting friendships through OutdoorLads. We’d love to welcome you. 🌸🍻
See
here
for further details and the menu etc...
Facebook page....
Photo's used with permission of the Victoria Inn
After lunch, we will aim to have a leisurely stroll up to the Nantcol waterfalls. Approx 3.5 miles in total there and back to the Victoria Inn.

event, so please join us all if you are in the area !

hills of Wales

Title: A Cotton Famine Reimagination - 2026
Summary:
Back for 2026 ! Join Andy and OutdoorLads for a satisfying summer solstice weekend Saturday leg-stretcher along the Cotton Famine Road.
We will be following the route where the unemployed cotton workers and slaves transported cotton during the panic of 1861 - 1865. From Rochdale, we will take a relatively straight path on the Cotton Famine Road, reaching the peak before dropping down into Rossendale village via the Pennine Bridleway, where we will stop for lunch and refreshments before making our way back via the tops of Lee Quarry in Bacup.
The Cotton Panic 1861 - 1865
Between 1861 and 1865, the communities of Lancashire and the wider North West of England experienced one of the most significant industrial crises in British history: the Lancashire Cotton Famine, also known as the Cotton Panic. At the heart of the region’s identity during the nineteenth century was the cotton industry, whose mills, warehouses, canals and railways had transformed towns such as Manchester, Bolton, Blackburn and Oldham into thriving centres of global trade. Yet this prosperity was deeply tied to the import of raw cotton from the southern United States, making Lancashire vulnerable to events far beyond Britain’s shores.
The crisis emerged during the American Civil War, when the Union blockade of Confederate ports disrupted the supply of baled cotton reaching Britain. At the same time, years of overproduction had already saturated international markets with finished cotton goods, causing prices to collapse. Speculators hoarded remaining cotton supplies in the great dockside warehouses of Liverpool and other ports, while the price of raw cotton rose dramatically. The once-powerful textile economy of Lancashire ground to a halt, and mill owners reduced production or closed altogether.
The effects on the historic mill towns of Lancashire were profound. Communities that had prospered during the Industrial Revolution suddenly faced widespread unemployment and poverty. Skilled mill workers, previously regarded as some of the best-paid industrial labourers in Britain, became dependent on charitable relief and the Poor Laws. Across the region, churches, civic leaders and philanthropic organisations established relief committees, soup kitchens and educational schemes to support struggling families. Sewing classes and industrial schools were introduced, reflecting Victorian attitudes toward self-improvement and public welfare during times of hardship.
The Cotton Famine also left a lasting mark on the physical and social landscape of Lancashire. Following the Public Works (Manufacturing Districts) Act of 1864, local authorities were permitted to borrow money to fund major civic improvements. Many towns used this opportunity to modernise sewer systems, improve roads, clean polluted rivers and create landscaped public parks—features that still shape the character of the region today. These projects not only provided employment but also represented an important phase in the development of Victorian urban infrastructure.
By 1864, cotton imports gradually resumed and the mills slowly returned to operation. However, the famine had permanently altered the region. Some towns diversified beyond textiles, while thousands of workers emigrated in search of opportunities elsewhere. The Lancashire Cotton Famine remains a defining chapter in the industrial and social history of North West England, illustrating both the region’s dependence on global trade and the resilience of its communities during a period of extraordinary hardship.
In order to facilitate a deeper understanding of what it was like to live in this area, there shall be some short breaks in order to reflect on the literary works of author Edwin Waugh, 1817 - 1890, including, "Home-Life of the Lancashire Factory Folk During the Cotton Famine". The readings will give OutdoorLads members a deeper understanding of what it was like for the people who lived in this area at the time and how their lives were impacted by the cotton famine; industrial revolution; slavery, and economical downturn.
You may also wish to bring your camera/binoculars to capture pictures from this amazing area.

Title: Isle of Purbeck Camp
Summary:
Join us on this smaller camp near the sea on one of the South-West's most beautiful coastlines. Explore the Dorset 'Isle of Purbeck' with its rolling hills, industrial heritage, rocky bays and caves, 3 mile long sandy beach (part naturist), heritage steam railway, and the mysterious Corfe Castle!
The area offers stunning coastal and country scenery. Walking along the South West Coast Path gives breathtaking views over the coastline and the area is also the eastern gateway to the Jurassic Coast.
The Jurassic Coast is England's first Natural World Heritage Site. The story of this coastline began 250 million years ago. The rocks record the Mesozoic era - the ‘Middle Ages’ of life on Earth - comprising the Triassic, Jurassic and Cretaceous Periods of geological time and they burst with the fossils of dinosaurs. Today the spectacular geology is beautifully exposed and accessible within the World Heritage Site.
The campsite has everything we need to have a great time, there are free showers and toilets as well as dish washing facilities along with a breakfast option for those who prefer not to cook.
Dogs:
Well behaved dogs are welcome at no additional cost, provided they are under control .
Campervans
:  Welcomed, dependant upon site availability and at additional cost.  Please contact the OutdoorLads office on
support@outdoorlads.com
or 01614200001 to enquire.
Photos:
Provided by Outdoorlads Members.
Difficulty & Distance: Walking 7 or 9 miles camping with coastal walking generally flat

Title: The Barmouth & Mawddach Panorama Incl. Dinas Oleu
Summary:
Join Andy and OutdoorLads for a winding Welsh woodland adventure in Barmouth and surrounding areas with spectacular views aplenty !
This is one of the most captivating walks along the Welsh coast, offering visitors an unforgettable journey through some of the most spectacular scenery in southern Eryri (Snowdonia).
Rising gently above the picturesque harbour town of Barmouth, this beautiful trail combines peaceful woodland, open hillside, and historic pathways to create a walk filled with stunning views and memorable moments at every turn.
As the route climbs above the town, we will be rewarded with breathtaking panoramic views stretching across the Mawddach Estuary, the golden coastline of Cardigan Bay, and the rugged mountain peaks that define this remarkable corner of Wales.
The ever-changing landscape provides a perfect backdrop for walkers, photographers, and nature lovers alike, with each section of the trail revealing a fresh perspective of sea, mountains, and countryside.
Along the way, we will pass through the enchanting Dinas Oleu nature reserve, a peaceful haven rich in wildlife, wildflowers, and fascinating local history.
Birds sing from the woodland canopy, coastal breezes drift up from the shoreline below, and the tranquil surroundings offer the perfect opportunity to slow down and truly appreciate the natural beauty of the area.
The reserve itself holds special significance as the first piece of land ever donated to the National Trust, adding a unique sense of heritage to the experience.
From colourful fishing boats bobbing gently in the harbour to sweeping sandy beaches and dramatic coastal cliffs, the scenery constantly changes throughout the walk, ensuring there is always something new to admire.
On bright, clear days, the far-reaching views extend for miles, creating picture-perfect moments that make every step worthwhile.
A perfect accompaniment to the
Shell Island Summer Camp Weekend
this walk offers a wonderful balance of relaxation, discovery, and adventure. Whether you are searching for a gentle scenic stroll, an inspiring photography route, or simply a peaceful escape into nature, this walk showcases the very best of the Welsh coastline and countryside.
Rich in natural beauty, coastal charm, and breath-taking viewpoints, the Barmouth Panorama Walk is more than just a trail — it is an experience that invites you to pause, explore, and fall in love with the landscapes of Eryri.
Well-behaved, socialised and under-control dogs are welcome on this event.
Dogs must be kept on a lead whenever this is signposted and whenever we are in the vicinity of livestock, and at the event leader's request. It's a good idea to check what the route may be like with the event leader before booking to find out more about the potential suitability of the walk for dogs.

Title: A Woodland and Waterside Wander to Darwen Tower
Summary:
Step into some of Lancashire’s most beautiful scenery on a refreshing walk through the Roddlesworth valley and up onto the wide‑open moorland above Darwen. We’ll wander past a chain of peaceful reservoirs, weave through ancient woodland, and climb to the iconic Darwen Tower for sweeping views across the West Pennine Moors. With varied terrain, rich local history and plenty of natural beauty, this route is the perfect blend of woodland calm and moorland drama.
Points of Interest:
Higher Roddlesworth Reservoir
A tranquil upper reservoir surrounded by dense woodland and quiet trails. Its sheltered position makes it a haven for wildlife—expect ducks, herons and the occasional deer moving through the trees. The reflections on still days are stunning.
Lower Roddlesworth Reservoir
Sitting just downstream, this reservoir is edged by well‑maintained paths and offers wider views across the valley. It’s a popular spot for walkers and a natural gateway into the Roddlesworth Woods network.
Roddlesworth Woods
A highlight of the route: a sprawling mix of conifer and broadleaf woodland with winding paths, streams and moss‑covered stonework.
Earnsdale Reservoir
Overlooked by the rising slopes of Darwen Moor, Earnsdale is known for its peaceful shoreline and the nearby Sunnyhurst area. From here, the landscape opens up and the moor begins to dominate the skyline.
Darwen Tower (Jubilee Tower)
The star of the route: built in 1898 to celebrate Queen Victoria’s Diamond Jubilee, this stone tower stands proudly on Darwen Hill. Climb the steps to the top for panoramic views stretching to the Lake District, the Yorkshire Dales, and even North Wales on a clear day.
Darwen Moor
A broad expanse of heather, peat and sweeping ridgelines. The moor offers a dramatic contrast to the sheltered woodland below, with big skies and far‑reaching views. It’s a classic Lancashire moorland experience—wind‑shaped, open and full of character.

Title: Inverbervie and Johnshaven Circular Walk
Summary:
Circular walk along the Aberdeenshire coast with inland return
Inverbervie is the birthplace of Hercules Linton, the designer of the famous tea clipper Cutty Sark. We head down to the beach and trace the coastline south to the fishing villages of Gourdon and Johnshaven. We then head slightly inland to Mill of Benholm and return to Inverbervie by an old coaching route with good views of the sea from the highest point of the walk at Gourdon Hill (136 metres). The route is by paths, old railway line and sections of beach. May be muddy in places.
Transport
Car parking (free) is available in the square and the Stagecoach X7 Coastrider bus service between Dundee and Aberdeen stops at the square.

Title: Dunnottar Castle and Woods Circular Walk
Summary:
6 mile circular walk to Dunnottar Castle and nearby Dunnottar Woods
Our walk starts in the market square in Stonehaven and we head towards the boardwalk along the shore before starting a short but steep climb uphill providing lovely views looking down to Stonehaven Harbour. In a short while we reach the War Memorial before continuing on the path to historic Dunnottar Castle. The plan is to visit the castle (optional) before continuing our walk slightly inland towards Dunnottar Woods and heading through the woods and back down to Stonehaven where there are ample opportunities to have fish and chips or ice cream  - or both!
Dunnottar Castle Prices
: £13.00 or £11.50 concession.
The castle is a ruined medieval fortress but with some surviving buildings. The Honours of Scotland (Scottish Crown Jewels of crown, sword and sceptre) were hidden in the castle to prevent Oliver Cromwell's invading army getting hold of them. The castle and surrounds affords some great photo opportunities.
Transport
There is a car park in Market Square (charge) but there are plenty of side streets nearby with free parking. The railway station is a 15 minute walk to Market Square. The X7 Stagecoach Coastrider bus service between Dundee and Aberdeen stops in Stonehaven.

Title: Doncaster - Relaxing, Calm Walk
Summary:
Join Shaun for a very relaxing, chilled, slow- paced leisure walk around Doncaster. 4.2km (2.62 miles).
This intriguing walk takes you through a tranquil area just outside Doncaster, away from the hustle and bustle of city life. Along the route, you’ll spot a variety of birds and ducks, as well as a range of beautiful flowers, making it a perfect setting to unwind, socialise, and enjoy nature.
There’s also an opportunity to explore some local history. The South Yorkshire Aircraft Museum is nearby and can be visited either before or after the walk. Entry is £8.50 for adults, and it’s open from 10:00 am to 5:00 pm. If you’re interested, feel free to check out their website:
https://www.southyorkshireaircraftmuseum.org.uk
About halfway around the lake, you’ll find options to grab refreshments, including Rustic Pizza Co or Subway—ideal for picking up food, drinks, or even an ice cream during the walk.
Dogs welcome.

Title: Branscombe Devon 2026 -Summer Camp and Beach
Summary:
One of the very popular summertime camping events we return to every year. This is a great little campsite with sea views and a shortish walk to the beach. We can have a BBQ and campfires and can walk to a lovely village pub as an option.
On Saturday we will be walking the coastal path to Sidmouth. Enroute we will stop on a secluded
beach
for a leisurely lunch . In Sidmouth we can have a wander for an ice cream or beer ;)
There will also be something organised for Sunday morning depending on demand and the weather.
The campsite whilst basic has everything we need to have a great time, there are showers and toilets with sinks in the cubicle as well as dish washing facilities.
Dogs:
Well behaved dogs are allowed on site when kept on a lead and upon payment of £3 per dog per night directly to the site on arrival.
Motorhomes, Campervans, Caravans:
Small campervans (i.e. VW Transporter) are welcome at no additional cost, with no EHU and likely on a grass pitch.
Campfires & BBQs
: This event will include a campfire/BBQ at the site.  Whilst our event leaders will take safety precautions there is still a risk to members. Please consider your behaviour around the campfire for your own and others safety.
Saturday walk
: This is from the campsite to Sidmouth. It is approximately 40 minutes walk to Branscombe beach where there is a cafe and small shop where you can get some supplies for the walk if you need to. From there we set off for Sidmouth.  There are no shops or treated water until we reach Sidmouth. We will stop for lunch and a rest on Weston mouth beach midway between Branscombe and Sidmouth. Note, the
beach
is popular with naturists.  It is approximately 2 hours walk from Branscombe beach to Weston mouth beach and a further 2 hours walk from Weston mouth beach to Sidmouth.
The walk is along the coast path that follows the sea cliffs. There are significant climbs and descents as we walk down into and out of the valleys that reach the coast. Some will find these challenging particularly in hot weather. Whilst you can take your time , a certain level of fitness is required. Other options will be available if you feel this may not be for you. There is also the option to drive to near to Weston Mouth beach and meet at the half way beach.
It can often be hot and the walk is somewhat exposed so please bring plenty of water (if you are unsure of quantity please ask the leader for advice)  and sun protection (cream /hat etc).
It may be useful to bring light shoes to walk on the pebbles as they are hard under foot. The waves can be powerful as the beach drops into the sea steeply , please be aware of this!
Underfoot it is a typical coastal path with varying terrain , walking boots are recommended.
Evenings / Nigh time
: There are various evening options and this will be weather dependent. We will aim to have a camp fire, where there will be music and talking, that may go on reasonably late. Whilst this will be kept to a minimum after midnight , noise does travel in an open field. If this is likely to disturb you please consider the fire pit location in relation to where you pitch your tent. Please be aware that we may be sharing the camp field with other groups that may be noisy and we will have little control over this. It is always advisable to have some good earplugs should this be likely to disturb you.
Photos:
By OutdoorLads leaders and used with permission.

Title: Ledsham and Fairburn Ings Evening Walk
Summary:
This easy 8km circular summer evening walk will begin in the pretty Yorkshire village of Ledsham, just 10 miles from Leeds City centre. Good paths and tracks take us from the village through fields and woods towards Fairburn Ings RSPB Nature Reserve. This former coal mine is now a centre for biodiversity including woodland, pools, grassland and marshland. Taking the path around the reserve we may see a variety of wildlife, including a Kingfisher. Our path home takes us back across fields and woodlands and into Ledsham. Hopefully a sunny summer evening will allow us to sit and have a drink in the beer garden at the Chequers Inn pub before we head home.
If the event is fully booked:
please join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.

Title: Settle to Stainforth ft. caves and waterfalls…
Summary:
Join Mike and OutdoorLads for a 9-mile circular walk from Settle to Stainforth in North Yorkshire. We will start our journey through the quaint market town centre before an uphill ascent via the Dales Highway, with spectacular views of the surrounding countryside and town below.
Our route will then take us through Attermire Scar; here we will pass the larger Victoria Cave and smaller Jubilee Cave, carved into the limestone cliff. Victoria cave was discovered in 1837 during the reign of Queen Victoria, hence the name. It is considered an important archaeological site due to the discovery of animal bones up to 130,000 years old! There was also the discovery of 11,000-year-old tools and artefacts, providing the first evidence of human settlement in the area.
On our descent into Stainforth we will pass Catrigg Force, a waterfall cascading over a deep gorge. The beautiful village of Stainforth awaits; we will stop for lunch here after crossing some stepping stones over Stainforth Beck. After lunch, we will pass another waterfall, Stainforth Force, before taking a gentle stroll along the bank of the River Ribble leading us all the way back to Settle. There will be an opportunity to stop for a drink at one of the local establishments in Settle at the end of the walk.
If the event is fully booked:
please join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.
All photos taken by walk leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: The walk is approximately 9-miles long through mixed terrain including a couple of steep uphill climbs. It can be quite uneven and rocky underfoot in places.

Title: Mynydd Mawr - An easier Eryri Panorama
Summary:
Mynydd Mawr (Welsh for 'big mountain') overlooks Llyn Cwellyn and is at the edge of the Moel Hebog group. It's actually by no means one of the biggest mountains but because it stands out it looks to be that way. On its western flank are the remains of ancient settlements and field systems. Set apart from the main mountain ranges it affords fantastic views without the crowds. The summit offers extensive views, on a clear day the views extend to the Llŷn Peninsula and Caernarfon Bay, Anglesey, Moel Eilio, Moel Hebog, Yr Wyddfa and beyond.
This event is ideal for those looking for a bit more of a challenge from a day walk, but who don't feel up to a whole day of full on mountain hiking. As mountains go, it's not a difficult ascent and as such is offered as a hill walk, but be in no doubt it is an uphill route and reasonable fitness is needed.
After leaving the meeting point we soon begin the steady ascent, initially through a forest, then on to open moorland, where the views keep on expanding. It's a well trodden path in the main and when we level out at the summit there's a 360-degree panorama. We will be going back down the same way, but as you'll be facing the other direction everything will look better.
Photos:
AndyM, event leader - taken from a previous trip, these give you a sense of the landscape you'll see.
Bad Weather
This event will be altered or cancelled if persistent heavy rain, high winds, lightning or poor visibility is forecast - you will be notified by at least 18:00 hours the night before in this case.
Dogs
are welcome to join us on this event but please note the following:
We may pass through areas with livestock where your dog needs to be on a lead.
Not everyone likes dogs so please ensure your furry pal doesn't worry other members.
There may be obstacles to carry your dog across, and your dog needs to have the stamina to keep up with the group.

Title: Foxton Locks and the Grand Union Canal
Summary:
Foxton Locks
is a Grade II listed site - home to the UK's longest and steepest staircase of ten locks. In 1810, an Act of Parliament authorised a new section of canal to be built from the Leicestershire and Northamptonshire Canal at Foxton to the Grand Junction Canal at Buckby. For the first time, boats and cargos could travel from the River Thames at Brentford all the way through to the East Midlands, including the coalfields of Nottinghamshire and Derbyshire.
However, a bottleneck of boats trying to pass through so many locks prompted the building of the
Foxton Inclined Plane
– a steam-powered mechanical lift system – alongside. The inclined plane had a journey time of just 12 minutes for two boats up and two down, compared with over an hour through the lock system. But dwindling canal traffic in the face of competition from the railways meant the Inclined Plane closed in 1911. Today, you can see the remains of this scheduled Ancient Monument and learn about its history in the Foxton Locks Museum.
Our walk takes us across the fields from Foxton to the village of
Laughton
and an opportunity to enjoy the tranquility of the beautiful rolling hills of South Leicestershire.
From Laughton, we head across to
Saddington
- a small hilltop village which overlooks the Saddington Reservoir, constructed in 1802 to feed the Grand Union Canal. We'll stop for lunch in the village churchyard which just happens to be right beside the
Queen's Head
pub for anyone who's in need of a pick-me-up!
Next, we head down towards the
Saddington Reservoir
before veering off to join the
Grand Union Canal
and a 4 mile stroll along the meandering tow path, passing Debdale Wharf Marina before arriving back at Foxton Locks.
With two pubs, two cafés and a canal museum, there'll be plenty of scope for further refreshments at Foxton Locks and to explore the bustling 34 acre site at your own pace.
Dogs are welcome:
Foxton Locks is a very popular visitor attraction – dogs will need to be kept on a lead. Our walk includes some short stretches on country roads and village streets. We may encounter sheep, lambs, cows or horses and there are a few fields where landowner signs request dogs on a lead.

Title: Delight in the Dassett Hills of South Warwickshire
Summary:
Join us for a beautiful 9.5 mile circular walk which begins with spectacular views from the top of the
Dassett Hills
and includes a collection of
South Warwickshire
villages and a peak at a National Trust stately home.
The
Burton Dassett Hills Country Park
comprises a group of ironstone hills, rising to 211m / 692ft above sea level. They are situated half a mile east of the M40 motorway and the Windmill Hill Beacon is a prominent landmark for drivers just north of Banbury.
Starting from the Beacon, we descend down to the small village of
Northend
, before turning to walk in the shadow of the hills across the fields to
Fenny Compton
. Here, the village hall was thrown into the national spotlight when chosen as a central meeting point for the start of the fightback by victims of the Post Office scandal.
Climbing up from the village, we join a short stretch of the
Oxford Canal
, before arriving at our lunch stop at
Wormleighton Reservoir
. This point also marks the start of the
d'Arcy Dalton Way
, a 65 mile footpath which meanders across to Gloucestershire before heading down through Oxfordshire to the Ridgeway Path.
Suitably refreshed, we'll head across the fields to
Farnborough
– not the Hampshire home of the International Airshow, but a much smaller, sleepy village which is just a few hundred yards from the county border with Oxfordshire.
We'll pause at the gates to Farnborough Hall, a magnificent Grade I listed stately home which has been looked after by the National Trust since 1960.
Soon, we join Warwickshire's
Centenary Way
- a 100 mile route, created to celebrate one hundred years of the County Council. With views across to Edge Hill – scene of a pitched battle of the First English Civil War in 1642 – we arrive at
Avon Dassett
and a potential pub stop at the
Yew Tree
.
With our thirst quenched, we'll climb back up into the Dassett Hills Country Park, passing the 12th-century
Burton Dassett
parish church and its holy well which still provides water. The park and the church were used as a location in the Tom Selleck 1990 film
Three Men and a Little Lady
.
Finally, we return to the Windmill Hill Beacon and another opportunity to enjoy the breathtaking views across Warwickshire, Oxfordshire and Northamptonshire. Bring a kite!
Dogs are welcome:
There are some short stretches on country roads and village streets and we cross an A-road. We may encounter sheep, lambs, cows or horses and there are a few fields where landowner signs request dogs on a lead.

Title: South Cornwall Double Day 1 - Polruan & Lantic Bay Circular
Summary:
The first of two day walks around the beautiful South Cornwall coast
This is a beautiful 9.5 mile circular route taking in part of the SWCP with stunning sea views and hidden bays. We'll start inland and work our way to the coast via the amazing Lantic Bay beach before stopping in the village of Polruan for a drink and making our way back. There will be a few ups and downs but it will be pretty relaxed with time to enjoy the beaches and outstanding scenery.
More information about the route can be found
here
.
Fancy making a weekend of it?
This is day 1 of a weekend of walks in the South Cornwall coast area. Day 2 is a circular walk doing more of the SWCP across the river in Fowey. See
Gribbin Head Circular
I'll be camping at the lovely
National Trust Highertown Farm
campsite. Welcome to join me there for beers under the stars and maybe even a fire on the beach. Outdoorlads currently have a 20% off discount for NT campsites. More info
here
.
If you're not a camper there's plenty of accommodation options in in the area.
Dogs
Your well behaved dog is very welcome on this walk but there at times where it will need to be put on a lead. There's also a lot of stone stiles to climb over, so you might need to carry your dog.
Photo Credits:
All photos taken by event leader and permission given to OutdoorLads for further use.
Difficulty & Distance: 9.5 miles. Some ups and down. Mostly along the coastal path or through wooded glens although there's a short stretch on the road

Title: South Cornwall Double Day 2 - Gribbin Head Circular
Summary:
The second of two day walks around the beautiful South Cornwall coast
This is a beautiful 8 mile circular route taking in part of the SWCP. We'll start in the pretty town of Polruan before taking the boat over to Fowey (£3.20 each). We'll then make our way to the coast and around Gribbin Head, passing Readymoney Cove Beach, Saint Catherine's Castle, Coombe Haven, Gribbin Head and Tower, and Polkerris. There will be a few ups and downs but it will be pretty relaxed with time to enjoy the outstanding scenery. We'll then get the boat back over the river to Polruan and return to the car parrk.

Title: Cambridge, Grantchester and Byron's Pool
Summary:
Join Alex for this ten-mile walk around colleges, parks and meadows in Cambridge and Granchester.
We will begin at Cambridge station and head into the city, crossing the wide open green of Parker's Piece, the historic field where the rules of football were codified in 1863. From there we will continue north through the streets of Cambridge until we reach the River Cam. We will follow the riverbank past the parklands of Midsummer Common and Jesus Green as the Cam curves through the city.
Turning south we will pass several constituent colleges of Cambridge University, including St John's, Trinity and the grandest of them all, King's. Here we will cross the river and turn south along the Backs where we will have views of King's College Chapel and the Mathematical Bridge, both symbols of the city, as well as punters gliding along the water with varying degrees of success.
Continuing southwards, we will leave the city centre and head through the suburb of Newnham, still following the River Cam as it leads us out toward open countryside. We will continue upstream, crossing Granchester Meadows and passing through the Orchard Tea Garden, once frequented by famous writers such as Rupert Brooke, EM Forster and Virginia Woolf. Our route will then take us through Grantchester itself, past the picturesque parish church and out of the village towards Byron's Pool, a section of the river named after the romantic poet who used to swim here.
Leaving Grantchester we will head east across Trumpington Meadows and the turn north where our route will join the tree-lined path beside Vicar's Brook, a small chalk stream. We will follow this wooded nature reserve back towards the city centre and our starting point back at the station.
Don't waste that space!
Summer always means lots of new and existing members excited to be getting back out more. Please only book if you know you can make it. If your plans change please free up the space for someone else.

Title: Lochinver 1: Watch the Summer Sunset over the Sea
Summary:
This is the first of three, consecutive, separate day events from Lochinver in the NW Highlands. Book one, two or all three events as you wish! Book your own accommodation if you wish to stay overnight, some suggestions below.
Day 2 (Saturday 27th June) here:
Lochinver 2: Quinaig
Day 3 (Sunday 28th June) here:
Lochinver 3: Canisp
Watching the sunset is, of course, subject to weather conditions but we will set off to hike one of the many coastal rises in the Lochinver area in time to wait on the summit as the sun sinks in the west, over the sea. Behind us will see the sunset colours, with luck, change the look of one of Scotland's most iconic mountains, Suilven. After we've have of fill of evening glory we'll return to Lochinver where we can have a drink or two before ending our day.
NB The three Lochinver-based events are currently limited to eight places. The remaining four places will be reserved for potential new members attending Highland Pride on June 6th. After that, any remaining places will be released.

Title: Lochinver 2: Quinag (Three Corbetts)
Summary:
This is the second of three, consecutive, separate day events from Lochinver in the NW Highlands. Book one, two or all three events as you wish! Book your own accommodation if you wish to stay overnight, some suggestions below.
Day 1 (Friday 26th June) here:
Lochinver 1: Watch the Summer Sunset over the Sea
Day 3 (Sunday 28th June) here:
Lochinver 3: Canisp
This is a stunning and quite complex mountain or three separate Corbetts to conquer. Some argue that this is one of the finest mountain walks in the Highlands. It has pointy peaks, open and airy ridges and amazing views (weather permitting!).
It's an 8.5 hour hike, with a total height gain of 1130m, over 14km / 8.75miles. It's a circular walk with spurs!. The Corbetts' names are Spidean Coininch, Sail Gorm and Sail Garbh.
Corbetts are a classification of mountain to heights of between 2500 and 2999 feet.
NB The three Lochinver-based events are currently limited to eight places. The remaining four places will be reserved for potential new members attending Highland Pride on June 6th. After that, any remaining places will be released.

Title: Lochinver 3: Canisp (Corbett)
Summary:
This is the third of three, consecutive, separate day events from Lochinver in the NW Highlands. Book one, two or all three events as you wish! Book your own accommodation if you wish to stay overnight, some suggestions below.
Day 1 (Friday 26th June) here:
Lochinver 1: Watch the Summer Sunset over the Sea
Day 2 (Saturday 27th June) here:
Lochinver 2: Quinaig
A challenging, out-and-back hike to the summit of Canisp. It's only 12km or 7.5 miles but it's across often pathless, boggy and stony ground making the going very slow at times. We will have 710 metres of ascent so we will be rewarded, with a bit of luck, with some very dramatic views from the summit. Iconic mountains such as Suilven and Stac Pollaidh dominate the sky-line, but the vastness of the landscape of Assynt can take your breath away. There are lots of mountains to see in the distance but unlike much of the Highlands where they're squashed in together, almost holding each other up, these rise individually and dramatically from a boggy flat landscape which is one of the richest carbon deposits in Europe.
Canisp is a Corbett, which is a classification of mountain between 2,500 and 2,999 feet. The summit is 847m high (2779 feet).
IMPORTANT -
DO NOT USE THE POSTCODE FOR SATNAV PURPOSES.
Follow the text directions.
NB The three Lochinver-based events are currently limited to eight places. The remaining four places will be reserved for potential new members attending Highland Pride on June 6th. After that, any remaining places will be released.

Title: Birmingham Canals and Edgbaston Resevoir
Summary:
Easy 8 mile circular walk from central Birmingham onto the Birmingham canal old line at Brindley Place before making our way to Edgbaston Reservoir. Edgbaston Reservoir, originally known as Rotton Park Reservoir and referred to in some early maps as Rock Pool Reservoir is a canal feeder reservoir maintained by the Canal and River Trust. This will be our lunch stop before following a hidden disused railway line to Harborne before joining the Worcester and Birmingham canal back to the city via the Mail Box.
Well behaved dogs are welcome on this walk.
There will be opportunity for a coffee or pint afterwards.

Title: Bridgnorth Circular Walk
Summary:
Join Samuel for a 10 miles/16kms walk around Bridgnorth in Shropshire.
We’ll begin in the charming “High Town” before making our way down to the river in “Low Town,” following the scenic riverside path. From there, we’ll meander along bridle paths, across fields, and through hidden woodland. The route takes us under the Severn Valley Railway line via a tunnel, then beneath the Oldbury Viaduct, passing Daniels Mill. Finally, we’ll return to town along the river and climb back up to “High Town” via the steep steps at Castle Hill.
Well behaved dogs are welcome on the walk but may need to be on a lead when passing through grazing land with livestock and horses.

Title: Lichfield Short Walk & Picnic in the Park
Summary:
Come join Samuel for a short walk on a picturesque 5miles (8kms) walk in Lichfield, a vibrant and historic city in Staffordshire.
We start our walk, in the centre walking past the Lichfield Cathedral, an Anglican cathedral & the only medieval English cathedral with three spires, we then make out way Into the surrounding countryside before returning to the historic city of Lichfield before an afternoon of music performed by Lichfield Concert Band (one of our members Patrick -dgered) will be performing band
Dogs will be welcome on the walk but will need to be kept under control.
Following this there will be a short intermission before taking your seats in Beacon Memorial Gardens for 2pm and enjoying a couple of hours of music performed by Lichfield Concert Band. Bring a picnic, blanket, foldable chairs or sit on the grass - for the concert I would like to request if possible, that you wear ODL branded items. Signature red t-shirts encouraged if possible. Anyone who doesn't have branded items they can be purchased via the office or online shop along with an array of other branded items, hoodies, hats and bags etc. Or maybe just a red t-shirt if you don't have OutdoorLads branded kit.
Concert is free of charge

Title: Birmingham Museum & Art Gallery
Summary:
Birmingham Museum & Art Gallery is set in a beautiful Grade II listed building that has overlooked the city’s Chamberlain and Victoria Squares since 1885.
It showcases many of the city’s collections and tells stories of Birmingham as a diverse, vibrant, and multicultural place, featuring highlights like the Pre-Raphaelites, Staffordshire Hoard, Birmingham History Galleries, Round Room, Industrial Gallery, Bridge Gallery, Wild City, and various exhibitions.
The tearoom and museum shop offer a fresh food and drink menu and unique gifts created with local artists and makers.
After the visit, there’s a chance to enjoy a casual drink or grab a light bite for anyone interested.

Title: Corserine and the Rhinns of Kells
Summary:
A high, airy ridge with big Galloway views, steady climbing, and a real sense of journey.
The Rhinns of Kells rise above the eastern edge of the Galloway Forest Park, in the south-west corner of Scotland and make for a fine ridge walk, the highest of its three peaks being Corserine at 814m, one of just seven Corbetts in the Scottish Southern Uplands.
Our walk will traverse the ridge in an anti-clockwise direction and if conditions are favourable we should enjoy some fine views across the Galloway Hills towards the Ailsa Craig and Arran in the West, the rolling hills of the Scottish Borders to the east and the Lake District to the south. If it is really clear we may even see the Isle of Man and Northern Ireland.
We will follow the route described on
WalkHighlands.
Photos:
taken on a previous visit to the Galloway hills in April 2019 (we'll try and get the flag the right way round this time!)
Difficulty & Distance: 10.5 miles (17km) with 903m of ascent

Title: The Natural Wonders of Malham
Summary:
Join Mike and OutdoorLads for a 10-mile circular walk in the Yorkshire Dales, starting and ending in Malham and passing some amazing geological features along the way.
Janet’s Foss
Firstly, our walk will pass Janet’s Foss, a beautiful waterfall cascading into the plunge pool below. It is believed that the waterfall takes its name from a fabled fairy queen who reportedly lived in the cave beside the falls. This spot is a haven for wild swimmers.
Gordale Scar
We will then head towards Gordale Scar, a breath-taking limestone ravine with two waterfalls and overhanging cliffs. For fans of ‘The Witcher’ on Netflix, Gordale Scar was used as a location for filming. This is a unique and interesting spot, where climbers can often be found tackling the crag.
Malham Tarn
An uphill ascent takes us towards Malham Tarn, a glacial lake 377 metres above sea level and one of only eight upland alkaline lakes in Europe. The site is owned by the National Trust and is designated as a wetland of international importance.
Malham Cove
For the final part of the walk we will arrive at Malham Cove, a curved limestone cliff formed by glacial meltwater at the end of the last Ice Age. We will walk along the limestone pavement at the top of the cove taking in spectacular views of Malham and the surrounding countryside. The Cove is a haven for climbers and a popular beauty spot. For Harry Potter fans, the limestone pavement at the top of the cove was used as a location for filming in ‘The Deathly Hallows’.
We will stop for lunch at the midway point of the walk. There will be an opportunity to stop for a drink at one of the local establishments when we arrive back in Malham at the end of our walk.
If the event is fully booked:
please join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.
Image credits: All photos taken by walk leader, Michael Rhodes. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: The walk is approximately 10-miles long through mixed terrain including a lengthy and steep uphill climb. It can be quite uneven and rocky underfoot in places, especially on the limestone pavement.

Title: Rowing Taster Day at Derwent Rowing Club!
Summary:
A great
opportunity to try out the sport of rowing
on the river Derwent in Derby with Derwent Rowing Club , working in partnership with OutdoorLads!
This taster day is designed to give a fun, all round introduction to rowing in a friendly, small club environment. The day is suitable for complete beginners, so come along even if you've never been in a rowing boat!
Throughout the day you will experience learning on the rowing machines, an introduction to rowing equipment and getting out on the water in a variety of boats, from learner single sculls to larger crew boats. It will be fun, relaxed, informative, and a great experience for all.
The cost for the full day is £45
, paid to the club on the day. This includes all equipment and a buffet lunch. The full day will be at least 10am-4pm. As we will be booking out a full days use of the clubs equipment and catering, please only sign up for this event if you are absolutely sure you will attend!
The leaders partner, Rob Thornton, is an OutdorLads member and has been rowing for 20 years. He is a British rowing qualified coach and vice captain and club safety officer at Derwent Rowing Club, and will be running the day for us.
Tea and coffee will be available throughout the day and a buffet lunch of sandwiches/wraps provided (please let us know of any dietary requirements in advance). The event will go ahead whatever the weather, only thunder or high winds would lead to cancellation. We do row in the rain, but we have a warm clubhouse to dry out in!
Have a look at ‘instagram@derwent_rowing_club’ to get an idea of what the club is like and what the sport of rowing looks like.
On the day you will be required to sign a temporary membership form that includes a medical declaration and swim ability question. This brings you under the clubs insurance and allows us to best keep everyone safe.
If you have any questions please contact Rob at
safetyofficer@derwentrowingclub.co.uk
You will be sent the taster day pack PDF which details the location of the club, what to bring and what to expect.
All Derwent members helping on the day are volunteers. If the event fills up very quickly and has a large waitlist we will ask more volunteers to help for the day to try and increase the event size. If we are unable to do this please look out for future events, we’d love to see you on the water!
Photos:
Photos by Rob Thornton / Derwent Rowing Club and used by OutdoorLads with permission.
Dogs
: This event is not suitable for dogs.
Difficulty & Distance: New experience and energetic, but well in the grasp of most people

Title: Bury to Bromley Cross -GM13
Summary:
This is stage 13 of 20 on the Greater Manchester Ringway, a 200 mile (322km) walking trail exploring all 10 boroughs of Greater Manchester. You can find out more about it
here
.
Highlights include:
Riverside walking along the Irwell with views, if you’re lucky, of passing steam trains
The delightful Holcombe Brook in its narrow tree-lined valley
A gradual ascent of Holcombe Hill topped by the famous Peel Tower, offering stunning views across the West Pennines and Manchester
Semi-ancient woodlands and a waterfall in the peaceful Redisher Wood
Affetside village with its ancient cross and hilltop location on a Roman road
Note that parts of the trail may be damp, muddy and slippery at any time of year and especially after periods of wet weather.
This is a linear walk
. See the options in the location details below.
Well-behaved, socialised and under-control dogs are welcome on this event.
Dogs must be kept on a lead whenever this is signposted and whenever we are in the vicinity of livestock, and at the event leader's request. It's a good idea to check what the route may be like with the event leader before booking to find out more about the potential suitability of the walk for dogs.
New Members

My First Event
page.

Difficulty & Distance: Around 19km (12 miles), 514m ascent

Title: Gran Canaria Adventure Week 2027
Summary:
The last four years have proven to be hugely successful and popular...
so we're doing all it all again in 2027!!!
Gran Canaria Adventure Week
is back again for
January 2027
. We have some of what you've loved from previous years, plus new events and ideas to give you an even better winter sun experience.
Feedback from previous years:
"The socials were well organised and the group events were excellent"
"Loved the ability to do as much or as little as we wanted throughout the entire week"
"A fantastic event with something for everyone"
"Good range of activities to keep everyone happy, from leisure city tours to high octane hikes, canyoning, via ferrata - something for everyone! And the organisers are friendly and welcoming, feels like family which is nice"
"All the event leaders were great and super friendly and easy to talk to and ask questions"
Think you know Gran Canaria? Think again!
Let your next (or first!) visit to Gran Canaria be one filled with outdoor adventures. While the island is known as a year-round party destination for LGBTQ+ visitors, this trip is an opportunity to experience something different:
take part in outdoor activities; experience the beauty of the "real" Gran Canaria; and meet friends from around the world.
We are, once again, partnering with
CanCanaria
to bring you a full week of fun and adventurous activities. CanCanaria is a gay-owned and run company based on Gran Canaria, using their extensive local knowledge and the island's magnificent natural beauty to create a programme of excursions and trips that'll leave you with unforgettable memories and stories to tell. At the helm is former OutdoorLads' Chairman Patrick, so you know you will be in good and welcoming hands. Patrick and his team use trusted local volunteers, guides and instructors who are LGBTQ+ friendly, and who hold appropriate insurance and qualifications where necessary.
The
CanCanaria Adventure Week
is festival dedicated to outdoor activities, combining adventures with a holiday in the sun and sociable evenings. Expect
Hiking, Climbing, Sailing, Surfing and Scuba Diving alongside tourist trips, socials, restaurants, bars, a pool party and boat trips.
How do I sign up?
Sign up to this OutdoorLads event
in the usual way. This doesn't get you a place on the CanCanaria event, but it will enable us to see how many of our members are attending, make arrangements together and share details and information easily in the run up to the trip.
Sign up to the CanCanaria Activity Week
on their website
by following this link
. This step is free and simply gets you a place on the overall event. It will also enable CanCanaria to contact you with details of the activities as they become available. You'll be asked to indicate the types of activities you are interested in, and the organisers will then use that information to formulate the final programme.
Sign up to whatever combination of activities and parties you fancy via the pages which CanCanaria will publish in due course
. If you've signed up at Step 2, you will also be notified as the various activities get arranged.
***VIP Offer***
CanCanaria have put together a VIP package. For a fixed price, you get:
50 euro voucher to spend on Adventure Week activities,
only for orders made before September 1st 2026
VIP Breakfast/Brunch
- only available to those with VIP Tickets (10:00 - 12:00: Wednesday 27 January)
Free Entry To:
The Welcome Party, (Monday 25 January)
The Main Pool Party, BBQ included (Wednesday 27 January)
The Sundown BBQ, men only event - BBQ included (Friday 28 January)
Office Adventure Week 2027 T-shirt
One CanCanaria Passport
- a book of discount vouchers to use in bars, shops and restaurants in Maspalomas
Those of us who have attended in previous years have had an amazing trip. There has been a great range of activities which really showed off the beauty of the island, and the evening socials (in a different bar each night) meant there was a regular opportunity to catch up with everyone, get to know your fellow travellers and ask any questions of the events team.
Check out some of the photos on this page which are from previous years!
It is entirely up to you whether you come to Gran Canaria for some or all of the week, or indeed whether you arrive earlier or leave later to make it part of a longer trip.
Here is the Program of Events for 2027 so far, with links to the activity pages where they are ready for booking:
Monday 25th January
The Rainforest Island Tour
Hiking
Via Ferrata
Afternoon Group Workout
Adventure Week 2027 Official Opening Party
Adventure Week Opening Night Social
Tuesday 26th January
Strength and HIIT Beach Workout – Playa del Ingles Beach
Hiking
Learn to Surf – Group Surf Experience
SUP (Stand up Paddleboarding) and Snorkel Tour
5km Run with 20-minute Beach Workout and 10-minute Stretching and Mediation
Adventure Week Social
Wednesday 27th January
Canyoning
Stretch and Meditation on the Beach
Adventure Week Brunch
The Big Balls Sportswear Pool Party
Afternoon Walk
Group Workout
Adventure Week Social
Thursday 28th January
Strength and HIIT Beach Workout – Playa del Ingles Beach
Discover Scuba Diving
Hiking
10km Walk and Talk (Mental Health) followed by Workshop on Goal Setting for 2027
The Volcano Heart Island Tour
Snorkelling Tour
Adventure Week Sundown BBQ
Friday 29th January
Hiking
Mountain Buggy Tour
Adventure Week Social
Saturday 30th January
Hiking
Sunrise 5K Run and Mindful Stretch
Sunset Kayaking
Official Closing Party
