CanCanaria Beach Games – Gay Beach Kiosk 5
Farewell Drinks
Accommodation
Gran Canaria has accommodation to suit a variety of budgets, from basic self-catering apartments, to full service hotels and villas.
You are welcome to message the event leader or Patrick at CanCanaria for accommodation ideas, and to use the event page comments to arrange sharing. In due course we will also set up an OutdoorLads Telegram group too.
Flights & Transfers
Gran Canaria is easily accessible from various UK airports, with a flight time of around 4 hours. The transfer from the airport to Playa del Ingles is straight forward, either by public bus, taxi or pre-arranged holiday transfer service.
Please note: This is not an OutdoorLads event and is arranged by an external organisation, CanCanaria. The OutdoorLads refund policy does
not
apply and your contract is with Cancanaria. No insurance is provided by OutdoorLads. This is simply an event page to enable members to co-ordinate their holiday and activities together.
Photos:
Used by OutdoorLads with permission of event leader and CanCanaria.
Difficulty & Distance: You can make this week as easy or difficult as you like - you are in control of the activities you book.

Title: Let's go roamin' among Roman ruins (and a pub lunch)
Summary:
Starting from St Albans City station, we will enjoy this 8.50 mile bimble exploring
Roman Verulamium
.  We will head through a small part of town, then amble alongside the River Ver on the Hertfordshire Way.  Much of our path will be on the grounds of the Gorhambury Estate, which is full of Roman, Elizabethan and 18th century history.  We will pass the Roman Theatre of Verulamium (built circa AD 140), pass Gorhambury House on our way to
Old Gorhambury House
, a Grade I listed manor house, built between 1563-1568.  This English Heritage site is free to enter and we will allow time to explore the ruins.
Just after 4.5 miles in, we will stop for a wonderful pub lunch at
The Holly Bush
, located in the leafy village of Potters Crouch.  As of the date of publishing, I am working on the pre-order menu with the pub and that will be shared later.  Expect slightly upscale pub grub, with the likes of pave of pork belly, pappardelle beef ragu, lamb rump and artisanal pizzas!
After lunch we will head back toward St Albans, passing through Park Wood and back onto the edge of the Gorhambury Estate.  As we near St Albans Cathedral we will see sections of the Roman wall bordering the estate, and up to the Great Gateway of the Monastery.  This gateway has existed since roughly AD 948 and served as the entrance gate of the almonry school ( a medieval monastic charity school).  This is now the £30,000 a year Sixth Form St Albans School.
Depending on what time we back at the cathedral grounds there may be time to explore the cathedral itself, although it does close at 5.00 every day.
Our walk will end at the cathedral grounds and we can all make our way back to the station (walking up Holywell Hill to Victoria Street.  Depending on your return train times, feel free to explore the shops and pubs along Victoria Street.
The event booking will close at 17.00 on 12 August, in order to allow the pub ample time to prepare.
Photos provided by
www.unsplash.com
and OutdoorLads members and used with permission and thanks.
Difficulty & Distance: Flat walk around St Albans

Title: The Geordies are Coming, a Toon Social
Summary:
Join the OutdoorLads in a celebration of our independence from the USA  on the fourth of July!
This event is a good chance to experience a night in one of the friendliest and liveliest cities in the UK.
Starting very close to the Monument Metro station, we will be hopping from venue to venue and experiencing some of the best this city has to offer.
We will generally be heading to bars in a southerly direction and finishing in the vicinity of the gay scene.
Visiting bars for both cocktails and beers , an ideal event for new members to make contact and for other active members to catch up with the group and hear what is happening locally.
Image credits:
Images provided by event leader and co-leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them
Difficulty & Distance: Social event with limited walking between venues

Title: Top o' th' Calder, Todmorden.
Summary:
We will explore the upper extreme of the Calder Valley between Todmorden and Cliviger.
The infamous River Calder ("violent water") is mostly a culverted stream at this stage of its life.
We will pass close to its source at Heald Moor (the source of two River Calders!).
The valley here is at its steepest, bleakest, quietest and remotest. It has its own unique beauty in comparison to further down the catchment.
We will have hills, great views and water! The walk is approximately 10 miles with a 580 metre total ascent.
We will call at The Bridestones, approximately 430m above sea level. They were formerly the abode of druids and the site of ritualistic and sacrificial ceremonies. It was probably named after Bridia, a Brigantes deity. Legend also suggests weddings took place on the moor giving the "Great Bride Stone" and smaller "Groom Stone" their name. This is also a key filming location for Emerald Fennell's 2026 film adaptation of Wuthering Heights. This section of moorland is now community owned and currently a conservation and rewinding programme.
We will follow the Calder way To Pudsey Clough and straddle the Lancashire borders before heading up and along the opposite side of the valley by Dobroyd Castle, an important, historic building built in 1866 and continue back to Todmorden.
This is a dog friendly walk, so well behaved dogs welcome. There are a few stiles on a small section, so bigger dogs might need extra hands.
We will finish in The Golden Lion for a great selection of beers and Thai food.
Image / photo credits:
All leader's own images - OutdoorLads has permission to use and them.
If the event is fully booked:
please
join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.
Difficulty & Distance: 10 miles, 580m ascent

Title: Leeds Pride Parade 2026
Summary:
Come and  celebrate Yorkshire biggest Pride event at Leeds Pride with OutdoorLads
We will march as part of the pride parade to help celebrate Outdoorlads (and ourselves) and be part of a beautiful proud day in the LGBTQ+ pride calendar.
The parade is organised by
Leeds Pride
The parade starts at 12.15pm but we will need to meet up to prepare for our place in the parade at least one hour before. No experience is necessary. We will have banners and flags to wave. Please wear your red Outdoorlads t-shirt. If you don't have one you can purchase one from the outdoorslads online shop. Otherwise, feel free to wear what you want!
If the weather is nice then we will find a place to sit in the centre to have a bite to eat (you can bring your own or purchase something in Leeds).
If you don't wish to take part in the parade please come along to our
Leeds Pride Social
event later in the day.
Image credits:
all photos provided by the event leader and OutdoorLads member Wilk82). Outdoorlads has permision to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: Parade march first but then you're free to do as you wish after.

Title: Leeds Pride 2026 - The Social
Summary:
Join us for
Leeds Pride 2026
There’s lots of fun to be had at Leeds pride this year from market stalls to live entertainment, with a variety of music, performers and dancing throughout the day (and late into the evening too)
This social event takes place after the Pride Parade.
Please sign up separately for the parade
if you would like to participate.
We will meet in the heart of  the gay area just outside the Bridge Bar where we can enjoy the celebration. We will head up to Lower Briggate where there will be a variety of  great activities, including entertainment on the main stage. Later on, we will visit  some of our favourite bars including Blayds Bar, Viaduct and Queens Court. We may even head to New Penny to dance the night away!
Come along and join us for a rite Yorkshire celebration. You won’t be disappointed!!!
Difficulty & Distance: Pride / Social Event

Title: Mytholmroyd, Cragg Vale Coiners Day Walk (summer edition)
Summary:
Starting and finishing in Mytholmroyd, this walk traces the bottom of the Cragg Vale Valley along the edge of Cragg Brook stream. The walk meanders through the valley's tight woodland and between the remains of its industrial past. Leaving the woods, we pass by a disused textiles mill before arriving at the small settlement of Cragg Vale. From there we will zigzag upwards past centuries old properties, through working sheep farms and onto the exposed, gritty moorlands that define the Yorkshire landscape. After descending, the route returns to Mytholmroyd along the canal towpath.
Total distance 13Km (8 miles). There will be some steep uphill sections (total height gain: 426m)
Option to go for a pint in one of Mytholmroyd's pubs after the walk.
Please bring appropriate clothing and footwear.
Dogs:
unfortunately this walk is not suitable for dogs due to working sheep farms and other livestock.
Waiting List:
If this event is fully booked and you would like to attend, please add yourself to the waiting list. Places often become available closer to the date, and it is likely you will still get a place.
New Members:
If you are thinking of making this your first event with ODL and have any concerns or questions, please don't hesitate to drop me a message, or go to our
'My First Event
' page.
Start times:
We will aim to start on time, but appreciate different people have different needs. To accommodate this, I will aim to be at the meeting point 20 minutes before the start time (I will be wearing a bright red Outdoor Lads T-shirt). We will wait up-to 10 minutes after the start time for people to arrive before setting off.
Route:
mapped on OS Maps App, available from: BlobFish85
Photos:
All photos taken by event leader, ODL have permission to use.
Difficulty & Distance: 13km (8 miles) on a variety of terrain with some hills

Title: Ashurst Lunch and Stroll
Summary:
Join us for this Sunday summer afternoon in the New Forest.
We'll start with lunch at The New Forest pub, right by Ashurst station. You can
view their menus here
, which includes traditional Sunday lunch options.
We'll then burn off the calories in a short circular walk from Ashurst along the edge of the Deerlepe enclosure, a chance to see some lovely New Forest countryside.
Possibly finishing with a drink back in Ashurst if people are keen!
Photos taken by @anthonyg86
Waitlist
: if this event is fully booked and you want to join us, add yourself to the wait list because places often become available closer to the event date. Unfortunately I cannot expand the table booking with the venue on this occasion.
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is 4 miles / 6.5km. Terrain is mostly flat and on trails.

Title: Upper Cwmbran and Twmbarlwm Hill Fort Walk
Summary:
A leisurely 18 km walk up through the Blaen Bran Woodland and disused Reservoir, then ascending and along the most Easterly ridge of the Welsh Valleys to get to the Iron Age Twmbarlwm hill fort; with excellent views of Cwmbran, Newport, Cardiff and across the Severn Estuary to Avonmouth and Exmoor.
We’ll start off in the Blaen Bran Woodland, seeing evidence of the old colliery, brickworks and the Blaen Bran Reservoir. Then quite a long climb will take us to the top of the Myndd Henllys ridge, with excellent views over Cwmbran and Wale’s largest ancient woodland on the horizon. We’ll then head South along the ridge to ascend the Iron Age Twmbarlwm hill fort (419m), with excellent views of Newport, Cardiff and across the Severn Estuary to Avonmouth and Exmoor. Then back along the ridge over Myndd Maen (458m) and on to Myndd Twyn Glas (472m) before heading back down throught the Blaen Bran Woodland.
Dogs:
Dogs under close control are welcome on this event, so long as owners are able to lift them over locked gates and high stiles.
Photo Credits
: All photos taken by Paul Ridealgh; permission is granted to OutdoorLads for further use.
Difficulty & Distance: 18km walk walk with 500 metres of ascent. Fairly short, fairly but steep climb over rough ground and bracken

Title: Indoor Climbing Kendal - (Experienced Climbers)
Summary:
This event is intended as a safe space for the LGBTQ+ community; everyone will be warmly welcomed
Join us for a full day at one of the UK's tallest walls. With big over hangs and feature walls up to 25m high it offers both fun and more challenging routes!
Most of the centre is set up for lead climbing with a few top-rope setups so this sessions is better suited to experienced climbers. If you have done some leading at other centres you will be fine.
Climbing is the main focus of the event, but this is also a great chance to meet other members and build lasting friendships. Many Outdoor Lads members join through climbing, so if you're new to the group and want to know more, come along and chat over a cuppa during the tea and cake break.
I'm new to Climbing - can I come along?   If you've never climbed before but want to give it a try - then OutdoorLads run a weekly event in Manchester (and many other cities across the country!) where we're more than happy to show you the ropes  and teach you everything you need to know to climb safely, but unfortunately this event is for climbers who do not need to be supervised - and allows our volunteer leader to push themselves a little more. Therefore, if you answer NO to any of the following three questions, then please check out some of our other local climbing events which will be more suitable for you;
Can you fit a climbing harness correctly?
Can you tie in to a rope using the correct knots?
Can you belay another climber correctly and confidently?
Everything else you need to know
You will need to register with Kendal wall before your first visit to the centre but you only need to do this once. If you are an experienced climber, you can save time and do this online
here
, selecting the full registration option.
Photos provided by j.hutton
Difficulty & Distance: This centre has plenty of lead climbing walls making it more suitable for experienced climbers. Some top rope walls are available.

Title: Bike Week 2026: World Naked Bike Ride London
Summary:
Bike Week 2026 is taking place from June 9–15, and it's our chance to be part of the UK’s biggest celebration of cycling
Whether you ride every day or haven’t cycled in years, Bike Week is all about showcasing how cycling can fit into daily life - for everyone.
Join Outdoorlads and around 1000 other cyclists on the annual World Naked Bike Ride (WNBR) in London. It's a free, fun,  legal event, run by volunteers with the aim of making the world a better place for everyone. You can see a preview of the ride at
Video for participants - WNBR London
Cleaner environment, safer roads, healthier lifestyles, better attitudes
The ride demonstrates the vulnerability of cyclists while celebrating body freedom and is a protest against fossil fuels and car culture.
Bare as you dare
As it's a "naked" ride, clothing is optional. OutdoorLads requires you to wear a helmet and suitable footwear (trainers / cycling shoes)
There is
no pressure to be completely naked
. The event has a carnival feel, so you are encouraged to adorn yourself with colourful body paint and slogans, wear fancy dress, disguise yourself a bit (eg sunglasses, helmet, mask) or remain partially dressed – be as bare as you dare.
Many passers by and photographers who know about the event will take photographs before the event begins and along the route. There will be lots of people with cameras and phones. If you're at all uncomfortable with appearing naked in photos, you're welcome to wear shorts, speedos, etc. You may prefer to start the ride with your clothes on.  We stop soon after the start to allow everyone to join up, so that can be a good time to undress.
Photo credit
: All images taken/created by the ride reader and used with permission
Difficulty & Distance: A fun leisure cycle

Title: Maghull to Liverpool canal walk (LLC#8)
Summary:
The linear walk from Maghull to Liverpool follows a stretch of the 204km (127.25 miles) Leeds & Liverpool Canal, which opened in 1816 after more than 40 years of construction.
This is the
final stage (8 of 8)
which is covering the full length of the Leeds & Liverpool Canal.
Starting in the leafy town of Maghull, the route moves from the quiet to the vibrant as we pass into the heart of Liverpool city.
There's still charm on the canal as we pass through green spaces in the urban sprawl, but as we close in on our destination we'll be passing through Bootle with many old warehouse, bridges and remnants of Liverpool's maritime heritage.
It’s a fitting finale to a journey that spans countryside, towns, and the industrial legacy of northern England - passing from one powerhouse city to another.
For anyone that wants to join, we'll have an optional pub trip in Liverpool before our return to celebrate the completion of the Leeds & Liverpool Canal.
We'll return by public transport (for those that need to get back to Maghull) - however the best option will depend on your individual onward travel plans.
Stage 1: Leeds to Bingley - 30 Aug 2025
Stage 2: Bingley to Gargrave - 20 Sep 2025
Stage 3: Gargrave to Burnley - 21st Feb 2026
Stage 4: Burnley to Blackburn - 21st March 2026
Stage 5: Blackburn to Adlington - 11th April 2026
Stage 6: Adlington to Parbold - 16th May 2026
Stage 7: Parbold to Maghull - 6th June 2026
Stage 8: Maghull to Liverpool - 27th June 2026
Dogs:
No dogs on this leg of the walk due to the length of walk and narrow towpaths which will be busy with cyclists and other walkers.
If the event is fully booked:
Please
join the wait list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.
If this is your first OutdoorLads walk event:
If this is your first event, we’ll make sure you feel welcomed. Please
check out the My First Event page
, and don’t hesitate to drop me a message if you have questions or want to know more about this walk.
Image / photo credits:
All images are those of OutdoorLads leaders - with permission for OutdoorLads to use.
Difficulty & Distance: Total distance of 22 km (approx. 13.5 miles). Flat, on paved towpaths. Climb of 34m total. Approximately 6.5 hours including lunch.

Title: Parbold to Maghull canal walk (LLC#7)
Summary:
The linear walk from Parbold to Maghull follows a stretch of the 204km (127.25 miles) Leeds & Liverpool Canal, which opened in 1816 after more than 40 years of construction.
This is
Stage 7 of 8
which will span the full length of the Leeds & Liverpool Canal.
Starting in the charming village of Parbold, the route weaves through the heart of Lancashire’s countryside and heritage with a blend of rural tranquility and industrial echoes.
We'll take in a very peaceful stretch of the canal with wildflowers and even a sunflower field, but we're never far from the clues of the canal's industrial heritage. The path skirts around Ormskirk before heading south to Maghull where our walk finishes.
It's recommended to travel by public transport, but for anyone driving you'll need to check your return journey (more details under the map). The best option will depend on your individual onward travel plans.

Stage 2: Bingley to Gargrave - 20 Sep 2025

check out the My First Event page
, and don’t hesitate to drop me a message if you have questions or want to know more about this walk.
Image / photo credits:
All images are those of OutdoorLads leaders - with permission for OutdoorLads to use.

Title: The Saltings and Moorings of Manhood - The Annual Nudist Beach Walk
Summary:
A manhood or two might well be seen on the isolated naturist beach we'll visit, but Manhood is also the name of the peninsula that stretches south from Chichester to Selsey Bill. It is a strongly horizontal landscape of contrasting blocks of colour. Wide golden wheat fields and broad brown mudflats are backed by big skies, while the regular rows of poplars, the massed masts of yachts and the sporadic spires of churches provide vertical punctuation. Much of the area has been reclaimed from the sea and is drained by the ditches we'll follow, along with the Chichester Canal. The scattered settlements domesticate an expanse so flat that despite walking 17 kilometres, we'll not climb at all.
The sights:
The Manhood Peninsula:
The southernmost part of Sussex and part of the Sussex coastal plain. Bordered to the west by Chichester Harbour and to the east by Pagham Harbour. 'Manhood' comes from the Old English
maene-wudu
meaning "men's wood" or "common wood", i.e. common land, so it isn't nearly as exciting as it sounds.
Chichester Canal:
Opened in 1822, its last load was transported in 1906. Runs 4.5 miles (7.2 km) from Birdham to Chichester. Some of its length is still navigable and the towpath is a popular route.
Hunston:
Not much to write about this village, but pretty Spire Cottage was built using stone reclaimed from Chichester Cathedral tower when it collapsed in 1861.
Sidlesham:
Set among cornfields are half a dozen scattered hamlets, the nicest being Sidlesham Quay beside Pagham Harbour, which looks like a miniature Bosham. We will have lunch here. The historic
Crab & Lobster
pub has a lovely garden.
Pagham Harbour:
A glorious, tranquil area of saltmarsh, mudflats, saltings (for salt extraction) and shallow lagoons. Precious for wildlife as it's an SSSI, a Local Nature Reserve and a Ramsar Site. You could see curlew sandpiper and shelduck on the mudflats and long-tailed tit and willow warbler in the hedges.
Church Norton:
Just the chancel remains from an old church, simple C13, with some fine tombs. The setting is the remarkable thing: surrounded by trees and almost on the shore, the leafy, isolated locale is rare on the Sussex coastal strip. The secluded shingle spit nearby is a nudist beach.
Swimming
This walk
does not
include swimming. If you choose to swim, you do so at your own risk as it is not covered by OutdoorLads's insurance.
The sea water is always clean as there are no sewage outfalls nearby. You're invited to check the quality at Southern Water's
Beachbuoy
site or at the
Surfers Against Sewage
site.
The
route
(please note that this is a linear walk. A bus will take us back to Chichester)
:
We'll leave Chichester by way of the canal and walk along it to Hunston. At Hunston, we'll walk to Hunston Church along Church Lane. We'll then use footpaths to go to South Mundham and use Fisher Lane to Fisher Farm. Further footpaths will take us to Bramber Farm and Honer Farm. We'll then walk around Paghan Harbour to Halsey's Farm and then pretty Sidlesham Quay for lunch. We could also have a drink at the Crab & Lobster pub. Further walking around Pagham Harbour will bring us to the hinge of Pagham Harbour Spit at Church Norton for a break. Footpaths and then a walk along Grange Lane and Rectory Lane will take us to the B2145 and the bus stop.
Getting the bus back:
The No. 51 Stagecoach bus departs for Chichester every 30 minutes (at 33 and 53 minutes past the hour). It takes around 30 minutes to walk from the beach to the bus stop. The fare is £3, payable by any means.
Dogs:
I love having dogs on my walks and this walk is suitable for them apart from during very hot weather. There is sea for them to splash in but little shade. Dogs are permitted at Church Norton Beach but there are no poop bins. A dog off the lead must be obedient.

and are here attributed to their copyright holders.)
(#295)
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is 17 km (10.5 miles); relief is flat (just 55m of ascent); terrain is dirt, grass, beach shingle and tarmac. Please note that this is a linear walk that uses a frequent and reliable bus service to get back.

Title: Suffolk Sands, Big Skies & Saxon Secrets
Summary:
You are invited to a weekend of camping in the ancient county of Suffolk.
Suffolk offers a very different kind of walking experience to the South East. Instead of hills, ridgelines, and busy, well-trodden routes, you get open landscapes, big skies, and a real sense of space. The terrain is flatter but more varied than you’d expect—coastal paths, heathland, forest, and marsh.
Weekend Itinerary
Friday Evening - Arrival
Arrive, pitch up, and settle in. Relax and unwind around the campfire, getting to know your fellow campers.
Saturday - 14 mile circular walk
Suffolk unfolds in soft layers of sand, sky, and story, as we wander from the quiet charm of Shottisham through landscapes shaped by time—past Suffolk Punch horses in Hollesley, across the RSPB marshes, and out to the wild coastline of Shingle Street with its Martello towers standing watch. This is not a walk of steep climbs but of space and atmosphere, where wildlife, coastal winds, and centuries of history come together under vast, open skies.
Saturday Evening
As the day winds down and the Suffolk sky turns golden, we’ll settle into a relaxed evening of conversation and easy company.
Sunday
After packing away and off site by midday, there will the option for a shorter afternoon walk for those that are interested, or you can explore some of the local history such as Sutton Hoo (National Trust) which is 10 mins away.
Campsite Amenities
This campsite is a small, sustainable, family run small site offering modern facilities, including a working smallholding with animals (alpacas, goats, pigs, chickens). Guests can often help with feeding animals. The site offers direct access to walking routes through heath, forest, and fields. There is a mix of tent pitches and also electrical hook-ups for campervans.
2 toilet/shower blocks (modern + older units, hot water, including accessible toilet)
Dishwashing areas and wash-up points
Drinking water points around the site
Shared Fridges for use (located at dishwashing area)
Food & Drink
On-Site Option:
The campsite sells home-reared free range sausages, bacon and eggs from their rare breed pigs and hens; home-made jams, jellies, chutneys and honey; firewood and fire lighters
There is an onsite cafe, which on Fridays evenings offers woodfired pizza's please check out the menu here
Blue Rabbit Cafe
Evening Options:
Cook your own feast at your tent
The
Sorrell Horse Inn
, a 16th century pub is within 5 minutes walk of the campsite offers  food and of course, local suffolk cider.
Pack your tent, walking boots, and sense of adventure—we can’t wait to see you there!
New to Camping ?
Don't worry we have a useful guide to help,
what do I bring camping?
Transport:
Lift sharing or using public transport is strongly encouraged for this event, due to how the campsite operates. Please make an effort to offer a lift or find one from others signed up, where practical. Getting a train and then a lift from the station from another attendee also encouraged.
Campervans:
VW-style camper vans are welcome, although an additional charge will apply and will be paid locally. If you're bringing a van, you can camp alongside others in the group who are in tents. Plus, you can park right next to your camping area—no long hauls with heavy gear!
Dogs:
Well-behaved dogs on leads are welcome, the campsite does require an additional charge which will be payable locally.
Photos:
All photo's were taken by the event leader in April 2026, and permitted for use by ODL.
Difficulty & Distance: The suffolk terrain is relatively flat, however we will be walking 14 miles on Saturday.

Title: Portsmouth at War, Southsea at Play, the Lads at Sea
Summary:
As this event includes a bespoke
boat tour
, it is £11.50 for Full/Concession Members, and the usual £12.50 day walk cost for PAYG Members (the public would ordinarily pay £13.00 to join a boat tour).
Portsmouth was granted city status in 1926, so on this walk around its most attractive and absorbing areas, we'll see it at a moment of significant civic pride. We'll see the city at war: the historic dockyard with the ships
Warrior
(1860),
Victory
(1759) and the
Mary Rose
(1545), and Southsea Castle from where Henry VIII saw the latter ship sink. We'll see Southsea, Portsmouth's seaside suburb, at play: its esplanade, funfair, pier and rock gardens. We'll see Portsmouth Harbour from the water too, as I have chartered a boat to take us on an exclusive hour-long tour. We'll see ferries and frigates and aircraft carriers and cruise ships, and we'll see why Portsmouth is home to two thirds of our naval fleet.
What you could do in Portsmouth and Southsea after the walk:
Sunbathe on a beach nearby: Head back to the Hot Walls in Old Portsmouth. I will have shown you where this is. And Portsmouth also has a nudist beach.
Explore the ships in the Historic Dockyard: Click
here
. 12-month tickets are £51, although there is a cheaper afternoon ticket if arriving after 14.00, I believe.
Shop in Gunwharf Quays: There's a range of outlet shopping to be had with many big brands.
Ascend the Spinnaker Tower in Gunwharf Quays: Click
here
. Tickets are £15.50 (or £10.50 if bought online).
Go the Blue Reef Aquarium: Click
here
. Day tickets bought online are £15.95
The sights and
route
(please see this at the Ordnance Survey website)
:
Starting at Portsmouth Harbour Railway Station, we'll walk northwest along The Hard, to enter, having had our bags checked, the...
1. Historic Dockyard:
Established from the late C17, greatly expanded in the Georgian and Victorian periods. A large and coherent complex of naval buildings and basins, of outstanding historical importance, though architecturally varied. The Historic Dockyard is the publicly-accessible part of HM Naval Base Portsmouth. Visitor attractions include the ships
Warrior
,
Victory
and the
Mary Rose
. The Rope Houses are among the earliest cast iron-framed buildings in Britain, landmarks in industrial design. The Historic Dockyard is free to enter and walk round, although the ships in it aren't.
And continuing deeper into the dockyard, we'll see...
2. HMS Warrior:
1860, by Isaac Watts and Thomas Lloyd. Iron-hulled, armoured warship. A landmark in naval engineering. Built in response to the launching of the steam-powered ship
Napoleon
by France in 1850,
Warrior
's arrival was hastened by the launch of France's
Gloire
in 1859. It began an arms race between France and Britain that lasted for a decade.
Warrior
never saw action and became obsolete in 1873 when Britain launched the mastless HMS
Devastation
. Unless you stay after the walk finishes, we won't be paying to board it.
Then, further on...
3. HMS M.33:
One of the last surviving ships from the Great War. Built in 1915 under the direct orders of Winston Churchill, then First Lord of the Admiralty, HMS
M.33
is the only British ship still standing from the Gallipoli Campaign (1915-1916).
And close by...
4. HMS Victory:
1759, by Sir Thomas Slade. Three-decker ship of the line and Nelson’s flagship at Trafalgar. A masterpiece of C18 naval architecture. Now at risk of collapsing under its own weight and mostly enclosed in scaffolding, its restoration will be complete in 2030. Unless you stay after the walk finishes, we won't be paying to board it.
And finally in the Dockyard...
5. The Mary Rose Museum:
2013, by Wilkinson Eyre Architects. A dark, angular envelope enclosing the conserved hull of Henry VIII’s carrack from 1545. A dramatic and controlled space enclosing its central exhibit: most of the starboard side and keel of the ship, lifted from the bed of The Solent in 1982. The artefacts recovered are a Tudor time-capsule. Unless you stay after the walk finishes, we won't be paying to enter the museum.
We'll then leave the Historic Dockyard and walk along to Hard to enter...
6. Gunwharf Quays:
A redevelopment of 1990s–2001 on the former HMS
Vernon
site, a naval scrapyard and ordnance supply base. A mixed commercial ensemble of limited architectural distinction, but red brick was used in deference to conservation interests. Dominated by the Spinnaker Tower (2005, 170 m), the sweeping, sail-like form of which provides a crude but beloved landmark on the harbour skyline. Survivals from before redevelopment include the Vulcan Building (No. 1 Storehouse), 1814–15, a long brick naval store by the Admiralty, with austere but refined industrial character, now adapted to commercial use. A crane for lifting shells, torpedoes and ships' figureheads evoke sense of place. The redevelopment is highly successful commercially and socially, opening up a prime piece of waterfront.
We'll then have our boat trip at 12.15 around...
7. Portsmouth Harbour:
A large natural harbour, its size and double high tide (owing to the narrowness of the entrance) most advantageous for shipping. Also protected by the Isle of Wight across The Solent. An important RAMSAR wetland site but developed all round the edge. The commentary on board the charter boat will indicate the naval base, the cruise ship terminal, the tropical fruit import quay and the submarine museum at Gosport. No cruise ships will be in on the date, but ferries will be seen in abundance. The Navy doesn't publish lists of ships present, but Portsmouth is home to the aircraft carriers
Queen Elizabeth
and
Prince of Wales
and the Type-45 Destroyers
Daring, Dauntless, Diamond, Dragon, Defender
and
Duncan
.
Disembarking the boat, we'll follow the Millennium Walkway round to Gunwharf Road and White Hart Road and we'll reach Feltham Row, a walkway beside...
8. The Camber:
Taking its name from the River Camber (now built over), the site of the medieval and Tudor dockyard. Now the home of the city's modest fishing fleet.
By going along northwest along Broad Street then round Bath Square and Tower Street we can see see some of...
9. Old Portsmouth:
The area covered by the original medieval town of Portsmouth. Situated in the south west corner of Portsea Island. The roads still largely follow their original layout. Plenty of distinctive maritime character but mostly rebuilt after wartime devastation. A very pleasant part of the city.
In particular, we'll enjoy...
10. Portsmouth Point/Spice Island:
Historic waterfront quarter associated with victualling and sailors’ taverns. Much rebuilt, post-war, but retains a degree of maritime character. The inspiration for Thomas Rowlandson's rollicking engraving Portsmouth Point and hence Sir William Walton's overture of the same name. Great views over the Harbour to Gosport here.
Ascending onto the defensive ramparts at the Hot Walls we'll pass through the...
11. Round Tower:
1418, rebuilt later C15 in stone. Cylindrical harbour defence, visually more striking than the Square Tower, its curved form well suited to its exposed position. A feature familiar from news broadcasts where the city gathers to watch naval vessels departing and arriving.
And then through the...
12. Square Tower:
1494, later modified. A solid, rectilinear artillery tower guarding the harbour entrance. Plain but powerful, with thick masonry and minimal ornament.
This is all by the Millennium Trail and promenade which we will descend to the High Street to see...
13. The Dolphin:
A fine C18 pub in which Nelson stayed, scratching his name with a diamond ring on a window pane.
And opposite...
14. Portsmouth Cathedral:
Parish church of St Thomas dating from 1180, chancel dating to C13. Largely rebuilt after Civil War devastation in 1642, including choir and tower rebuilt and cupola added in 1660s after the Restoration. Made cathedral 1927. Nave extended 1930s by Charles Nicholson in a simplified Norman-Byzantine idiom. The rebuilding interrupted by war, it was only finished in 1991. Modest in scale; an interesting and pleasing blend of post-medieval survival and interwar ecclesiastical design.
Then we'll head southeast down Pembroke Road, Duisburg Way and Clarence Parade to enter the town of Southsea and...
15. Southsea Common:
Open ground established from the C18 as part of a defensive glacis, later landscaped as a public amenity. Expansive and informal, valued more for setting and views across the Solent than for designed features. The large English elm trees are a notable asset, protected from Dutch elm disease by Portsmouth's isolation.
We'll follow a path (Serpentine Road) to emerge at Clarence Esplanade where we'll turn left to see...
16. Landing Craft LCT 7074:
1944. The last surviving Landing Craft Tank from D-Day operations, now restored and linked with the D-Day Museum. Purely functional naval engineering, its interest entirely historical.
At the D-Day Museum we'll head south to...
17. Southsea Castle.
1544, for Henry VIII, attributed to Richard Lee. A low, angular artillery fort of early modern type, later altered but demonstrating advances in gunnery made in C16. Vantage point from which Henry saw his flagship Mary Rose capsize in 1545. Rugged and compact, retaining much of its original defensive character. Stumpy lighthouse with foghorn. Free to enter, which we will. We can also have tea or coffee here.
We'll then proceed northwest along the promenade to see the new...
18. Sea defences:
A £185 million coastal defence scheme is rebuilding 4.5 km of seafront to reduce flood risk to over 10,000 properties. Works include widening the beach with imported shingle, raising promenades, and installing new barriers. Ongoing phases (2024–2027) involve closures and upgrades, improving resilience against storms and rising sea levels. The stretch at Clarence Esplanade we'll walk along was only opened in April.
And further along Clarence Esplanade we'll see...
19. World War I and World War II Memorials.
Naval Memorial, 1924, by Sir Robert Lorrimer. Impressive and abstract, with naval motifs sometimes likened to a columna rostrata (with ships' prows protruding at the top). Identical to memorials at Chatham and Plymouth. Flanking additions after 1945 by Sir Edward Maufe, carefully handled to preserve the unity of the ensemble. The 2-metre statues of service personnel in various garbs are imposing.
Further still along Clarence Esplanade, we'll reach...
20. Southsea Hoverport:
The journey to Ryde takes 10 minutes and is (the operators claim) the only year-round commercial hovercraft service still operating in the world. Hovertravel operates a pair of craft capable of 40 knots.
We'll then go past Clarence Pier along...
21. Long Curtain and Battery:
C17 sea defences, an earthwork and masonry rampart. Altered, but legible as part of the continuous line of coastal fortification stretching around Old Portsmouth.
Well then descend the fortification to the...
22. Royal Garrison Church:
Domus Dei, founded 1212 as hospice and chapel. Later adapted for military use. The marriage of Charles II of England to Catherine of Braganza took place here in 1662. Nave left roofless after 1941 bombing, chancel restored. A moving ruin, its open shell of a nave contrasting with the intact chancel.
Walking up Penny Road and then turning left along Pembroke Road, we'll the turn right up the High Street past...
23. Buckingham House (Ye Spotted Dogge):
Late C15 or early C16. Timber-framed, with later alterations; one of the oldest survivals in Portsmouth, refronted C18. Traditionally associated with John Felton, who killed George Villiers, 1st Duke of Buckingham (1628), favourite of James I of England.
And further up...
24. Portsmouth Grammar School:
Founded in 1732, from when the present restrained but suave frontispiece dates. Selective private school. Alumni are known as Old Portsmuthians and include Roger Black, Mel Stride, Murray Gold and Ant Middleton. And...
At Cambridge Junction we'll turn left and walk along St George's Road past...
25. Landport Gate: 1760–63:
Modest Portland stone triumphal arch forming part of the fortified enceinte (enclosure of naval buildings). A restrained classical composition with rusticated base and central arch, its original defensive context lost.
We'll then walk north along Ordnance Row back to The Hard and the railway station. Then you are welcome to do as you wish. There are some suggestions below.
Dogs:
This walk is partly suitable for dogs. There are stretches where they can walk extensively on and off the lead, but they are not permitted on the beaches we'll pass, nor are they permitted in the naval base. Hot weather could make the walk exhausting for them.
(All images were taken by the leader.)
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is 9 km (5.5 miles); relief is flat; terrain is gravel, tarmac and concrete.

Title: A Ramble by the Channel, and a Traversal to a Castle
Summary:
This walk is a staple of the OutdoorLads schedule, but this version includes a ferry trip from the harbourside hamlet of Keyhaven to the sea-guarding stronghold of Hurst Castle. The boat ride will give great views of the multitudinous marsh of the harbour and the precarious mass of the castle, precarious because Henry VIII's and Victoria's great guardian of The Solent has just been stopped from collapsing into the sea. The Henrician part is safe and open to visitors, but the Victorian part is unsafe and closed while it is rescued by English Heritage. The attractive town of Lymington is at the start and end of this walk where the New Forest meets the sea and we gaze out over it to the Isle of Wight.
The sights:
Lymington:
The New Forest visitor website states:
'
The historic town of Lymington offers plenty for the visitor to the New Forest. This colourful coastal town is an ancient seaport with a rich maritime history. Situated on the west bank of the Lymington River, on the edge of the Solent, Lymington is a major yachting centre with three marinas...  From the Middle Ages and up to the 19th century, Lymington was well-known for its salt making and from the early 19th century a thriving ship building industry. Much of the architecture you see today in Lymington is Georgian and Victorian. The High Street, which looks predominantly Georgian, is in fact made up of an eclectic mix of Georgian, Victorian, Art Deco with some traces of Medieval architecture.'
Keyhaven:
A pretty hamlet with a harbour beside Keyhaven Marshes and behind Hurst Spit. There are no shops, but some attractive cottages and a pub.
Lymington and Keyhaven Marshes:
Forming in the lee of Hurst Spit, the combined area of the two nature reserves covers over 1200 hectares. It includes mudflats, salt marshes, shingle banks, coastal grazing marshes, and saline lagoons. The nature reserves support important populations of birds and rare and specialist plants and invertebrates. Salt harvesting was a source of income for local people from Roman times to 1865 when the last salt pan closed. For information click the link
here
. The ferry goes from Keyhaven across the harbour to Hurst Castle continuously. The fare is £6. If there are many of us, we'll have to cross in shifts.
Hurst Castle:
English Heritage website states: 'Built by Henry VIII...it was one of the most advanced artillery fortresses in England. Commanding the narrow entrance to the Solent, it offers stunning panoramic views. The castle was used as a prison for eminent 17th century captives, including Charles I. It was later strengthened during the 19th and 20th centuries and played a role in defending the western Solent from invasion threats from the Napoleonic Wars to the Second World War...In February 2021, a section of wall on the 19th-century east wing of the castle collapsed, after the sea exposed and undercut its foundations'. New rock armour has been laid to protect the castle and the structure has been secured but not restored. For more information click the link
here
. If you would like to go in the castle, it costs £9.50.
Hurst Beach and Spit:
A mighty bank of shingle that half-closes off The Solent to the English Channel. Naturally formed by longshore drift but now requiring the regular artificial replenishment of its material to avoid being breached by the sea. A breach would be disastrous for the nature reserves behind the spit and for the commercial shipping and leisure boating in The Solent.
The
route
:
We'll head south from Lymington town centre to the Flushards area. We'll then follow lanes heading southwest to Normandy across the boatyards which is where we'll pick up the Solent Way to take us across Pennington and Keyhaven Marshes. At the jetty for the ferry we'll embark on the two kilometre trip to Hurst Castle. After lunch and a possible look at the castle we'll walk along Hurst Spit and have a swim and ice creams at Hurst Beach. The return journey will entail walking along the harbourside at Keyhaven and then along a byway through Pennington and then a lane through Woodside which is a suburb of Lymington. A route north will bring us to the High Street in Lymington which we'll walk down to end up at the quayside for a drink or the station for home.
Dogs:
I love having dogs on my walks and this walk is suitable for them apart from during very hot weather. There is sea for them to splash in much of the way but little shade. A dog off the lead must be obedient.

Title: Go Ape 🦧
Summary:
Go Ape
Climb, cross and zip through the treetops of Crawley — high ropes thrills in a stunning woodland setting.

Experience their boldest adventure in the heart of Tilgate Park. Title: BIG20 SE1000 The Ultimate Challenge - Haslemere
Summary:
Be part of
Outdoorlads BIG20
with this monster of a challenge!
Conquer
1000m
of ascent over a
half marathon
length walk 21.7k (13.5 miles).
Exclusive
to Outdoorlads and
a first
in the South East of England.
Be awarded a
champion medal
on completion.
Steep slopes, scrambly banks, and hidden paths through access land: this is a route for stamina, power and resilience! Incredible beauty, through gorgeous scenery, heathland, forests, nature-filled copses and valleys. THis is the make up of
SE1000 The Ultimate Challenge
.
Leaders will coach, give details of Kilometres (K) and ascent (m) covered and how far to go. We'll share tips on handling the terrain and developing muscle: expect to be super-charged all over from this experience!
To enjoy this challenge
you should be ready and have:
Completed an SE500
walk or mountain walk or equivalent, and feel confident about another
Readied you muscles
in the 2 weeks before the walk, such as inclined treadmill or walking slopes
Have good footwear,
worn in and able to take the rough paths in places
Wear appropriate layers
and checked the kit list
Plenty of water and snacks
to keep you going - expect to burn 4100 calories!
The route is awesome, includes one of seven wonders of the south of England and four of the highest hills.
Meeting at
Haslemere railway station
, we first rise up Weydown Road with a 5 minute gentle warm up. Then we descend on a path alongside community fields and cross a stream, before taking an ancient byway through shaded trees to Farnham Lane. At this point, you will have completed 2.4K and 111m of ascent. Warm up over, you are now in for your first steep slopes in
Polecat Valley
.
The valley’s name is fitting because captive Polecats have been returned to the wild here in recent years. The descent is steep and scrambly in wet weather. The rise through scrub is also steep but rewarding, giving you the first taste of greater ascents and descents to come.
The rise up
Hindhead Spur
is steeper, 50 metres in one go, and over stony ground in places. The gravel and rounded stones in the clay rich soil slopes were deposited here in the last but one ice age, giving a primeval feel to the landscape. The heath is a reserve which support reptiles including lizards and natterjack toads, as well as birds such as the woodlark and nightjar and the silver-studded blue butterfly. Bell heather and bilberry also thrive here.
There’s no stopping you, tough neanderthal warriors! By the path junction where the spur levels, you will have completed 4K and 220m of ascent. Climbing up and down through the valley there is now a steep descent followed by a steep rise into Coomb head and then woodlands managed by the forestry commission alongside the bridleway beyond. We rise to
Hurt Hill, 262m
, one of four major tops on this route.
On the descent we pause to look at the Temple of the four Winds. This was a folly built in 1910 by a businessman fascinated by the outlook seen from here. Next, a descent to the Roundles and a rise to the second and highest point of the walk,
Gibbet Hill at 272m
. Highwaymen robbers were executed here for their crimes. There is a Celtic Cross memorial here. The area is also of literary fame: Charles Dickens mentions it in Nicholas Nickleby, as a staging point on his route to London.
We are now above the
Devil’s Punch Bowl
. This is a natural amphitheatre, formed by springs eroding the landscape from underneath. According to legend, the devil tormented Thor, the god of thunder, by jumping from hill to hill here. Thor threw lighting bolts at the devil, who evaded them by scooping up earth to throw at Thor. That scooped up earth left the depression known as the Punch Bowl.
At this stage, if you are feeling worn out, there is a short cut to the visitor centre café. You can have an extended break whilst the group completed the northern section. The short cut reduces the total by 300m and 6K.
The north part continues with a steep descent into the Punch Bowl, then a rise to the third main hill of the day,
Highcombe Hill 236m
. The scenery is wild and this is a Site of Special Scientific Interest, home to the rare lesser spotted woodpecker, common redstart and in summer, the wood warbler. Going over two ridges, the route descends to a valley then climbs back to the
café
for a pause for refreshments. This is the top of
Hindhead at 257m
, You have now completed 17.3K and 896m of ascent with 4.2K and 124m to go, so there’s much more downhill now!
Miss James of Tyndall’s wood was a pauper born in 1831 and servant to a family of wine merchants here. When they died without heir, they bequeathed the whole estate to her, and she left it to the National Trust. We walk through her lands and on to
Nutcombe Valley
, which features a bat barn where the pink tinged Natterer’s bats roost.
We continue through
Polecat Copse
which reputedly has the largest redwood trees in Britain. Some have names including King Kong and T rex, and one rises to over 43m high. Nearly there! One last descent and then rise again into Haslemere for a Champion’s completion back at the railway station.
The whole route is 1020m of ascent over a half marathon length 21.7K. Follow the
route on OS Maps
.

Title: Round Dunsop Bridge on a Thursday
Summary:
Join Stephen on an
11 mile
walk in and around
Dunsop Bridge
in
Lancashire
in the heart of the Forest of Bowland and in the centre of Great Britain.
We will start by following the River Hodder for a while then climb up to onto the moors overlooking the Hodder Valley.
We will venture through many farms and walk along the tops with views across to the Bowland Fells then returning back to Dunsop Bridge.
When we arrive back in the village -let's find the Centre of Great Britain marker!
There is no pub in the village  - but we can take a short drive to the wonderful Inn at Whitewell for some post walk drinks - here is a link -
http://www.innatwhitewell.com/
Image / photo credits:  All leader's own images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.
Are you new to Outdoorlads and considering your first event?
Have a look at this link, hopefully it will help.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
Don’t worry if the event is full.
Do sign up and join the waiting list.
Often those on the main list cancel nearer the time.
There is a very high chance you will still get a place
Difficulty & Distance: There is slow ascent up onto the fells

Title: Berwick to Alfriston Circular Leisure Walk
Summary:
Join us for a relaxed and social countryside walk through the heart of the South Downs
🚶 Walk Overview
A popular
8.5‑mile circular leisure walk
starting and finishing at
Berwick Railway Station
. The route begins on the scenic
Cuckmere Pilgrim Path
, following the river valley and open countryside with stunning panoramic views.
The walk is
generally easy to moderate
, mostly flat with a few gentle inclines and short sections of road walking. Some paths can be muddy after rain.
⭐ Route Highlights
🌄
Cuckmere Valley
– wide open views and peaceful paths
🗿
The Long Man of Wilmington
– one of Sussex’s most iconic landmarks
🍺
Long Man Brewery
– award‑winning local ales brewed on site, with a relaxed taproom and countryside views; a great spot for lunch and an optional pint or tasting or visit to the café next door
🏘
Alfriston Village
– charming historic high street
The
Star Inn
(dating back to 1345)
The
Clergy House
(England’s first National Trust property)
You’ll have around
45 minutes free time in Alfriston
to explore, relax, or grab a slice of cake or ice-cream.
📏 Walk Details
Distance:
Approx. 8.5 miles
Difficulty:
Easy / Moderate
Terrain:
Largely flat, some gentle climbs, possible muddy sections
Pace:
Leisurely and social
🚆 Travel Information
Start / Finish:
Berwick Railway Station (East Sussex)
By Train:
Easily accessible from
Brighton, Lewes, and Eastbourne
By Car:
Parking available at the station or near Berwick Station
By Bus (Optional Return):
It is possible to return by
local Cuckmere Buses
from
Alfriston
back towards
Berwick or Seaford
.
Services are limited and
run on specific days only
, so please
check routes and timings in advance
.
👉
Check up‑to‑date timetables:
https://www.cuckmerebuses.org.uk/timetables.htm
Common routes that may be useful include services
126
and
47 (Cuckmere Valley Ramblerbus)
, subject to day and seasonal operation.
New Members
If this is your first flight or you are new to OutdoorLads passenger we’d love to see you. This type of event is perfect for you to get to know other ODL members. Please message me if you have any questions.
Photo credits martiebear free for outdoorlads to use
Difficulty & Distance: 8.5 mile  generally easy to moderate

Title: An evening with Leeds Male Voice Choir
Summary:
Join us for a memorable evening as we continue to celebrate 110 years of Leeds Male Voice Choir and bring in ‘The Sounds of Summer’.
With an exciting variety of music including pop favourites, showtunes, and sacred works - the concert promises to be a thrilling evening celebrating the past and inspiring the future, with familiar faces from OutdoorLads in the choir.
Tickets can be purchased online:
https://leedsmalevoicechoir.co.uk/tickets

Title: The Greatest Showman Sing-A-Long
Summary:
Join us for a social evening of entertainment as you watch and sing along with The Greatest Showman on a huge open-air screen on the lawn in front of the grand stately home.
The movie that has sold out cinemas all over the world! Inspired by the imagination of P. T. Barnum, this incredible musical celebrates the birth of show business and tells the story of a visionary who rose from nothing to create a spectacle that became a worldwide sensation.
Pack some food to share, bring a blanket or camping chair, and sing-along with The Greatest Showman. Plus, music to enjoy before the movie with a soundtrack of specially curated songs.
Tickets can be
purchased online
from Adventure Cinema and cost around £17.00. We will sit together (location to be arranged on site).
Further information about the event can be found on
Adventure Cinema's event page
, but please note:
Gates open at 6pm and the show runs betweeen 7.30pm and 9.15pm
Alcohol must be purchased on-site only, but there is an on-site bar
No dogs, except assistance or guide dogs
No glass
Images created by AI.

Title: Bike Week 2026: Leisure Cycle to Monton
Summary:
Bike Week 2026 is taking place from June 9–15, and it's our chance to be part of the UK’s biggest celebration of cycling
Whether you ride every day or haven’t cycled in years, Bike Week is all about showcasing how cycling can fit into daily life - for everyone.
Join Paul on this leisurely social bike ride starting from Central Manchester.
The 17 mile circular route starts with a brief venture through the heart of the City Centre, passing the Palace Theatre, the iconic Central Library and the Opera House on our way to Salford Central station via Spinningfields. Next, we will head out past Salford Cathedral and Salford University to discover the safer cycling route that runs along the busy East Lancs Road towards Worsley.
The route then follows a former railway loopline to Monton, where we have the option of a pub/snack stop. Finally, we will join the Bridgewater Canal which takes us past the Trafford Centre and Old Trafford all the way back to the city centre.
Pace and Terrain
This will be a leisurely ride, suitable for all levels of fitness. We'll aim to maintain a steady pace of around 8-10mph.  The route is relatively flat with level surfaces, apart from a few short, cobbled bits. Suitable for any roadworthy bike. Please make sure your bike is in good condition - check the tyres are well inflated, chain is oiled, and you've got a spare inner tube, drinks and snacks with you.
The first part of the ride includes roads, but once out of the city centre we have the option of using shared cycle/footpaths and cycle/bus lanes which offer protection from the traffic. The second part of the ride, once we are on the loopline is almost all off road, There's just a short on-road stretch where we will have to leave and rejoin the Bridgewater Canal to cross the Barton Road Swing Bridge.
Photos
taken by the ride leader and used with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: A gentle leisure cycle

The North-West City Social Weekender
is a new monthly gathering, holding a number of events over the weekend in the nominated city. Our aim is to offer more than what we can with our standard social events, giving an opportunity for members to travel from outside their local area, being reassured of a structured weekend of events, as well as our loyal local members meeting some new faces across the country.

A 5 to 8km walk, as a city experience, highlighting some of the city iconic landmarks. It may be best to travel light, so that you can check out, and join us with your back-pack! The evening before the walk on the

Title: Spanning the Humber: Hessle to Barton Circular Walk
Summary:
Join Chris for a 10-mile (16 km) walk along the Humber Estuary, including a crossing of the Humber Bridge with wide views across the water. Opened in 1981, the bridge has a main span of 1,410 metres and was once the longest single-span suspension bridge in the world. It remains one of the longest bridges you can walk across in the UK.
After crossing, the route continues into Barton-upon-Humber and through Far Ings National Nature Reserve. We then visit the remains of the old cement works on the foreshore before walking beneath the Humber Bridge and stopping for a drink at the Viking Way Café, located at the start of the 147-mile Viking Way trail.
We then head back across the bridge towards Hessle. On the north side, we pass under the bridge again and walk down to the Humber foreshore. There is another stop at The Country Park Inn before continuing through Humber Bridge Country Park and returning to the start point.
Your First OutdoorLads Event?
We know that new members in particular like to know in advance more about what an OutdoorLads event will be like.
If this is your first OutdoorLads event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the OutdoorLads website.
What if there is a Waiting List?
If this event is fully booked and you want to attend, just add yourself to the waitling list, because places inevitably become available closer to the date.

Sign up for the
North East England & Yorkshire Region Telegram Chat Group
, and bookmark our upcoming events list via the short link:
https://outdoorlads.events/northeast
.

All images taken by the event leader with permission for ODL use.
Difficulty & Distance: 10-mile (16km) long. 305ft (93m) ascent

Title: Fresh Air & Fairly Flat-ish: A Surrey Circular
Summary:
This circular walk starts in Milford and winds its way through peaceful countryside toward Bagmoor, with fresh greenery, birdsong, and that unmistakable “everything’s finally waking up” feeling in the air. The paths weave through woodland and open stretches, offering a relaxed, scenic start that eases you into the day.
From there, the route opens onto the heathland of Witley Common and skirts the edge of Thursley Common. It’s the kind of place where you might pause to take it all in—ostensibly for the views, but really just to justify another snack break.
As always, this is a friendly walk, and newcomers are very welcome. A great chance to get outdoors, stretch your legs and meet other Outdoor Lads.
Total distance is 16 km (10 miles) with a 136 m (447 ft) of ascent spread throughout the route. We will be walking over dirt, grass and tarmac.
Dogs
This walk is suitable for dogs, but keep in mind that we have quite a few stiles. There are some open spaces but we will mainly be walking by the canal.
Don’t Waste That Space!
Walks get filled up quite quickly, so please only sign up if you know you are free to attend. Plans do sometimes change, but remember to free up your space so someone else can attend.
New Members:
If this is your first event or you are new to OutdoorLads we’d love to see you. Please message me if you have any questions.
(Photos taken by Felipe Rogatto)
Difficulty & Distance: Total distance is 16 km (10 miles) with a 136 m (447 ft) of ascent. We will be walking over dirt, grass and tarmac.

Title: Pang Valley: Where A Future Queen Hails From & Once Roamed
Summary:
Join us for a superb 12-mile walk through a tranquil corner of the West Berkshire countryside 🌿
Set between Reading and Newbury, this route explores the Pang Valley, centred on the River Pang, a classic chalk stream rising near Compton and flowing south to meet the River Thames at Pangbourne. The landscape is defined by rolling downland, wooded slopes, and charming villages including Stanford Dingley, Bradfield, and Bucklebury, all of which feature along the route. The Pang’s clear waters support wildlife such as trout, water voles, and kingfishers.
Berkshire is well known for its royal associations—hence “Royal Berkshire”—largely due to Windsor. However, this quieter western area has a more modern connection: Catherine, Princess of Wales, who grew up in Bucklebury. Her engagement to Prince William brought increased attention to the village, where the Middleton family still live today. She was known to enjoy walking and cycling in the vicinity as well as visiting The Pot Kiln pub in nearby Frilsham, which we will pass en route (though not stop at as it's a bit posh!).
Don't worry if you are not fans of the Royal Family, especially in light of recent events, as I won't be banging on about them during this walk. This area is worth visiting despite the royal connection, and I had indeed done this walk in the 90's well before Bucklebury gained wider attention, and appreciated the area for its peace and understated beauty, and the fact that it has remained largely unspoilt.
The Route:
Starting from Chapel Row, we will immediately travese part of Bucklebury Common before heading north into more open and hilly countryside to Bucklebury. We will walk through the village, past the church, cross the Pang for the first time and continue northwards through a largely open and agricultural landscape. Later we enter a more wooded area, part of the Yattendon Estate, pass the Pot Kiln Pub at Frisham Common before turning back to walk southwards through more woodland and farmland to arrive at Stanford Dingley.
We will have lunch at Stanford Dingley, where there are some benches available, and are spoilt by having the choice of 2 pubs for a drink stop, either the Bull Inn or the Old Boot Inn. After lunch the remaining 6 miles is very much around the Pang valley, so we start following the course of the River Pang which eventually comes into view as we get to Bradfield, where you will get glimpses of the large school buildings and cricket ground. After walking through the village we start to loop back towards Chapel Row, taking paths more towards the top of the valley and traversing a number of quiet wooded areas in the process.
The route can be found
here
:
The Sites:
Chapel Row
A small, scattered hamlet centred around a pub, café, and village green. A notable feature is the avenue of oak trees along Bradfield Road: the first were planted to commemorate the visit of Elizabeth I to Bucklebury in 1568, with further trees added to mark later royal visits by Elizabeth II and, most recently, Princess Anne, following a gift from the late Queen to the Queen Mother for her 80th birthday.
The unusual name of the pub, the Bladebone, stems from a local legend of a mammoth bone belonging to a creature that once terrorised the area before being killed. In reality, the bone is believed to be from a whale discovered in the Kennet Valley in the 17th century; the pub sign is said to incorporate the original bone encased in copper.
Bucklebury Common
A large expanse of open land characterised by a mix of heathland, woodland, clearings, and grazing cattle.
Bucklebury
Often regarded (unofficially) as the capital of the Pang Valley, Bucklebury has origins dating back to Saxon times. Its prominence grew when Reading Abbey, which held the manor, built a residence here for the Abbot. The manor later passed to the Winchcombe family, who held it for around 150 years until the early 18th century; their influence helps explain the frequency of royal visits.
The original manor house was destroyed by fire in 1830 and subsequently demolished. The village church is Norman, with a particularly fine south doorway featuring ornate carvings of flowers and faces. In more recent history, Bucklebury is known as the home village of the Middleton family.
Stanford Dingley
Our lunch stop, this village feels notably secluded and largely unchanged. The old mill dates from Norman times. The name “Stanford Dingley” derives from an early Lord of the Manor. The church has Saxon origins, although the present building largely dates from the 13th century and retains medieval tiles and wall paintings from that period.
Bradfield
Dominated by Bradfield College, a prestigious independent school set within extensive grounds. During term time, the village can be busy with students and staff. Founded in 1850, the college is notable for its open-air Greek theatre, which remains in use and is widely regarded. Notable alumni include David Owen, Sir John Nott (politicians), Sam Thompson (Made In Chelsea), Nick Higham (journalist), and Gus Atkinson (Cricketer).
River Pang
A clear chalk stream rising near Compton, flowing through the Pang Valley before joining the Thames at Pangbourne. It is ecologically significant, supporting species such as trout, water voles, and kingfishers, and contributes greatly to the area’s tranquillity.
Dogs:
Well-behaved dogs are welcome, there are some road sections, crossings. stiles and some fields with farm animals (especially sheep in this area) where they will need to be on a lead. Please abide by the Countryside Code (
https://tinyurl.com/rlqvp3h
) and remember not everyone loves dogs as much as you do so be mindful of other members.
Photos taken by the leader 30.03.26 and permission given to OutdoorLads for re-use.
Difficulty & Distance: 12.5 miles, paths of grass, dirt and tarmac, moderately undulating with a total ascent of 300 meters

Title: Bike Week 2026: World Naked Bike Ride Manchester
Summary:
Bike Week 2026 is taking place from June 9–15, and it's our chance to be part of the UK’s biggest celebration of cycling
Whether you ride every day or haven’t cycled in years, Bike Week is all about showcasing how cycling can fit into daily life - for everyone.
Join Outdoorlads and 100s of other cyclists on the annual World Naked Bike Ride (WNBR) in Manchester. It's a free, fun,  legal event, run by volunteers with the aim of making the world a better place for everyone.
We'll meet at 6.30pm, ready to join the ride round the city centre which starts around 7.00pm and takes about 90 minutes at a very gentle pace.
Cleaner environment, safer roads, healthier lifestyles, better attitudes
The ride demonstrates the vulnerability of cyclists while celebrating body freedom and is a protest against fossil fuels and car culture.
Bare as you dare
As it's a "naked" ride, clothing is optional. OutdoorLads requires you to wear a helmet and suitable footwear (trainers / cycling shoes)
There is
no pressure to be completely naked
. The event has a carnival feel, so you are encouraged to adorn yourself with colourful body paint and slogans, wear fancy dress, disguise yourself a bit (eg sunglasses, helmet, mask) or remain partially dressed – be as bare as you dare.
Many passers by and photographers who know about the event will take photographs before the event begins and along the route. There will be lots of people with cameras and phones. If you're at all uncomfortable with appearing naked in photos, you're welcome to wear shorts, speedos, etc.
Photo credit
: wnbrmanchester
Difficulty & Distance: A fun leisure cycle

Title: Nine Edges Derbyshire Challenge Walk
Summary:
"If your dreams do not scare you, they are not big enough." (Ellen Johnson Sirleaf)
“The best moments in our lives are not the passive, receptive, relaxing times . . . The best moments usually occur if a person’s body or mind is stretched to its limits in a voluntary effort to accomplish something difficult and worthwhile” (Csikszentmihalyi, 1990).
A challenging, linear day walk of about 35 km (22 miles) with 933 m (3,061 feet)
of total ascent traverses nine different gritstone edges in the Peak District. The walk will take about 10 hours to complete including several stops along the way.
We traverse the following Peak District edges:
Derwent Edge
Stanage Edge
Upper Burbage Edge
Lower Burbage Edge
Froggatt Edge
Curbar Edge
Baslow Edge
Gardom’s Edge
Birchen Edge
The highest point is Back Tor at 538 m (1766 feet) near to the start of the walk. The route features great views across the High Peak from the edges, and it includes Derwent Dam, the Longshaw Estate, and woods between Baslow and Gardom’s Edge. The route takes us to the trig points and/or Ethels of
Back Tor, High Neb, Stanage Edge, Birchen Edge, Lost Lad, and White Path Moss
.
We begin at Ladybower Reservoir from Fairholmes car park, past Derwent Dam then up to Lost Lad and Back Tor at the start of Derwent Edge. From here we continue on to Stanage Edge, completing the two longest edges first.
Next, we traverse Upper and Lower Burbage Edges, before heading, via the Longshaw Estate, onto edge number five, Froggatt Edge. The next two, Curbar Edge then Baslow Edge, are beautifully linked.
Finally, we walk through beautiful woods to the final two, Gardom’s Edge and Birchen Edge. The last edge is just over 1 km from the trig point to the car park at the end of the walk.
Note
, this is a linear walk
. We will rendezvous
at the end point leaving half of the cars there to travel together to the start point to begin the walk. At the end of the walk we will convey the drivers who left their cars back to the start point. The drive from the finish at Birchen Edge car park to Fairholmes car park is 16 miles, about 30-minute drive, via Hathersage.
There are limited options to do this walk via public transport. The nearest train station to the start is Bamford and at the end of the walk it’s Grindleford station, which is closer to Froggatt Edge, so quite a distance away. The walk leader will facilitate a lift from and back to Derby for anyone who needs it.
Facilities on the walk
There are public toilets available at the start of the walk at Fairholmes car park and also when you come down from Lower Burbage Edge and walk through the Longshaw Estate. There’s also a small shop/cafe you can buy takeaway food at Fairholmes.
In addition, The Grouse Inn pub is open for takeaway food/drinks before Froggatt Edge where we could stop for refreshments and a toilet stop.
At the end of the walk, there are no toilets, but The Robin Hood pub allows us to finish in style.
We will walk at a reasonable average pace of about 3.5 kph (2.2 mph) with regular stops along the way, as well as longer breaks for a packed lunch and evening meal (you will need to bring both with you). This is a long and challenging walk but it is not a race!
There is a link to the
OS Maps
route for members, below.
Dogs are welcome
but they must be capable of walking the distance and owners must ensure dogs are well nourished and kept under control at all times.
This is a very challenging walk,
published in plenty of time to prepare as necessary.  Personal safety and wellbeing is paramount, so arrangements will be put in place to 'rescue' participants, if necessary. You must ensure you are fit and well enough to undertake the walk unaided, with suitable footwear. It is 35 km (22 miles) over 1200 metres of ascent within about 10 hours.
Note:
If there are no places left on this event, join the waiting list, as people frequently drop out during the week before an event, and you are likely to get a place.
Want to keep in touch with what's going on in the Midlands / ODL Central Region?
Join our
Central Region Telegram Chat Group
, or the more more local
East Midlands Telegram Chat Group
and bookmark our upcoming events list via the short link:
https://outdoorlads.events/central
Difficulty & Distance: Distance 35 km (22 miles), total ascent 933 m (3,061 feet), approximately 10 hours. Some paths are uneven and may be boggy in wet conditions.

Title: An Evening with the Stars in Nottinghamshire
Summary:
We have secured a booking for a group visit to
Sherwood Observatory
.
Since its recent re-opening, the Observatory has been booked solid with group tours so we are pleased to offer this opportunity in Autumn 2026.
The visit promises a 90 minute adventure you'll never forget including a chance to ask anything you want to know about space and beyond. Additionally there will be:
a 20+ minute planetarium show and presentation by the expert team
a captivating talk in the lecture room that will spark your imagination
a thrilling opportunity to gaze at the stars and planets or our closest star, the Sun, depending on the season, (weather dependent)
a tour of the radio astronomy facilities
a visit of exhibition in the Science Centre to learn more about human space flight and the history of the Reservoir
Photos: Sherwood Observatory and used with permission
Difficulty & Distance: Sit and watch the stars go by. The site is broadly accessible but visiting the main telescope involves climbing some steps

!

Title: Isle of Portland Climbing and Walking Weekend (3 nights)
Summary:
Join us for an exciting
three-night
weekend of climbing and walking on the tiny island of Portland, Dorset, well-known for its rugged beauty, long coastline, and incredible climbing. Our accommodation is located across the street from the beach, so a lovely setting in which to enjoy the summer!
David (
david209)
will lead the outdoor climbing events on Saturday and Sunday. Spaces are limited to 14 climbers on each day, including two leaders. It is a great opportunity for newer members and novice climbers to meet and learn from more experienced OutdoorLads.
Please sign-up to your preferred
climbing
events separately (below):
Saturday climbing
Sunday climbing
Peter (
MaccMan
) will lead the walking events on Saturday and Sunday.
Please sign up to your preferred
walking
events separately (below):
Saturday walk
- Maiden Castle and the South Dorset Ridgeway
Sunday walk
- Abbotsbury and Chesil Beach
The bunk rooms are simple, each with a small sink and private shower. Bedding and towels are provided. There is a spacious communal lounge and kitchen, as well as toilets, all on the first floor.
Please note
, this is a three-night hostel event from Friday evening to Monday morning to allow two full days of activities over the weekend.
Waitlist:
If an event is fully booked, please
add yourself to the waitlist.
Places often become available closer to the date. If you decide not to attend,
cancel your attendance
as soon as possible to allow someone else to take your place.
Regrettably, the hostel does not have disabled access due to very narrow hallways and all communal space being on the first floor with no lift access.
Dogs:
Sorry, dogs are not permitted at this accommodation.

To be informed of all our South West events please click on this Telegram link
http://outdoorlads.events/SouthWestNotifications
Photos:
Courtesy of
www.unsplash.com
, The Bunker and Derventio, and used with permission and thanks.
Difficulty & Distance: Outdoor climbing, suitable for beginners, and walks about 19 km (11.8 miles) with reasonable ascents.

Title: A Day of Climbing in Portland - Saturday (Beginners Welcome)
Summary:
Join
David
and
Neil
on the stunning island of Portland for some adrenaline-pumping sport climbing! This is a fantastic opportunity to get out on the famous limestone cliffs overlooking the English Channel. Whether you're a seasoned sport climber or have been leading indoors and want to venture on to some real rock, this event is perfect for you.
For those who haven't done sport climbing before, there will be a quick tutorial session at the start of the event, you just need to be competent at tying in, top rope belaying and have a pair of climbing shoes. If you've done three or more indoor climbing events in recent history, that's about all the experience you need. If you are unsure about the difficulty of the event, or whether you have necessary skills & experience, please message the event leader.
On this day, we will explore some of the best climbing areas that Portland has to offer. There will be a range of grades available to cater to all levels of experience, so if you haven't climbed outdoors before, just let the organiser know when you sign up so we can make sure we have a good mix of experiences.
The event is part of an OutdoorLads hostel weekend, with some members taking part in a walking event. After the day's climbing, we will return to the Bunker Hostel for a well-deserved rest and enjoy a friendly and sociable evening. Make sure that if you'd like to stay in the hostel, you
book the hostel event separately here.
IMPORTANT!
Please note that every person participating should be aware of (and accept) that those members perceived as ‘in charge’ or leading the event are not necessarily qualified, do not need to be qualified, and are not regarded by OutdoorLads, or themselves as ‘technical experts’ but are amateur climbers with some experience and who are happy to impart their knowledge. Any advice given should be considered with this in mind by the recipient.
Photos:
Taken by OutdoorLads members and used with full permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Climbing requires a reasonable level of fitness and agility
