Title: Maiden Castle and the South Dorset Ridgeway
Summary:
Join us for a circular walk near Dorchester that includes Maiden Castle, a length of the South Dorset Ridgeway and the Hardy Monument!
The route is 11.5 miles / 18.5 kilometres, with 430 metres of ascent.
This walk is associated with an ODL hostel event on the Isle of Portland, but local ODL members who are not on the hostel event
are welcome to join this walk
.
Maiden Castle
is the largest and most impressive Iron Age hillfort in Britain, covering an area bigger than 50 football pitches.  Work on the complex defensive site began about 2,400 years ago and continued for 300 years.  When it was first built, the gleaming white chalk ramparts would have towered over the surrounding landscape.  At the hillfort’s peak of development, hundreds of people lived there in a settlement of thatched buildings. Many had central hearths for cooking, warmth and light.
The
South Dorset Ridgeway
is part of the
South West Coast Path National Trail
and was the original route until a lower level path was created.  It now provides an alternative, higher level, route with great views of the Jurassic Coast and also of the inland scenery of river valleys and chalk downland.  This landscape was well known to the author
Thomas Hardy
(1840-1928) who used it as the background to several of his novels.
The highpoint of today’s walk is the impressive
Hardy Monument
, built in 1844 in memory of Vice-Admiral Sir
Thomas Masterman Hardy
, Captain of HMS Victory at the Battle of Trafalgar.  Hardy is remembered because, amongst other things, it was in his arms that Nelson died, saying the immortal words ‘Kiss me, Hardy’.
The Hardy Monument is a stone tower, built on the summit of Black Down and designed to look like a spyglass, which Admiral Hardy would have used on board ship.  It is 22 metres / 72 feet high and may be open for us to climb its 120 steps when we pass by.  On a clear day you can see Devon and the the Isle of Wight, both of which are around 40 miles / 65 kilometres away, and to the north Pen Hill in the Mendip Hills, which is a similar distance away.
Dogs
: well-behaved dogs are welcome on the walk, but must be under close control at all times and on a lead in the vicinity of livestock or where wildlife may be disturbed.
Waitlist
: if this event is fully booked and you want to join us, add yourself to the wait list because places usually become available closer to the event date.

Title: Abbotsbury and Chesil Beach
Summary:
Join us for a circular walk along part of Chesil Beach, returning along part of the South Dorset Ridgeway!
The route is 9 miles / 15 kilometres, with 300 metres of ascent.
This walk is associated with an ODL hostel event on the Isle of Portland, but local ODL members who are not on the hostel event
are welcome to join this walk
.
One of the most significant features along Dorset’s 95-mile
Jurassic Coast
is
Chesil Beach
.  The name Chesil is derived from the Old English
ceosel
or
cisel
, meaning gravel or shingle.  Over 18 miles / 29 kilometres long and up to 220 yards / 200 metres wide, the beach is estimated to be 100 million tonnes of pebbles.  It rises to 16 yards / 15 metres above sea level.  Thought to date back some 6,000 years, the beach is the result of onshore currents and rising sea levels.  It is moving inland at a rate of 6 yards / 5 metres per century.
Interestingly, the pebbles are naturally graded in size from fist-sized near Portland (at the eastern end of the beach) to pea-sized at Bridport (at the western end of the beach). The pebbles are mainly a mix of flint and chert.  The beach shelters the
Fleet
, which is Britain’s largest tidal lagoon.  The water is up to 3 metres deep. The Fleet is home to many wading birds and the
Abbotsbury Swannery
.  This is a managed colony of nesting swans, thought to have been established by monks at the former monastery of Abbotsbury.
The
South Dorset Ridgeway
is part of the
South West Coast Path National Trail
and was the original route (the route around Portland was only created in 2003).  It now provides an alternative route to walking over the pebbles of Chesil Beach, with great views of the Jurassic Coast and also of the inland scenery of river valleys and chalk downland.  This landscape was well known to the author
Thomas Hardy
(1840-1928) who used it as the background to several of his stories.
The walk starts and ends in the village of
Abbotsbury
, which is known for its picturesque historic buildings and thatched cottages.  It was the site of a monastery, which fell victim to King Henry VIII’s dissolution of monastic establishments in the mid-16th century.  There are some remains of the abbey, including a large tithe barn (built circa 1400) and St Catherine’s Chapel, a 14th century pilgrimage chapel that stands on a hill between the village and the coast.
Dogs
: well-behaved dogs are welcome on the walk, but must be under close control at all times and on a lead in the vicinity of livestock or where wildlife may be disturbed.

Title: Mid-Summer Camping on the Canal
Summary:
Instead of our traditional July cider pub camping, join ODL as we visit a canal-side pub, between Pewsey and Devizes.  This is a new location for us, on the banks of the Kennett and Avon Canal, and not far from the Alton Barnes White Horse.
There is live music Friday night; currently scheduled to appear are The Blacksmiths, a 5 piece Hampshire based rock & folk band.  Here is a
video
of their song
Cam Ye' O'er Frae France.
Hikes will be confirmed closer to the event but may include this 9.75 mile
Vale of Pewsey
loop, this 8.25 mile
White Horse and All Cannings
loop, or for a real leg-stretcher this 12.5 mile
Honeystreet to West Kennet
loop.
Should you wish to not cook, the pub has a full menu (available Friday until 20.00, Saturday from 12.00 to 14.30 and 17.30 to 20.30 only; 12.00 to 16.00 Sunday) including burgers, "light bites", salads and traditional pub favourites.  A full drinks menu is available throughout the day.
Some of the site rules are:
Quiet hours are between 23.00 and 07.00
Campfires and barbecues are allowed, but must be raised off the ground
Tents must be a minimum of 3 metres apart
Cars can be parked at the pitches
If this is your first event, don't be put off if you lack some of the necessary equipment; just message me on
chris.keivit@outdoorlads.com
or
james.couldry@outdoorlads.com
and we will see what can be worked out!
Dogs:
The Inn's camping area is dog-friendly although they must be kept on a lead at all times (you
must
advise
support@outdoorlads.com
to book your four-legged friend, there is no additional charge).
Caravans and motorhomes:
Are welcome, subject to site availability; if you need an EHU, there is an additional charge of £6 per night (no charge if no EHU is needed). You
must contact
support@outdoorlads.com
to reserve for campervans and caravans. Fire drums are available for hire, and fire wood is available for purchase.  I will confirm closer to the event if we are able to bring in our own wood.
Want to Get Out More?
Why not check out the South Wales and South West Calendar
here
.
Campfires & BBQ's
: This event will include a campfire/BBQ at the site.  Whilst our event leaders will take safety precautions there is still a risk to members. Please consider your behaviour around the campfire for your own and others safety.
Photos:
provided by Outdoorlads Members.
Difficulty & Distance: Canalside Camping

Title: Summer Solstice Weekend Camping in the Cotswolds
Summary:
A lovely campsite nestled below the Cotswold escarpment. It's on a fruit farm which also has an onsite cafe and shop. Nearby are a ruined abbey, medieval church frescoes, idylic Cotswold hamlets and villages (used for film/tv period dramas), grand country houses with landscape features, a heritage steam railway (also used for film/tv) and a pub we can walk to across the fields. There is a village store within two miles for any supplies you need.
If we are blessed with good weather there are stunning views down the Vale of Evesham looking out towards the Malverns and into Wales. We will see and hear the steam train whistling and puffing its way through the landscape.
We have two evenings on this idyllic site and Sunday 21st will be the Summer Solstice - the longest day of the year. Late chats around the fire-pit but please note the owners ask there be no noise after 11pm and that all firewood is purchased from the onsite shop.
Lots of walking opportunities from the campsite, almost entirely off road and whilst the escarpment itself is only about 200m we may ascend and descend this multiples times. Opportunities for a pub stop on the way back and/or an extended lunch break of your choice in a nearby picture-postcard-perfect town with cafes, pubs, delis and a supermarket. There are a couple of optional exciting possibilities for our main walk on Saturday - these will incur an extra cost to be paid direct to the venue. More info will be published here once I can make a reservation for us.
There are also excellent road cycling routes in the area including extended flatter sections and hill climbs if that is your thing but this is not officially part of the event and at your own risk/judgement.
Dogs:
Dogs are welcome on the campsite but are subject to an additional charge of £1 per night each paid directly to the campsite on arrival. Dogs will need to be kept under control throughout the event. You must let me know if you intend to bring your dog as we must pre-inform the owners.
Gazebos:
Please note this campsite makes an additional charge for Gazebos.   You are welcome to bring one but will need to pay the fee of £14 per night directly to the campsite on arrival. I will bring one big gazebo/canopy for our camp.

Title: From Heathland High Society to a Victorian Valhalla
Summary:
This walk includes an optional visit to Highgate Cemetery which charges £10 per person. If you would like to visit, please be prepared to pay this at the cemetery gate.
An urban walk in three parts, the last of which is open and hilly enough to suggest a rural one. Part 1 is a tour of Hampstead Village, the pretty, leafy home of the liberal intelligentsia such as DH Lawrence and John Keats. Part 2 is a tour of where many of such people ended up, the fashionable metropolitan necropolis of Highgate Cemetery. Part 3 crosses an 'island' of wild-seeming countryside, Hampstead Heath, which Victorian campaigners protected from development and facilitated access for all, something which suave Kenwood House, being free to enter, also offers. Not accessible for all is the Men's Bathing Pond, which is where we'll end the walk and where you might want to cool off.
The sights and
route
(please click the link to go to the Ordnance Survey website)
:
Part 1: Hampstead Village
(This part is borrowed, with some changes, from
33 Walks in London That You Shouldn't Miss
by Nicola Perry.)
We'll start in the centre of leafy, literary Hampstead on Hampstead High Street, outside...
1. Hampstead Underground Station:
Designed in his signature oxblood-red glazed tiles by Leslie Green in 1907. Its platforms are the deepest on the London Underground network, at 58.5 metres below ground level, and with the deepest lift shaft at 55 metres.
We'll turn down Church Row, one of the loveliest pockets of Hampstead, to see...
2. HG Wells's House:
No. 17, a lovely Queen Anne house where he wrote
The History of Mr Polly
and would have received George Bernard Shaw and the author Rebecca West who became Wells's lover.
And then...
3. St John-at-Hampstead:
The parish church, designed in 1747 and expanded in 1870 and 1911, In 1958 the dark Victorian interior decoration was removed and the lighter, whitewashed scheme closer to the original appearance was reinstated. Elegant and playful in white with gold details, the baroque interior is delightful. The enchanting churchyard teems with notable burials too many to list here (so see
here
) including the pianist Alfred Brendel, the painter John Constable, the actor Peter Cook, the Labour leader Hugh Gaitskell, the actor and presenter Nicholas Parsons, and the measurer of longitude John Harrison. We'll get time to search for some graves.
We'll then walk downhill along Frognal Way and enjoy views over London, plus...
4. Gracie Fields's House:
No. 20, an attractive inter-war villa for the Lancastrian star of cinema and music hall, 'Our Gracie'.
On Frognal is...
5. 66 Frognal:
Modern house from 1937-38 built to a design by the architects Connell Ward & Lucas. Grade II* listed. Hugely influential two decades later.
We'll circle back round to the Parish Church, along Church Row, and turn briefly up Frognal Gardens and back to see...
6. Alastair Sim's House:
A Victorian house where the Scottish actor of famously comically sinister roles lived.
Back on Church Row,  we'll turn left up Holly Walk. We'll then turn right on to Mount Vernon and left on Frognal Rise then cross the street to Windmill Hill to see...
7. Bolton House:
The home of the poet an dramatist Joanna Baillie, one of the first women to be honoured with a blue plaque. Visitors to the house would've included Wordsworth, Byron and her near-neighbour, Keats.
The next street is Admiral's Walk, in which we'll see...
8. Admiral's House:
The roof resembles a ship's quarterdeck and the occupant, one Lieutenant Fountain North, would fire a cannon from it to celebrate the anniversaries of British naval victories and royal birthdays, the inspiration for Admiral Boom in
Mary Poppins
.
At the end of Admiral's Walk, we'll turn right on to Hampstead Grove, another street of beautiful houses and see...
9. Fenton House:
An exceedingly handsome C17 house with unusual collections and picturesque walled gardens owned by the National Trust. (We won't have time to enter.)
We'll get back on Holly Hill and go back down the lane of Holly Mount to go to...
10. The Holly Bush:
A famous C18 tavern, still lit by gas lamps and frequented by figures such as Charles Dickens and Samuel Johnson.
We'll cross back over Back Lane to Flask Walk to see another pub...
11. The Flask:
A pub mentioned in Samuel Richardson's scandalous 1748 novel
Clarissa
, a tragic story of a young woman, Clarissa Harlowe, whose quest for virtue is continually thwarted by her grasping, status-conscious family. The street it's on is a haven of old-world exclusivity and charm.
Further along Flask Walk is...
12. The Old Bathhouse:
A sign reading 'The Wells & Campden Trust Bath & Washhouses 1888', is a reminder of Hampstead's function as a spa town.
At Well Walk, well see...
13. Burgh House:
A Queen Anne house Built in 1703 and later occupied by the daughter of Rudyard Kipling, it is now Hampstead Museum and a licensed buttery.
We'll then turn left up New End Square and right up Well Road to see...
14. Daphne Du Maurier's House:
A Regency house where the novelist lived in the 1930s.
Taking an alleyway right off Well Road, we'll get to back to Well Walk to see...
15. The Chalybeate Spring:
a source of iron-rich water exploited as a clean, if bitter, water source.
And almost opposite the spring...
16. John Constable's House:
One of two residences the painter had in Hampstead from 1819 to 1837.
Turning back down Well Road, we'll turn left down Christchurch Hill an on to Willow Road, where we'll find...
17. No. 2 Willow Road:
The family home of Ernö Goldfinger, which he designed along with numbers 1 and 3, with Gerald Wanklyn Flower, in 1937. Goldfinger was a Hungarian-born British architect of startlingly modern buildings such as Trellick Tower and this, a house of movable partitions, folding doors and art by Max Ernst and Henry Moore, which visitors can all see all unchanged since the 1930s courtesy of the National Trust.
Turning right down Downshire Hill, we'll find...
18. Lee Miller and Roland Penrose's House:
No. 21, an end-of-terrace Regency house, in which the
Vogue
war correspondent Lee Miller lived with surrealist painter Sir Roland Penrose and another lover in a ménage à trois. A place of parties which the art world elite, including Pablo Picasso, attended in the 1930s and 1940s.
And further down the road, the church of...
19. St John's, Downshire Hill:
Grade I-listed. Regency stuccoed, cream-painted facade. Its notable features include a Doric porch, portico and cupola. The church has no recessed chancel or pulpit characteristic of the emphasis on preaching in evangelical Anglican ('low') churches.
Turning east down Keats' Grove, we'll reach...
20. Keats' House:
No. 10, where the young poet came to lodge in 1817 after he met Charles Wentworth Dilke and Leigh Hunt who introduced him to Shelley. While living here, Keats met the love of is life, Fanny Brawne, a member of the Dilke Family. It is said that while wandering the garden here he composed "Ode to a Nightingale" and another poems. The lovers became engaged only for Keats to die in Rome of tuberculosis before they could marry.
Then, escaping the village, we'll use a track between Hampstead Ponds to reach the open expanse of...
Interlude - lunchtime
21. Hampstead Heath:
Four miles from Central London and covering 330 hectares (790 acres). It survives as a fairly untamed-seeming island of countryside in the urban sprawl thanks to the efforts of Victorian campaigners. It sits astride a sandy ridge, one of the highest points in London running from Hampstead to Highgate, which rests on a band of London Clay. The heath is rambling and hilly, embracing ponds, recent and ancient woodlands, a lido, playgrounds, and a training track, and it adjoins the former stately home of Kenwood House and its estate. The famous cruising ground (where George Michael admitted going) is behind Jack Straw's Castle pub on the West Heath, which we won't be visiting.
And on to...
22. Parliament Hill:
The gateway to the Heath. The famous panoramic view of London, including the Square Mile, is a view protected by law, and one of
England's 100 Best Views
, according to Simon Jenkins. The name comes from the story that it was the spot that Guy Fawkes told his band of conspirators to meet to see the destruction of Parliament on 5 November, 1605. We'll have lunch on the grass on that same spot.
Part 2: Highgate Village and Cemetery
We'll leave the Heath at Highgate West Hill and follow Swain's Lane all the way to the equally desirable and prosperous village of Highgate, including...
23. Highgate Cemetery:
Established in 1839 as part of London’s 'Magnificent Seven'. Famed for its Gothic Revival design and romantic, overgrown landscape. Designed by Stephen Geary, it includes the dramatic Egyptian Avenue and Circle of Lebanon. It became a fashionable Victorian burial ground, with elaborate monuments reflecting status and taste. Notable interments include Karl Marx (reinterred 1883 and again in 1956), the scientist Michael Faraday, the author George Eliot, the presenter Jeremy Beadle and the author Douglas Adams. After mid-C20 decline, restoration began in 1975 under the Highgate Cemetery Trust which charges £10 to visit. We will have a two hour-long self-guided tour of the West and East Cemeteries. I will provide you with maps of the graves.
Continuing up Swain's Lane, to South Grove, we'll reach...
24. The Scientific and Literary Society of Highgate
: Founded in 1839, and embodying Victorian ideals of education and self-improvement. Its premises include a lecture hall and library. It hosted lectures, debates, and exhibitions, forming a centre of local intellectual life.
Walking left along Pond Square and left down Highgate West Hill, we'll see...
25. The Flask, Highgate:
C17, The Flask developed alongside Highgate’s spa culture, when mineral waters attracted visitors. The present building reflects Georgian and later alterations. It is associated with William Hogarth, who is said to have stayed or visited, and with local legends of the highwayman Dick Turpin, who reputedly frequented the area.
Then after turning right down The Grove, we'll see...
26. George Michael's House:
No. 5, a house from 1688 and remodelled late C18, the home of the singer George Michael, who lived here from 2002 until his death on Christmas Day in 2016. The house became a site of public mourning, with fans leaving tributes.
And almost next door is...
27. Samuel Taylor Coleridge's House:
No. 3, a late C18 Georgian townhouse reflecting Highgate's emergence as a desirable suburban retreat. The poet lived here from 1816 until his death in 1834. The house thus became a site of literary importance, where Coleridge wrote, received visitors, and spent his final years.
Down Highgate West Hill is...
28. St Michael’s Church:
Highgate Parish Church, built in 1832 to reflect Highgate’s growth as a suburban parish. Poet Samuel Taylor Coleridge worshipped here while living nearby in the early 19th century, reinforcing Highgate’s strong literary associations during this period of expansion.
Walking southwest down Holly Terrace, then turning right down Merton Lane, we'll reach Millfield Lane and turn right onto a track across the Heath to...
Part 3: Hampstead Heath, Kenwood House and the Men's Pond
29. Kenwood House:
Originally a C17 villa, transformed between 1764–1779 by Robert Adam for the Earl of Mansfield into a neoclassical masterpiece. Its celebrated library exemplifies Adam’s refined decorative scheme. The estate later passed to the Iveagh family, who gifted it to the nation in 1927. Set within landscaped grounds, it houses a major art collection, including works by Rembrandt, Vermeer and Turner. It is owned by English Heritage but admission is free. I am in the process of booking in the group of us for 16.00 (my thanks go to Alex Young for his advice here). Find out more about the house
here
.
Crossing the Heath south, we'll pass the...
30. Highgate Open Air Theatre:
Established in 1956, it reflects post-war community arts initiatives. Set within a garden environment, it is designed to integrate performance with nature. Known for seasonal productions, particularly Shakespeare, it offers an intimate and informal theatrical experience.
Then, veering southeast past the Model Boating Pond, we'll reach our last stop...
31. The Men’s Pond:
Created in the early C18 by damming streams to form reservoirs, the Men’s Pond later became designated for male bathing. Surrounded by woodland, it preserves a naturalistic character distinct from formal swimming facilities. Swimming costs £4.80, and the water is opaque, deep and unheated, and lifeguards are present. There are also showers. The temperature should be about 17°C in early June. Find out more
here
.
If you don't want to go swimming, you can head back west across the Heath to Hampstead Underground Station where we started, which is also the route we'll follow if you do.
Booking swimming at Highgate Men's Pond:
A bookable slot system for swimming comes into operation in May. Pay £5 and book your ticket for the evening session on Saturday 6 June
here
if you wish to swim (the walk will finish later than 18.30 if you do).
Disclaimer regarding swimming:
Although appearing to be a wild swimming location, Highgate Men's Pond is owned and managed by the City of London. The website states that 'the water is deep and opaque and suitable for competent swimmers only', and contains additional guidance for cold water swimming, but lifeguards are in attendance and the venue has public liability insurance.
Dogs:
This walk is partly suitable for dogs. The urban nature of it with lots of stops might prove boring for them, and they are not permitted in Highgate Cemetery or Kenwood House. They will enjoy the stretches across the Heath.

Title: Saturday Sport Climbing in North Wales
Summary:
Whether you're an indoor climber wanting to try the real thing outdoors or a seasoned veteran, Castle Inn has something to offer, with a great selection of well protected routes, with a good bunch at the easier end of the scale.
If you are not confident lead belaying or lead climbing, please message the leader as we may need to balance the number of experienced and novice climbers.
Once we have exhausted all the climbing here, the plan is to head over to Tram Stop Quarry in Llandudno, which is a small crag overlooking the town. It has a smaller selection of routes, but is closer to town and some good pubs which would a fitting way to end the day.
Event full?
Please join the waiting list. Places usually open up as the event gets nearer and, where necessary, we try to add additional leaders to increase the numbers, so you are highly likely to get a place.
Image
/
photo
credits
:  All images (c)
Andy
with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.
Difficulty & Distance: A sport crag is a good section of climbs at differing grades.

Title: “From Quills to Cobblestones: A Stratford Escape”
Summary:
Come and join us for a cultured weekend break in Stratford-upon-Avon, and enjoy fun times exploring the town's rich heritage!
City breaks are less formal and structured than our usual hostel breaks, you’ll have free time or time to do the suggested activities below, but they are all suggestions. City breaks do not have the usual Friday night buffet, (and indeed no food or drink is included in the price) however places to eat as a group will be suggested, or you may wish do your own thing or break into smaller groups.
Stratford-Upon-Avon is a renowned market town famously known as the birthplace and burial place of playwright William Shakespeare. Situated along the River Avon, this historic town draws over 2.7 million visitors annually to experience its Tudor-style architecture, the Royal Shakespeare Company theatres, and various Shakespearean heritage sites.
What’s going to happen
Itinerary:
Day 1:
Arrive late afternoon on Friday, meet & greet reception before heading off out for dinner and drinks.
Day 2:
Saturday morning, Optional Breakfast at the hostel before we head into the town centre for a leisurely
city morning walk
with local guide - (£10 - not included in event price.)
This is followed by an optional afternoon visit to Shakespeare’s Birthplace
Shakespeare's Birthplace
and perhaps afterwards the beautifully picturesque
Anne Hathaway’s Cottage
which is a 30 minute walk away.
In the evening we will reconvene at the hostel before heading to an Optional Ghost tour at 7.30pm at a cost of £12.00
Stratford Town Ghost Walk
or dinner followed by drinks in a few local bars.
Day 3:
Sunday morning, Optional Breakfast at the hostel then free time before checkout - option of a
local short walk
Accommodation
Hostel accommodation - 2 x  6 bed ensuite rooms which will only be shared with those attending the city break.
Please note that unlike most of our other hostel breaks food is not provided and we will not have sole use of the hostel, however you will only be sharing a room with other OutdoorLads members.
Dogs:
Unfortunately isn't an event for our four legged friends. Registered assistance dogs welcome, but please let us know when you book.
Photos:
pixabay users Steve Oprey, Susli and Various Photography plus users GeekyJames & Cassidyi, used by OutdoorLads with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Weekend City Break & Social

Title: BIG20: Islands on the Edge
Summary:
Saturday 13th to Saturday 20th June - 7 nights!
Join us for a
week away on the Outer Hebrides
to explore an archipelago of over 100 islands that stretch for 130 miles across the Atlantic Ocean., to
celebrate OutdoorLads turning 20 years old!
This is a trip not to be missed - it'll be a real highlight of your year of adventure!
There is history to discover, with the Callanish Stones dating from around 2900BC, Dun Carloway broch from around 1AD to the Blackhouses from the late 1700s, in which we are staying part of the week.  There are many other sights to take in during our stay on the islands - with otters, birds and sealife to spot, hills to climb, long sandy beaches to wander along and possibly long golden sunsets to take in. We will decide upon activities for each day when we are there, taking into account weather and the desires of the group at the time.
We will spend the first four nights staying at the amazing Gearrannan Blackhouses on Lewis before transferring on Wednesday to the five star accommodation of Nunton House on Benbecula for the remaining three nights.
As is usual for OutdoorLads hostel, we will cook and eat together each night. The cost of this is included in the price, the exception being the first Saturday night when we will be on the ferry and we suggest you buy dinner on the ferry.
Travel
Travel is NOT included in the price. This event page is for the standard hostel event costs only. We will soon make available an event page to book on the minibus. This will include pick ups in Edinburgh, Stirling, and Inverness.  You will also need to pay separately for ferry crossings - please await further travel info when you have signed up before making bookings. The minibus will be used during the event to for trips out as well.
PAY IN INSTALMENTS
If you would prefer to pay in monthly instalments, please
contact the office team
(or
raise a support ticket
) and we can discuss the options available to you, depending on the time available between booking and the event taking place. There will be additional terms and conditions to agree to before you commit. You won't pay anything extra to pay in instalments. You will need to be paid up on month before the event starts.
Dogs:
This event is not suitable for dogs.
Photos :
Taken by AMD and used by OutdoorLads with full permission.

Title: Bingley Theatre - Sherlock Holmes: The Valley of Fear
Summary:
Step out of the everyday and into a world of shadowy secrets, whispered warnings and classic detective drama. We’re swapping muddy boots for mystery as we head to the theatre for a gripping adventure brought to life by Bingley Little Theatre - Sherlock Holmes: The Valley of fear.
“It is the Valley of Fear, the Valley of Death. The terror is in the hearts of the people from the dusk to the dawn.”
The great detective is back! A mysterious, coded message is received, a warning of imminent danger. Drawing Sherlock Holmes and the faithful Dr Watson into a tale of intrigue and murder which stretches from 221B Baker Street to an ancient, moated manor house, to the bleak Pennsylvanian Vermissa Valley.
Tickets are £13 (you'll need to purchase your own prior to the event), details are below. Seats are reserved so we'll do our best to sit together!
Everyone is welcome on this relaxed evening. There’ll be chance to get to know one another and chat to other members. Whether you're new to OutdoorLads or been with us for a while, it'll be a friendly evening of fun. We'll meet for food before the event too - details will be shared closer to the time.
If this is your first OutdoorLads event:
If this is your first event, we’ll make sure you feel welcomed. Please
check out the My First Event page
, and don’t hesitate to drop me a message if you have questions.
Image / photo credits:  All images are those of OutdoorLads leaders - with permission for OutdoorLads to use.
Difficulty & Distance: A seated theatre event

Title: Think you know Croydon....think again!
Summary:
Waitlist: This event is now fully booked. By all means add yourself to the waitlist, but note the maximum number of places on this walk will be 20.
Yes that's right, a walking tour of Croydon! Well not quite the town centre, but it’s been my home for the past ten years and it's certainly had it's share of issues. But in this circular walk, I will show you an alternative side to the town, full of the places I love to visit, that are often overlooked by the media. Hopefully you may discover something about the town, you didn't know before!
We'll discover a
ghost station
and a
disused railway line,
part of which now forms the Croydon tramlink. We'll take in the views from
two different viewpoints
, ascend the site of a late
Mesolithic
Settlement
and
Bronze Age hill,
visit a
windmill
built in 1854 situated in a new housing estate, and stop for tea in a former
Victorian  stable block!
Points of interest:
South Croydon
Quieter than central Croydon, we'll meet here. Much of the land in South Croydon has been associated through the years with the Whitgift Foundation,  whose charitable work has benefited and influenced Croydon since its foundation in 1596. The Whitgift School and Whitgift shopping centre bear the name of the foundation.
Spencer Road Halt ghost station and disused railway line
The station was part of the Woodside and South Croydon Joint Railway, which opened in August 1885 and ran between Woodside in the north to Selsdon Road in the south. They also opened a small station — a halt — at Spencer Road. Much like the rest of the railway, it wasn’t a success, and having opened in 1906, it closed less than a decade later in 1915.
You can see the remains of the closed railway in the undergrowth and as you head over the bridge twice on this walk, notice that it still has official railway bridge marker numbers.
Look around the bottom of the footbridge. You’ll see some large concrete blocks — these are leftovers from WWII defences, which were put up around many railway sites to be used to prevent the enemy from advancing by rail had they invaded the UK.
After the railway closed, it was left dormant, but in 2000, about half the railway track was reused between Woodside and Lloyd Park station for the Croydon tramlink. It was a failure as a railway, but is huge success as a tramlink.
Croham Hurst
There is some evidence of a late Mesolithic Settlement which would have been occupied between 5000‑3000 years B.C. The flint tools of Neolithic or New Stone Age man were also found on the Hurst. Around 2100 B.C. the Bronze Age began – Bronze Age man was religious and he buried his dead in round barrows, one of which was found on the top of Croham Hurst.
Croham Hurst covers 35 hectares and the top most point is 145 metres above sea level. The hill is formed of Thanet Sand (which makes up the greater part of the Hurst) topped with a pebble bed and resting on an eroded surface of chalk.
Heathfield Estate
Acquired in 1964 from the estate of Raymond Riesco, the building is Grade II listed. Great views and varied specimen trees make this an unusual place in Croydon, although now somewhat neglected. The Croydon Ecology Centre is here with its orchard and ponds.
Addington Hills
Addington Hills reaches 460 feet (140 m) above sea level. It is a peaceful area with many pathways, close to central Croydon. There is a viewpoint with fine views across Croydon and to north London, including Docklands and Parliament Hill.
Shirley Windmill open for our visit
One of only four windmills open to the public in Greater London, Shirley Windmill is a Tower Mill, built in 1854 by Richard Alwen to replace an earlier (1808) timber post-mill on this site, lost by fire. It ceased working in about 1890, but most of the original machinery, including its two pairs of millstones, is still in place, and has recently been restored, so there is much to see. Note, we won't have time for the 60 minute guided tour.
Combe Wood Gardens & Café
Coombe Wood is a small woodland and garden area in the old village of Coombe, South Croydon. The Coombe Wood Gardens are divided into a series of rooms which together give an all-year-round display of shrubs and plants set against a woodland backdrop. The former stable block with the Coach House cafe visible through the arch. In 1898, Arthur Lloyd, brother of Frank Lloyd built Coombe Wood House.
Lloyd Park
It was created from land owned by Frank Lloyd (son of Edward Lloyd) a newspaper proprietor who died in 1927 which was bequeathed to the Borough of Croydon by his family, after his death.
Route
You can view the route in OS Map
here
.
We'll meet at south Croydon Station which is a short ride from East Croydon Station with connections to Central London and the South Coast.
We'll head through South Croydon, along numerous alleyways and cross a bridge over the disused Woodside and South Croydon Joint Railway and location of the former Spencer Road Halt. We'll continue south walking through an area full of mainly 1930s housing stock.
We reach Croham Hurst an ancient woodland with evidence  of a Mesolithic Settlement and climb to to enjoy far reaching views south across Surrey. From here we descend  and walk around the golf course. After passing the outskirts of Selsdon we navigate through Little Heath Wood and cross the Vanguard Way. We follow the path through Bramley Bank to reach the Heathfields estate. Once a large estate, now overgrown and rundown. We'll follow the London Loop through here.
Take care crossing  over the Tram line and continue until we reach the Addington Hills viewpoint. From here you can see central London and Canary Wharf in the distance. We'll descend through woodland and to visit the Shirley Windmill. Well stop here for a late lunch. There's a small visitor centre and café. Afterwards we'll walk through the golf course until we reach Combe Wood & Gardens where we'll stop for tea. There's a café here with outdoor seating in the former stables. You also can spend some time enjoying the gardens and use toilets.
The final part of the walk takes us through one favourite parks, Lloyd Park, and then we'll head back through South Croydon, once again crossing the disused railway line (look out for the old tracks below you) at the former Spencer Road Halt. From here its a short walk back to South Croydon station.
Afterwards
We'll grab a drink at a local pub.
New Members
New members are very welcome. Please message me if you have any questions or concerns before the day.
All Images were taken by Neil Prior & permission given to OutdoorLads for re-use.
Difficulty & Distance: 12 km/7 miles mainly paved and gravel paths. Could be muddy in places if wet. Total ascent 220m

Title: Halkyn Mountain (Hill Walk)
Summary:
Let’s head up Halkyn Mountain…
Join me near Mold as we take a walk up a mighty 226m “mountain” (
cough
hill).
With open views across North Wales, we’ll enjoy plenty of fresh Welsh air, stretch our legs, and take time to catch up with friends — or, if you’re new to OutdoorLads, make some new ones along the way.
Distance:
11 km (7 miles)
Dogs:
Well-behaved, socialised, and fully under-control dogs are welcome. Dogs must be kept on a lead where signposted and whenever we are near sheep or livestock.
New Members:
Please feel free to sign up to this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions, don’t hesitate to message me, or visit the “My First Event” section under “About Us”.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
If the event is fully booked, please join the waiting list, as places often become available in the week leading up to the walk.
Image/Photo Credits:
All images are the event leader’s own and are shared with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.
Difficulty & Distance: Some Hills but gradual Climb and decent

Title: Peacehaven to Seaford Fun Walk
Summary:
Join me for a relaxed coastal adventure from
Peacehaven to Seaford
, taking in cliff‑top drama, maritime history, sea air, and a well‑earned pub at the end!
Peacehaven is just (6 miles) east of Brighton city centre.
⭐ What to Expect
Start at Peacehaven’s Meridian Monument
Stroll the cliff tops with sweeping ocean views
Drop down into
Newhaven
,
passing the fort and harbour
Walk along shoreline into Seaford
💧 Water Break at Newhaven Fort
A short hydration pause at the Fort — a perfect opportunity to enjoy the coastal backdrop before continuing.
🚢 Ferry Sightings: Newhaven → Dieppe
As you pass Newhaven Harbour, you might spot the DFDS/Transmanche ferries operating on the historic             Newhaven–Dieppe route - the UK’s longest continuous ferry link to France, today carrying around 410,000 passengers a year, most of them tourists.
The ferries you’re likely to see are the
Seven Sisters
or
Côte d’Albâtre
, modern vessels branded under the Transmanche service and run by DFDS. This crossing has a remarkably long story, stretching back over 200 years.
(credits to  Newhaven Port/DFDS)
📸 Scenic Photo Opportunities Along the Route
Clifftop Walk (Peacehaven → Newhaven) – dramatic chalk cliffs and wide coastal panoramas
Fort Highpoint – elevated views of the harbour and Channel
Newhaven Harbour & Ferries – capture ferries arriving or departing
Tide Mills Ruins – moody abandoned village and ghost station
Seaford Beach & Bay – big‑sky shingle beach, perfect for shoreline shots
Seaford Head – sweeping panoramic finish
🥾
Distance and Difficulty
8.5 miles-cliff top path and shoreline
Easy–moderate
⚠️ Safety Briefing
This walk follow cliffpath footpaths and some roadside sections. Please stay aware of traffic, wear suitable footwear, and bring water and weather-appropriate clothing. If you’re walking with dogs, please keep them under control and clean up after them. As always, walk at your own pace and let the lead know if you need to take a break
New Members
If this is your first flight or you are new to OutdoorLads passenger we’d love to see you. This type of event is perfect for you to get to know other ODL members. Please message me if you have any questions.
Pictures courtesy of martiebear
🚍 Transport-Getting to the event.
I would suggest travelling by Bus or Car (free parking at Roderick avenue car park)
Linear walk
– if you drive to Peacehaven, you’ll need public transport back
Peacehaven is served by the
12 / 12X buses
from Brighton & Eastbourne
Train options via Brighton; buses run between
Brighton ↔ Eastbourne
Seaford
Buses 12/12x and local train station
🍻 After walk
We’ll wrap up in Seaford with a visit to a local pub.
Difficulty & Distance: 8.5 miles, and cliff top path and shoreline

Title: Eryri Scrambles - Moving On From Grade 1 - Day 1
Summary:
This event takes place on
Saturday 13th June
and is the first in a series of two, with a follow-up event taking place the following day -
Sunday 14th June.
(You can book into either or both, and its not a requirement to have attended one event to attend the other. Different routes will be chosen for each day).
This event is ideally suited for those members who are
experienced scramblers
, and who are comfortable completing
Grade 1 routes without a Leader or Instructor
, and who want to build their confidence to progress to
more challenging routes (Grade 2).
The Ogwen Valley offers a fantastic venue for scrambling with a large sections of routes across all grades, condensed into a small area which enables us to
link routes up
to deliver a
truly memorable scrambling day
.
Route choice will be made on the morning of the event and will dependant on the weather.
You must bring a
climbing helmet.
Please note that the event may be liable to change or cancellation due to inclement weather.
Photos:
Pictures taken by Andy Hewlett and used by OutdoorLads with permission.
IMPORTANT!
Please note that every person participating should be aware of (and accept) that those members perceived as ‘in charge’ or leading the event are not experts, do not need to be experts, and are not regarded by OutdoorLads, or themselves as experts, but are amateurs with some experience in the event type and who are happy to impart their knowledge.  Any advice given should be considered with this in mind by the recipient.
By signing up to and attending an OutdoorLads event, you are confirming that you have read, understood and accept the content of this statement.

Title: Over the Orme: an early evening Friday walk
Summary:
This 4 mile walk follows the
afternoon cruise on The Waverley
and will take you on a circle over the Great Orme's Head which we will see on the cruise from the sea. (The event is open to all members even if not on the cruise). You'll see some great views of the town and out to sea towards Llandudno bay and the famous pier from which the cruise departs, and the massive windfarm. Also in the other direction, towards Conwy Bay and the Menai Straits and in clear weather many of the peaks of the Carneddau mountains. We will also pass by the terminus of the historic tramway, and the cable car.  The terrain will be a mixture of tarmac loose stone trails and grass land paths.
After the event some of us may linger in town and have a drink or  bite to eat.
Founded in the 6th-century
Llandudno
, known as the "Queen of the Welsh Resorts," evolved from an ancient mining and fishing village into a premier Victorian seaside town, featuring the Great Orme Tramway (1902), an historic pier, and unique Victorian architecture.
The Great Orme
has 4,000-year-old copper mines, some of the largest prehistoric mining sites in the world. A cable car to the summit and a dry ski slope have subsequently been added.  The 'Orme is also known for the wild Kashmiri goats that live there, and the marine life of the coastline, including seals and dolphins.
Photos are property of AndyM leader and used with consent.
Bad Weather
This event may be altered or cancelled if persistent heavy rain or poor visibility is forecast - you will be notified by at least 18:00 hours the night before in this case
Dogs
are welcome to join us on this event but please note we may pass through areas with livestock where your dog needs to be on a lead.
Are you new to Outdoorlads and considering your first event?
Have a look at this link, hopefully it will help
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
Difficulty & Distance: We are walking 4 miles but with 900' of height gain

Title: Nottingham Summer Social
Summary:
Summer Social: Sun, Street Food & Sneinton Market
Join us for a summer session in the heart of Nottingham. This is a brilliant chance for everyone to catch up, and if you haven’t joined us before, it’s a great, low-pressure way to meet the group and see what we’re all about.
We’re heading to Bustler at Sneinton Market. Originally a 1930s wholesale hub, it’s been transformed into a massive social space with a great outdoor courtyard. With plenty of disco balls and a rotating selection of independent Street food vendors, it’s the ideal spot for a Saturday evening.
Later in the evening, we’ll move on to The Lord Roberts, one of the city’s long-standing LGBTQ+ landmarks, to finish the night in style.
Why not make a full day of it? A few of us will be on a
Peak District walk earlier in the day
—feel free to join us for the hike before heading back to the city for the social.
Any questions? Just drop me a message. See you there!
Image Credits: All images taken by the event leader with permission for ODL use.
Difficulty & Distance: Social Event

Title: Hathersage circular leisure walk with ice cream stop
Summary:
Join Mark for a relaxed stroll through the heart of the Peak District before we head into the city for the evening. This is a low-intensity "leisure walk"—perfect for getting some fresh air and a bit of a catch-up before the
Nottingham Summer Social
later the same day.
We’ll meet in Hathersage and head out to the famous Stepping Stones (bring your balance!) before following the winding paths of the River Derwent towards Bamford.
Thorpe Farm is the home of the legendary Hope Valley Ice Cream, made right there on the farm. We’ll take a proper break to sample a few scoops (the honeycomb is a firm favourite) before meandering back along the river to Hathersage.
This walk is timed perfectly to get you back with plenty of time to freshen up before we meet at Bustler in Sneinton Market. Whether you’re joining us for just the walk, just the social, or the full "Peaks to Pub" double-bill, it’s going to be a great weekend.
If you’ve got any questions about the route or the plan for the day, just drop me a message. See you there!
Difficulty & Distance: Total distance is around 11km (7 miles) with 300m (984 feet) of ascent. Approximately 4 hours. Some paths over fields are uneven and may be boggy in wet conditions.

Title: Cawdor Castle Walled Garden and Laburnum Avenue
Summary:
This is another in the series of Scottish garden visits which are designed to be as accessible as possible to the widest range of members. We are hoping that the date we have chosen will coincide with the flowering of the Laburnum Avenue at Cawdor Castle (weather dependent, of course). If we are in luck, this should be a spectacular sight!
The gardens at Cawdor are well worth visiting and the entry fee is £10 per person to be paid at the ticket office. For an extra £6.50 you can also tour the castle itself which, again, is well worth exploring and this could be done independently as an add-on to our planned walk. You don't have to decide until the day itself how long you want to spend at Cawdor. There's a very nice cafe for refreshments...
We will meet at the ticket office at 11am sharp.
This event has been planned as a relaxing walk to follow Highland Pride the day before and the Pride Social on the Saturday evening, although it is obviously open as a stand alone event to those who have not attended Pride events.
The link to the Pride event is here:
Our First Ever Highland Pride, Inverness | OutdoorLads
The link to the post-Pride evening event is here:
After-Pride Inverness Social | OutdoorLads

by Mark D and used by OutdoorLads with full permission.

Title: Post-Pride Inverness Social
Summary:
Time to chill, relax, make new friends and renew older acquaintances!
We'll start with food in one of the central Inverness eateries at 7.00pm, then make our way to Eden Court where we can chat at ease.
The Eden Court Theatre and Cinema is a LGBTQ+ friendly venue. Join us in the bar area for a relaxed evening of chat. Whether you've attended ninety or no events, you'll get a very warm welcome. Find out a bit more about OurdoorLads and what we have planned.
We may, as a group, head out to other venues if people wish.

Title: Our First Ever Highland Pride, Inverness
Summary:
This is OutdoorLads' first time attending Highland Pride, we want to build upon our membership and range of events in northern Scotland, so, whether you like climbing, bouldering, hostelling, cycling, camping, canoeing or day walks grab your OutdoorLads RED T-shirt and come join in the fun and help show the Highlands that OutdoorLads does #GetOutMore!
The procession starts and ends at Eden Court Theatre and Cinema on the banks of the River Ness. Eden Court is also where our stall will be where we intend to engage directly with potential members and existing members who may not have joined an ODL event for some time.
It would be great to see as many members as possible to help staff the stand. **Timings will be published nearer the time**
Why not make a Highland Weekend of it and join us at the post Pride social on Saturday evening:
After-Pride Inverness Social | OutdoorLads
and at Cawdor Castle Gardens  on Sunday 7th June:
Cawdor Castle Walled Garden and Laburnum Avenue | OutdoorLads

Title: CANCELLED - Don't get "bowled" out at the Oval!
Summary:
Sadly I must cancel this event (for personal reasons) but will look to rebook it a bit later int he summer!
-------------------------------------------------------------------------
Come spend the day at the beautiful Kia Oval, home of the Surrey County Cricket Club, and host to the very first Ashes match way back in 1882 (England lost that 3 day contest by 7 runs, by the way...)!
Today's battle won't have the historic implications of that match 144 years ago, but will pit county against county, as the home team Surrey Men welcomes Hampshire Men.  This is Day One of the four day
Rothesay County Championship
against Hampshire, and is expected to be a cracking good day of "
red ball
" cricket!  Fans are even allowed onto the pitch during the innings break.  Even if you know nothing about the game (runs are scored by the batters running back and forth between the wickets - 1 point for each successful "run", or by hitting the field boundary ropes - 4 points if the ball rolls to the ropes; 6 points if the ball goes over the ropes without touching the ground) the social aspect of the day is enjoyable.
Seating is all general admission, at a cost of £15 (tickets
must
be bought in advance) and tickets can be purchased
here
.  We will all meet at the Hobbs Gate entrance to The Oval, which is less than a 5 minute walk from Oval Northern Line station, and a 10 minute walk from Vauxhall station.
There is no ODL cost to join this event; you will only need whatever spending money you wish.  If the match is called due to inclement weather we may pop round to a local pub.
If the event page is full, please waitlist and I can add you!  I've indicated 10 attendees primarily so I can gauge interest but can always increase the numbers!
Photos are courtesy of
www.unsplash.com
and
www.outdoorlads.com
and used with permission and thanks.
Difficulty & Distance: Sitting watching cricket all day!

Title: Welsh boathouse three-night adventure
Summary:
Join us for an unforgettable long weekend retreat (three nights!) in a remote and idyllic coastal hostel in Ceredigion at the stunning Ty Cwch. Nestled in the picturesque cove of Cwmtydu, this unique accommodation offers breathtaking ocean views, glorious sunsets, and dark skies perfect for stargazing. Cwmtydu is on one of the most spectacular stretches on the Ceredigion Heritage Coast, with picturesque towns and villages, a wealth of wildlife, and a large variety of plants and insects.
Cardigan Bay has the largest population of bottlenose dolphins in Europe, seals come on to Cwmtydu beach to have their pups, there’s an abundance of sea birds nesting on the cliffs, (lesser black backed gulls, oyster catchers, guillemots, razorbills, kittiwakes, fulmers, shags, and peregrine falcons), and the next valley is home to one of the few sites for the pearl-bordered fritillary butterfly in Wales.
Expect plenty of time for clifftop walks, coastal exploration, and enjoying the company of your fellow OutdoorLads in this truly magical setting. Don’t miss out on this one-of-a-kind retreat!
We’ll have two and a half days to explore the stunning coastline and relax in this unique boathouse made from repurposed shipping containers for a perfect fun nautical theme. With fully insulated and heated living spaces and outdoor infra-red heaters under canopy, its an ideal for enjoying an evening sun-downer while soaking in the beauty of the Welsh seascape. Nemo will be mixing up some optional sea-themed cocktails too!
Feast with Nemo on a weekend of fabulous food including a barbecue, delightful desserts and fun entertainment.
With its incredible location and a touch of ‘Nemo’ magic, this promises to be an extra special summer 2026 getaway. Don’t miss out—sign up now for a long weekend of adventure, relaxation, and memorable moments on the Welsh Coast!
Dogs:
Sorry, this event is not suitable for dogs.
Note:
This event is for three nights (Arrive Friday, leave Monday morning.) If you can only do two nights, then that is fine, you can leave Sunday if needed.
Want to Get Out More?

Title: Cruise the Gt Orme on the Waverley Steamer
Summary:
The Waverley
is the last sea going paddle steamer in the world.  Built on The Clyde in 1947 to replace her name-sake that was sunk off Dunkirk in WW2, she now tours the UK bringing delight to all who sail on her. A trip on the Waverley is an unforgettable experience, not just for the coastal views - look out for Puffins, Seals and other widlife, but for the mesmerising engine room with public viewing galleries.  The ship is powered by an oil fired triple expansion steam engine that drives the paddles on the port and starboard beams.
The ship has bars, cafes restaurants and plenty of indoor and outdoor seating.  The lack of vibration from propeller propulsion and Diesel engines makes the experience all the nicer. It is licensed to carry around 800 passengers - there's plenty of space, and undergoes a major re-fit in dry dock every year,
Come on board, sit back with a beer and soak up the scenery as she glides around the N Wales coastline.
The Waverley has been used previously for transport to multiple Outdoorlads walking events on the Isle of Wight, and the event leader has travelled on it many times, so if you have any questions drop him a line, or refer to the company website FAQs.
IMPORTANT: OUTDOORLADS DOES NOT BUY TICKETS FOR THE WAVERLEY,
but you need to purchase your own ticket at this link
Waverley Cruises
Tickets are £45 pp. (Dogs £5). Further info in the meeting up section.
These afternoon cruises usually sell out, so if you want to go, book now.
ONCE YOU HAVE BOUGHT A TICKET, To say that you are coming, please register on this event,
If there are any spare tickets on the day, these are sold on board.
Tickets are collected when you disembark.
If the trip is found to be sold out, registration on the event will be closed, so once you have a ticket please book a place here.  There is no Outdoorlads fee for Social and cultural events.
Want to make more of the day?:
After the cruise there's a 4 mile walk up over the Great Orme
-
please sign up separately for this here
NB after arriving back to Llandudno, the ship will be continuing to Liverpool. Anyone who lives in Liverpool might want to consider taking the train to Llandudno and extending their time on the ship. An additional ticket is required for this.

Title: Coastal Suffolk Adventure: Boat Trip & Peninsula Walk
Summary:
Consider this maybe as more of a day out rather than just another walk!
Come join our trip from Essex to Suffolk and back, including a return boat trip* to a lovely coastal peninsula, around which we will do a 10.6 mile (17km) walk. Much of it is through part of the Suffolk and Essex Coast Heaths national landscape and we'll be taking in two estuary rivers.
(*Please note there is a charge of £15 per person payable for this event to cover the cost of the return boat trip needed to get to and from the Peninsula. The usual non-subscribed walk event fee of £12.50 will not be charged in addition for this event)
Our day will commence initially on the Essex coast in the historic maritime port town of Harwich. Originating in the 13th Century, this is now the second busiest ferry and freight port in the UK. We however, will be embarking on a very much smaller craft (as pictured) from the quiet little Harwich Half Penny Pier Harbour for a 10-minute trip across the water to the Suffolk coastal Shotley Peninsula.
From the attractive Shotley Point Marina, we'll then commence our circular walk. Initially heading along the harbourside and onto the outskirts of the small town of Shotley Gate, we'll then follow the banks of the Estuary River Stour. We'll follow the path as it meanders along the estuary banks until we head off across some lovely open Suffolk countryside across to the other side of the peninsula.
We'll then arrive at the small village of Chelmondiston - from where we'll head through some scenic low undulating woodland to get to the very pretty and rural estuary hamlet of Pin Mill. Aside from being a very cute place, Pin Mill is known best for its pub on the little quayside: The Butt and Oyster.
Of course, we'll make a stop here to take in quayside views and some liquid refreshment. The pub is quite small and very popular for food, so eating there wouldn't be practical for us sadly, but we'll find somewhere in the vicinity to also enjoy our packed lunch.
Once refuelled, we'll make out way through the beautiful area known as the Cliff Plantation which, as the name suggests, is an old plantation woodland on some low-level cliffs alongside the Estuary River Orwell. Through the trees, there are glimpses of the water and some interesting wooden houseboats as we walk along the riverside path.
The continuing estuary side route then takes us on some boardwalks and paths with wide open views of the Estuary River Orwell. As we get closer back to our start point, there are impressive views of the busy Felixstowe Shipping Port across the other side of estuary, creating a unique mix of the rural path we'll be on with the industrial container port across the water.
As we arrive back at Shotley Point, we’ll wander through much more of the marina this time with great views of the boats and harbour activity.
Depending on timings, we should then have time for another refreshment stop at a bar in the marina before we catch our return boat trip back across to Harwich.
*Note - the high-level view photos of the Marina and the Butt and Oyster Pub setting are drone shots. But nevertheless, the views we'll get on the walk are lovely!
(Please note that the boat crossing is subject to weather conditions.  Should the boat company need to cancel crossings I will message attendees around 48 hours before to offer an alternative linear verison of the walk with a different start location)

Title: BIG20: Shell Island Summer Camp
Summary:
Join us as we revisit one of the most popular camp events on the OutdoorLads calendar ! Celebrate our 20th year with us at the glorious weekend!
Nestled along the breathtaking coastline of North Wales, between the charming towns of Harlech and Barmouth, Shell Island offers a truly special escape into nature. With sweeping views across Cardigan Bay and the dramatic peaks of Eryri National Park, it’s a place where sea, sky, and mountains come together in perfect harmony.
Known locally as
Mochras
, Shell Island has a fascinating past. What we now experience as a peaceful peninsula was once part of the mainland landscape, home to a small historic settlement. In the early 19th century, a major engineering project altered the course of the nearby River Artro. By redirecting the river to flow north of the land rather than south, it gradually carved out the distinctive peninsula we see today—effectively setting Shell Island apart and shaping its unique identity. Traces of its past still linger, giving the area a quiet sense of history beneath its natural beauty.
True to its name, Shell Island is renowned for the extraordinary variety of shells that wash ashore. The powerful tides of the Irish Sea bring in an ever-changing treasure trove, making every walk along the beach a chance for discovery. Whether you’re a casual beachcomber or simply enjoy the rhythm of the waves, the shoreline here is endlessly captivating.
Beyond the island, there’s no shortage of places to explore. The impressive Harlech Castle stands as a striking reminder of Wales’ medieval past, while from nearby Porthmadog you can step aboard the historic Ffestiniog Railway or Welsh Highland Railway—both offering unforgettable journeys through spectacular landscapes, inspired by the elegance of Italianate design.
For those who love the outdoors, the possibilities are wide open. Wander along miles of golden sands, paddle in the refreshing sea, or venture inland for scenic walks into the hills and valleys of Eryri and the surrounding countryside. Whether you prefer a gentle stroll or a more adventurous hike, there’s something here for every pace and mood. Plans can be shaped together on Friday evening—so you’re free to do as much or as little as you wish.
And as the day draws to a close, Saturday evening brings a simple but memorable pleasure: gathering together on the beach for a fire, sharing stories under the open sky, with the sound of the waves as your backdrop.
Dogs
Well behaved dogs are welcome, but must be
kept on a
lead at all times
and owners must clean up after them.
Please note that wardens patrol the grounds regularly throughout the day and night and make strict checks regarding this.
XL Bully dogs and dogs listed in the Dangerous Dogs Act (1991) are not permitted, even if registered.
There is no additional charge for dogs on this event.
Caravans
This site is not suitable for caravans, but motorhomes and campervans are accepted. There are no extra charges as part of this event for motorhomes or campervans.

Title: An Amble to Amberley to Gaze at Gardens
Summary:
'Dear Amberley', as the village's website represents it and visitors fondly remember it, is a delightful hodge-podge of architectural styles and building materials perched on a ridge above the River Arun. Flowers cascade down its ancient walls, the modern age passes it by, the traditional rhythms of rural life beat on, and long, loose, pretty perspectives of cottages line a quadrilateral of lanes, one of which goes to the 12th-Century church and the 14th-Century castle. These quiet lanes will be busier on Sunday 14th June when the biennial opening of the village's gardens happens (see the event's website
here
). £8 gains entry to many gardens of different sizes and styles with the proceeds going to charity.
The plan:
There will be no set plan for visiting the gardens and we won't go round in a big group: you're welcome to go wherever and with whomever you choose. You're also welcome to leave when you want, but there will be an agreed meeting place and time should you wish to depart as part of a group. You don't even need to book on to this event to see the gardens of course; we'll just happily just bump into you in the village! If you do come by train (or car but want a little more exercise), Amberley Railway Station is some way outside of the village, so we'll have a pleasant short walk along the River Arun to get there. Please see the route
here
.
Dogs:
This event is less suitable for dogs as they are not permitted in the gardens, but the walk to the village along the river would be highly suitable if they could be looked after by someone at each of the gardens.

Title: A Bimble to Bramshott to Gaze at Gardens
Summary:
This celebrity opening the gardens this year will be actress Joely Richardson (Event Horizon, The Tudors, The Girl with the Dragon Tattoo).
Scattered among the sunken lanes, flowery meadows and mixed woodland of East Hampshire is the ancient village of Bramshott. The claim that it is England's most haunted village (a total of 27 ghosts including a cat, a pig, a grey lady, a horseman, a milkmaid and a horse and carriage) will be of little significance in the sparkling sunshine of a delightful June day when around 17 varied gardens will beguile guests. More corporeal residents included Boris Karloff who retired to the village, and latterly Antonio Carluccio, Bill Bryson and Sandi Toksvig, all of whom have recently opened the gardens in the event's 39-year history. A regrettably early start will allow us to meet this year's celebrity: Joely Richardson.
Plan:
There will be no set plan for visiting the gardens and we won't go round in a big group: you're welcome to go wherever and with whomever you choose. You're also welcome to leave when you want, but there will be an agreed meeting place and time should you wish to depart as part of a group. You don't even need to book on to this event to see the gardens of course; we'll just happily just bump into you in the village! If you do come by train (or car but want a little more exercise). See the event's website
here
. Liphook Railway Station is some way outside of the village, so we'll have a pleasant short walk through fields and the village to get there. Please see the route
here
.
Dogs:
This walk is less suitable for dogs as they are not permitted in the gardens, but the walk to the village would be quite suitable if they could be looked after by someone outside of each garden.

Title: A Summer Social in Blackpool
Summary:
Join the OutdoorLads for A Saturday Night in Blackpool for A Summer Social !
Join us for an unforgettable evening in Blackpool, where the promenade truly comes alive after dark. We’ll begin with a relaxed walk along the seafront, soaking up the electric atmosphere as the lights shimmer against the Irish Sea.
As the evening unfolds, we’ll head into some of Blackpool’s popular local brew houses, and explore the new bars on the energetic LGBTQ+ scene.
Of course, no great night out is complete without good food. There will be opportunities to grab a bite along the way — whether that’s classic seaside fish and chips, late-night street food, or something a little more substantial to keep us going. And for a true taste of tradition, why not indulge in ice cream or fluffy candyfloss as we stroll? After all, you’re never too old for a little seaside sweetness !
This is set to be a lively, friendly, and flexible evening — perfect for enjoying Blackpool’s legendary nightlife at your own pace, discovering new favourites, and making brilliant memories along the way.
For more information about events in the area during this time, please click
here...
There are plenty of car parks in Blackpool, and there is free parking in the streets. A charge applies if you wish to park on the prom. Blackpool North and Blackpool South train stations are easily accessible. There are, of course, the trams which you can use. Alternatively, you may wish to utilise a horse-drawn fairy princess carriage !
The meeting point is located in between North and Central Pier. Blackpool North Train Station is approximately 15 mins walk away.
Smart casual clothing. No track suit bottoms or shorts please !
Many of us usually book accommodation over this weekend, so why not  make a weekend of it and take advantage of the variety of hotels and B & B's in the area ?!
The link to the daytime event, summer gay day walk is
here...

Title: A Gay Summer Day in Blackpool
Summary:
Join the OutdoorLads for A Gay Saturday Summer Stroll in Blackpool !
Join us for a delightful stroll along the famous promenade of Blackpool, where the fresh sea air, lively atmosphere, and sweeping views across the Irish Sea create the perfect setting for a memorable day out !
We will wander along the seafront, and take in the iconic sights and sounds that make this classic seaside resort so special — from the cheerful chime of amusement arcades to the laughter drifting from the beach.
Along the way, we’ll pass some of Blackpool’s most beloved landmarks, including Blackpool Tower rising proudly above the skyline, (a symbol of the town since 1894). There will be plenty of opportunities to pause for photos and have a look in the shops.
We usually stop for refreshments at the beautiful North Pier, the oldest and most tranquil of Blackpool’s three Victorian piers. Here, we can relax with a drink, and enjoy traditional seaside treats.
After soaking up the atmosphere and stunning views, we’ll make our way back at a comfortable pace and grab some lunch.
This is a fun, friendly, and flexible day out, tailored to what the group would most enjoy. If there’s somewhere else you’d like to visit — whether it’s the dazzling promenade lights, a cosy café, or another local attraction — we can adapt and make the day truly our own.
There are plenty of car parks in Blackpool, and there is free parking in the streets. A charge applies if you wish to park on the prom. Blackpool North and Blackpool South train stations are easily accessible. There are, of course, the trams which you can use. Alternatively, you may wish to utilise a horse-drawn fairy princess carriage !
Well-behaved, socialised and under-control dogs are welcome on this event.
Dogs must be kept on a lead whenever this is signposted and whenever we are in the vicinity of livestock, and at the event leader's request. It's a good idea to check what the route may be like with the event leader before booking to find out more about the potential suitability of the walk for dogs.
Please note that many establishments in Blackpool are dog friendly, however, some establishments may not allow dogs on the premises.
Many of us usually book accommodation over this weekend, so why not  make a weekend of it and take advantage of the variety of hotels and B & B's in the area ?!
The link to the evening summer social is
here...
A Telegram group will be set up nearer the time.
New to OutdoorLads ?
We know that new members in particular like to know in advance more about what an OutdoorLads event will be like.
If this is your first OutdoorLads event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the OutdoorLads website.
All photos taken by the event leader
Difficulty & Distance: 4 miles approx. Easy. Leisure walk with the lads. Flat.

Title: Priscilla, Queen of the Desert - The Musical
Summary:
Priscilla, Queen of the Desert
, based on the 1994 Oscar-winning film, is back with a dazzling, brand new 30th anniversary tour!
This joyous Broadway and West End hit musical takes you on a hilarious, heart-warming ride as three friends hit the road across the Outback to put on the show of a lifetime.
Priscilla, Queen of the Desert explores identity, diversity and the journey towards self-acceptance by challenging societal norms as the characters confront prejudice and adversity. It’s a true celebration of the power of love, friendship, unity, being unapologetically yourself and embracing others for who they are.
Starring
Adèle Anderson
(
Fascinating Aïda
) as Bernadette,
Kevin Clifton
(
Strictly Come Dancing, Chicago
) as Tick/Mitzi,
Nick Hayes
(
Groundhog Day, Remembrance Monday
) as Felicia/Adam, and
Peter Duncan
(
Hairspray, Pretender
) as Bob/Preacher, this production is bolder and more fabulous than ever.
You will need to purchase your own ticket from the
Sheffield Theatres website
.
This performance is likely to sell out quickly, so early booking is recommended.
If you would like to sit near the rest of the group, the leader will be seated in stalls row F.
We may go to a nearby bar for a couple of drinks after the performance.
Image credits: main image - Photo by cottonbro studio:
https://www.pexels.com/photo/drag-queen-with-an-afro-wig-4722083/
Other images provided  by the event leader.
Difficulty & Distance: Social event

Title: Postcards from Boswinger : Long Summer Weekend in Cornwall
Summary:
Escape the ordinary and join us for a truly special
3 night long weekend
at one of Cornwall’s best-kept secrets. Our hostel is a hidden gem tucked away in the "Deep South" of the Roseland Peninsula—a rare find for groups and a location that feels like a private slice of paradise.
Best of all? We’re hitting the coast on the
perfect weekend.
By visiting two weeks before the school summer holidays kick in, we’ll dodge the peak crowds and have the winding lanes, coastal paths, and pristine beaches almost entirely to ourselves. This is your chance to experience Cornwall’s magic without the bustle!
Whether you’re a hardened hiker, a sunset-seeker, a paddle-boarder, or just someone who needs some serious "Vitamin Sea," this weekend is built for you.
Where are we headed?
We’re based just five minutes from the nearest secluded beach, surrounded by the hypnotic charm of the Roseland.
The Wild Side:
High-energy hikes to
Dodman Point
, the highest headland in South Cornwall.
The Picture-Postcard Side:
Wander through the "fairytale" village of
Veryan
with its iconic thatched roundhouses or explore the sleepy, timeless harbor of
Portloe
.
The Iconic Side:
We’re just minutes from the world-famous
Lost Gardens of Heligan
and the bustling fishing port of
Mevagissey
. If you fancy a change of pace, the
Eden Project
is only 15 miles away.
The Itinerary
Friday: The Social Kick-off
Arrive from 4:30 PM and settle into your coastal home. We’ll kick things off with our legendary welcome buffet—a great chance to meet the crew and plot our adventures.
Note: Please bring a small contribution to share (enough for 2-3 people). Let’s keep it varied—creative salads, local meats, or snacks are great, but maybe let’s go easy on the quiche!
Saturday: The Grand Coastal Loop
We’re heading out on a great walk to explore the rugged coastline, trekking through the charming spots of
Portmellon
and
Gorran Haven
. You can grab a fresh local pasty or a pub lunch in town before we conquer the heights of
Dodman Point
and wind our way back to the hostel for a well-earned dinner.
Sunday: The Western Wilds
Today we head further west on our walk to
Portloe
. Expect dramatic cliffs, crashing waves, and that quintessential Cornish "hidden village" vibe.
Monday:
Breakfast, clean up and head home after a glorious weekend!
Your Weekend, Your Way:
While I’ll be leading walks both days, the weekend is yours! Want to spend Sunday at the Eden Project? Go for it. Fancy bringing your beachwear and chilling on the beach? Go ahead!
Food & Fuel
We’ve got you covered so you can focus on the views:
Included:
Hearty breakfasts on Saturday, Sunday, and Monday, plus delicious group dinners on Saturday and Sunday night.
Tea, Coffee & Cake:
Unlimited supplies provided throughout the weekend—because every hiker needs a sugar hit!
Your Part:
Just bring your Friday buffet contribution, your own preferred snacks/drinks, and either a packed lunch or some cash for a pub lunch on Saturday and Sunday.
Don’t miss out!
Opportunities to book this hostel for groups are rare, and this pre-holiday window is the "sweet spot" of the Cornish summer. Grab your spot and let’s make some memories on the Roseland!
Dogs
: Well behaved dogs are welcome on this event. Maximum 4 dogs overall, at a charge of £50 per dog for the weekend - this is a standard charge by the venue. Dogs and owners will need to sleep in a certain room together. Obviously dogs will need to be cleaned up after and kept out of the kitchen and off furniture.  Please contact the office on
support@outdoorlads.com
to book a place for your dog. Registered assistance dogs are free, please e-mail
support@outdoorlads.com
at the time of booking.
Images
All photos taken from
Pixaby
under their
Pixaby licence
or provided by OutdoorLads Members and used with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Activities to suit all abilities are available.

Title: Lake District Activity Week 2026 - Camping
Summary:
Tuesday 30 June to Sunday 5 July 2026
Join us for
five nights
in the Lake District, based at a campsite in a spectacular setting with quick access to classic fell days and big scenery in every direction. Expect a proper outdoors focused week, good company, and plenty of time on the hills.
Leaders will write their events on a whiteboard during the week, usually the evening before so you'll be able to see meeting times, location and type of event that way (example in the pictures). Leaders will decide from day to day and this laid-back approach isn't always a match for members who like to have their week super planned out.
This isn't an event for the partygoers but in the evenings people will go to the local pubs or sit around in the centre of the field socialising.
Other booking options
Campervan pitches
Arrange your own accommodation and join daytime activities
The location and what is nearby
We are right in the heart of some of the best known Lake District scenery, with big mountain days close by and plenty of brilliant lower level options too. Nearby highlights include the Langdale Pikes, Bowfell, Lingmoor Fell, Loughrigg Fell, and the views around Blea Tarn.
About the week
OutdoorLads events are led by volunteers, so the range of activities will depend on the leaders attending and what they are able to lead. The week will be mostly based around hill walking and mountain days. If we have leaders with the right experience, there may also be opportunities for other activities during the week, but these cannot be guaranteed.
Parking and lifts
Car parking is
very limited
at the site, so please arrange to car share where practical.
When you arrive, please park in the group field car park or around the edge of the campsite as directed. Please do not park on the sides of the access road.
You can use the comments section to offer and ask for lifts, or use the chat group which will be set up closer to the event date.
Dogs
Well behaved dogs are welcome. Dogs must remain on a lead on site and you will need to clean up after them. Dogs listed under the Dangerous Dogs Act 1991 are not permitted at National Trust campsites.
Photos:
Taken by event leaders and used by OutdoorLads with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Highly mountainous environment so very limited easy walks
